# Changelog

All notable changes to sluice are recorded here. The format follows [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/) and the project follows [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).

## [Unreleased]

### Fixed

**The apply-retry progress ledger now credits a young Run whose FIRST target outage strikes within one sentinel poll (~5s) of start (Bug 202 residual, re-filed as Bug 204 by the v0.99.292 regression cycle; affected the same v0.99.290+ window; loud, zero-loss).** The v0.99.292 ledger's baseline only ever advanced inside counted-failure iterations, so an early first outage left it invalid forever — the second outage's evidence had nothing to compare against and the compressed repro still exited `apply retry budget exhausted ... at position ""` despite verified durable progress between the outages, even when the fatal iteration itself held two successful anchor-bearing reads with different tokens. Two complementary, strictly evidence-based legs close it (D0-4: a failed read is never evidence): within-iteration advancement — the committed-progress sentinel now records its earliest AND latest successful observations, and any two successful observations inside one iteration (boundary reads or sentinel ends) showing different tokens credit a fresh budget with no baseline at all; and baseline seeding — the ledger baseline now seeds from the Run's first successful anchor-bearing observation wherever it occurs (including a reactive-resnapshot iteration's before-read, which skips the counted-failure bookkeeping entirely), shifting its semantics to "freshest successfully-observed token as of the last counted failure or earlier". A credit still requires a newer successful observation differing from the baseline, so the loud floors are pinned unchanged: a stuck batch (token frozen against the seeded baseline) and a target down from birth (no successful observation at all) both still exhaust in exactly the configured attempts. Pinned RED-before-GREEN by the compressed young-Run scripted-store sequence (outage at the first poll opportunity, recovery with progress, second outage) plus a resnapshot-iteration seeding cell that isolates the baseline leg.

**The large-object census's named-suspect WARN is now actually reachable — it runs BEFORE the schema read, so an in-scope `oid`/`lo` column gets the large-object context first and then the unchanged loud type refusal (Bug 205, filed by the v0.99.292 regression cycle; loud, zero-loss).** The v0.99.292 advisory ran after `ReadSchema`, but an in-scope `oid`/`lo` column — the very suspect shape the WARN names — is an unsupported column type that the schema read refuses loudly (`postgres: unsupported data_type "oid"`), so the named branch was structurally dead code end-to-end and only the quieter no-suspect WARN could ever fire; this also corrects the v0.99.292 release notes' claim that in-scope referencing `oid` values "copy faithfully as plain integers" — they refuse, on both migrate and sync (the refusal is pre-existing and correct: `oid` is genuinely unsupported as a column type, and that behavior is deliberately unchanged). The census probes the catalogs directly and needs no parsed schema, so both call sites (migrate's `phaseReadSourceSchema` and the sync cold start) now run it immediately before `ReadSchema`, with suspect scoping moved from the parsed schema to the operator's table filter (the same in-scope notion as the foreign-table census), and the named WARN rewritten to state what actually happens next: the schema read will refuse these columns — `--exclude-table` to proceed without them, convert to inline `bytea` via `lo_get` to carry the blobs, or export them separately (`lo_export` / `pg_dump --blobs`). `docs/type-mapping.md`'s "Postgres large objects" section now documents the real WARN-then-refusal sequence. Pinned RED-before-GREEN by a real-PG end-to-end integration test (blob + in-scope `oid` column: named WARN present AND the refusal still returned; suspect table excluded: quieter WARN and the run proceeds), with the quieter-branch and probe-failure unit contracts unchanged.

## [0.99.292] - 2026-07-23

Four new preflight gates (roadmap items 68c/68d/68e plus the item-25 sync-leg residual) — led by the `binlog_format=ROW` refusal, whose silent-empty-stream premise was ground-truth-verified on a real server before gating — plus three loud, zero-loss fixes from the v0.99.291 regression cycle (Bugs 201–203).

### Added

**`binlog_format=ROW` upfront gate — binlog CDC now refuses a STATEMENT/MIXED-format MySQL or MariaDB source at every CDC start, closing a fully silent empty-stream mode (roadmap item 68e, premise verified before gating).** Phase-A ground truth on a real `mysql:8.0` source with `--binlog-format=STATEMENT` (2026-07-23): the cold copy landed, then every live INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE was silently lost — DML arrives as QueryEvents the dispatcher treats as generic DDL (cache invalidation, nothing applied), the target froze at the copy snapshot, the persisted position never advanced, `Run` kept running with no error, and shutdown exited clean. The filed "quietly EMPTY CDC stream" premise was confirmed as the worst silent-loss shape, so the gate ships: the new `preflightBinlogFormat` reads `@@GLOBAL.binlog_format` at the same three chokepoints as the Bug-193 row-image preflight (StreamChanges — covering sync cold-start handoff, warm resume, and `backup incremental` — plus both snapshot openers so a cold start refuses before the bulk copy) and refuses anything but ROW with the new coded `SLUICE-E-CDC-BINLOG-FORMAT-NOT-ROW` naming the value and the fix (`SET GLOBAL binlog_format=ROW` / provider console). MIXED is refused too (deterministic writes are statement-logged — and MIXED is MariaDB's platform default, so an un-tuned MariaDB source hits this out of the box). Scoped to the binlog reader flavors only — VStream sources never take this path (vtgate owns the row-event contract) and bulk-only runs are not gated. Documented residual: a SUPER session-level STATEMENT override slips the global read. Pinned by a value-family unit matrix (ROW passes case-insensitively; STATEMENT/MIXED/unknown refuse; a failed read is a plain uncoded error) and a real-server integration matrix: mysql STATEMENT, mysql MIXED, and mariadb STATEMENT each assert the coded refusal fires with the target still untouched.

**Replication-headroom preflight — a slot-creating Postgres CDC cold start now refuses up front, coded, when the source has no room for one more replication slot or WAL sender (roadmap item 68d).** Before this, a source whose `max_replication_slots` were all in use failed mid-cold-start with the raw `ERROR: all replication slots are in use` (SQLSTATE 53400) — after the schema read, with no inventory of what occupies the ceiling and no pointer at the tooling that frees leftovers. The new preflight probes `max_replication_slots` against `pg_replication_slots` and `max_wal_senders` against `pg_stat_replication` before the slot is created, refusing with the new `SLUICE-E-CDC-REPLICATION-HEADROOM` naming the usage, the ceiling, each existing slot (active/inactive), and the remedies (`sluice slot list`, `sync decommission` for finished streams, `slot drop` for abandoned leftovers, or raising the setting) — with per-stream publications (ADR-0175/0176) making one-slot-per-stream the documented staged-wave pattern, a full default ceiling usually means leftover slots from finished waves. Cold start only: warm resume and the interrupted-COPY resume reuse their existing slot and never probe (integration-pinned on a server AT the ceiling). Managed-PG permission variance degrades gracefully — a failed census WARNs and continues, so the refusal only ever fires on a successful probe that proves the ceiling; the capability gate excludes `postgres-trigger` (slot-less) and MySQL sources entirely.

**Large-object stranding advisory — a Postgres source holding `pg_largeobject` blobs now WARNs at migrate/sync preflight that the blobs are not copied (roadmap item 68c).** Large-object contents live outside every user table; sluice copies the referencing `oid`/`lo` column values faithfully as plain integers, and until now the stranded blobs left no signal at all. The new census probes `pg_largeobject_metadata` plus the in-scope `oid`/`lo`-typed columns: suspects in scope → a WARN naming every `table.column`, stating the target's ids will point at nothing, and pointing at the new `docs/type-mapping.md` "Postgres large objects" section (carry recipes: `lo_export`/`pg_dump --blobs`, or convert to inline `bytea` via `lo_get` first); large objects with no in-scope suspect column → a single quieter WARN. Advisory only, never a refusal (an `oid` column is also a perfectly ordinary integer column, and only the operator knows whether the blobs matter), and a failed probe skips silently — the census adds no failure mode. Unit-pinned on the scoping/volume matrix and integration-pinned on real PG with an `lo_from_bytea` blob and an `oid` column.

### Fixed

**The ADR-0166 pre-create shape gate now guards the SYNC cold-start leg too — an empty-but-drifted pre-existing target table refuses up front with `SLUICE-E-TARGET-TABLE-SHAPE-MISMATCH` instead of cold-copying into the drifted schema (roadmap item 25 residual; the silent-coercion class ADR-0166 closed for `migrate` in v0.99.258, present on the sync leg for as long as sync has created tables).** The Streamer's cold start called `CreateTablesWithoutConstraints` (IF NOT EXISTS) bare, so a pre-existing empty table with a drifted column shape passed the Bug-9 populated-target check and the copy ran into the wrong schema — silent value coercion under a relaxed-`sql_mode` MySQL target, and on a strict target the mid-copy `Unknown column` error rode the ADR-0108 transient-retry wall (reproduced pre-fix: a drifted MySQL target wedged the sync for the full 10-minute test budget with no refusal). The comparison core is now extracted to a shared `existingTablesGate` (the Migrator delegates; migrate's behavior and prose are byte-identical) and the sync's default gate branch runs it after the Bug-9 check and before any target DDL: matching shape → INFO skip exactly like migrate (the create subset shrinks; the copy, indexes, and CDC still cover the table), drifted → the coded refusal naming the columns (phrased for the sync leg) with the snapshot stream Abandoned per the Bug-177 pre-anchor rule, uncomputable/no-mapping → WARN and proceed as before. The skip branches keep their contracts and never consult the target catalog: `--reset-target-data` and the non-idempotent `--restart-from-scratch` drop the in-scope tables first, `--schema-already-applied` is the operator's promise, the interrupted-COPY resume mirrors migrate's `--resume` carve-out, and warm resume never creates tables. Pinned by unit verdicts on the sync branch plus a real-MySQL integration matrix (drifted empty table → coded refusal with zero rows moved; matching table → skip + cold copy + live CDC convergence; `--reset-target-data` over the drifted table → proceeds and recreates the source shape). Known residual, filed in the roadmap: the multi-database (ADR-0074) sync cold-start path still creates tables ungated.

**Multi-table filtered sync on a native MySQL source no longer refuses at cold start (Bug 201 — affected v0.99.276–v0.99.291: filtered sync shipped in v0.99.276 and the concurrent cold-copy has been the 2+-table default since v0.99.70; loud, zero-loss).** The ADR-0101 native concurrent cold-copy reader never implemented `ir.RowFilterSetter`, so `sync start --where` on a native-MySQL stream covering 2+ tables refused at cold start with the misleading `source engine "mysql" does not support row-level filtering` (the serial `--copy-table-parallelism=1` path worked, which was the verified workaround). The concurrent reader now implements the setter, stamping the per-table predicate map onto every pinned-snapshot inner reader so each concurrent table leg ANDs its predicate server-side into both the keyless full scan and the keyed/chunked paged reads — identical semantics to the serial reader — and an ADR-0111 mid-copy re-snapshot recovery re-stamps the map onto its fresh readers so a recovery can never silently resume a filtered table unfiltered. The pipeline's refusal gate survives untouched for readers that genuinely lack the setter (SQLite/D1/flat-file sources). Pinned by unit tests on the stamp/re-stamp shape and the surviving refusal guard, plus a real-MySQL integration test (RED pre-fix on the exact refusal) running a 3-table cold start at parallelism 2 with two distinct predicates and an unfiltered bystander, asserting out-of-predicate rows absent per table, the concurrent path actually engaged, and the CDC leg still filtering after the handoff.

**The apply-retry budget no longer carries over across ridden-out outages (Bug 202 — affected v0.99.290–v0.99.291, since target-outage ride-out first worked in v0.99.290; loud, zero-loss).** The consecutive-failure counter's only reset path required a successful position READ from the target to prove progress — but the target is also the position store, so in exactly the outage class the ride-out arc exists for (target down again at the next failure) the read failed, progress applied between outages was never credited, and the SECOND target outage of a stream's lifetime exited `apply retry budget exhausted` on its first failures despite hours of verified progress in between. The retry loop now keeps an in-memory progress ledger with the D0-4 discipline (a failed read is never evidence in either direction): a committed-progress sentinel polls the persisted position at a bounded 5s cadence while each attempt is flowing (the same single-row SELECT rate as the stop-signal poller), and any successful anchor-bearing observation — boundary read or sentinel — showing the token advanced since the previous counted failure proves durable commits happened (position writes ride the batch tx, ADR-0007) and grants a fresh budget. The loud-failure floor is untouched and pinned both ways: a genuinely stuck batch (reads succeed, token frozen) and a never-reachable target (no successful reads) both still exhaust in exactly the configured attempts, the D0-4 `anchorSeen`/RestartFromScratch semantics are unchanged, and the idle-progress-timeout exemption still neither counts nor credits. Pinned RED-before-GREEN by a scripted-position-store unit sequence mirroring the deterministic field repro.

**The connect-phase retry classifier gained the PG connection-availability SQLSTATE leg — a retry re-establish landing in a restarting server's `57P03 database system is starting up` window now rides the budget instead of exiting terminal (Bug 203 — pre-existing at the applier-open re-establish site since v0.99.288, newly reachable at the ARCH-4 preamble sites since v0.99.291; loud, zero-loss).** The connect-phase classifier delegated to the shared network-shape matcher only, whose corpus deliberately excludes PG server-lifecycle wordings — but the site had no structured SQLSTATE check at all, so the deliberate exclusion became a hole exactly there: a re-establish ping that reached a restarting Postgres inside its startup window (~1–2s locally, 10–60s on managed-PG restarts) got a structured PgError, no network shape matched, and the stream exited terminal while the applier classifier and the trigger-CDC poll classifier both classified the same SQLSTATE retriable. The set (57P01/57P02/57P03 + class 08) is now SINGLE-HOMED as `nettransient.IsConnectionAvailabilitySQLState` — matched structurally via the driver error's `SQLState()` method so the pipeline stays engine-neutral and the shared package stays driver-import-free — with `postgres.IsReadTransientSQLState` delegating to it and a delegation-parity pin failing CI if either home drifts one-sided. Auth (28000/28P01), invalid catalog (3D000), undefined objects, and every other SQLSTATE stay terminal, pinned per cell through the real connect-phase entry point (RED pre-fix on all eight retriable cells). The MySQL sibling was ground-truthed rather than assumed: a docker-restart connect probe showed a restarting MySQL presents no structured handshake errno — the whole window surfaces as transport shapes the shared corpus already classifies — so no MySQL leg was added, and the asymmetry is documented at the predicate.

## [0.99.291] - 2026-07-23

The closing waves of the 2026-07-23 blind-audit remediation (`workspace/repo-audit-2026-07-23.md`, milestones M1–M3) plus the value-fidelity review of the temporal work: two HIGH silent-loss fixes in the filtered-sync client evaluator (temporal-literal granularity, non-finite NaN/±Infinity ordering), the new `sluice sync decommission` command, seven audit finding classes ratcheted into permanent deterministic gates, and a grpc security bump.

### Added

**`sluice sync decommission` — retire a finished stream's durable footprint in one gated command (audit 2026-07-23 DEVEX-3 / open question Q3).** A stopped wave leaves three durable objects behind: its replication slot and per-stream publication on the source, and its control row on the target. The slot is the expensive one — it pins WAL for the rest of a multi-week staged migration (slot invalidation field-observed at a few hundred MB on small managed instances) and, since v0.99.289's existence-semantics scope guard, blocks every later differently-scoped cold start; the only remedy was raw SQL in the staged-wave guide. The new command takes the cross-DSN `sync start` shape (`--source-driver/--source --target-driver/--target --stream-id`), verifies the stream exists (control row) and is not running, drops the slot (with the lingering-walsender 55006 retry idiom), drops the stream's RECORDED per-stream publication — never the shared `sluice_pub`, and a legacy/empty recorded name drops nothing and says so — then clears the control row last, so a partial failure keeps the record an idempotent re-run needs to finish. A live stream refuses loudly with the new coded `SLUICE-E-DECOMMISSION-STREAM-ACTIVE` (remedy: `sync stop --wait` first); the command itself is gated on `--yes` (`SLUICE-E-CONFIRMATION-REQUIRED` otherwise) with `--dry-run` as the frictionless preview. MySQL-family sources have no source-side objects (the binlog is the stream) — decommission clears the control row and says so; trigger-CDC sources keep their SHARED change-log table and triggers (`trigger prune` / `trigger teardown` own those). Pinned by orchestration-order/partial-failure/idempotency unit tests, real-PG and real-MySQL integration lifecycles (active refusal, dry-run no-op, `sluice_pub` bystander survival, idempotent re-run), and the staged-wave guide's step 5 is now this command (raw SQL kept as a fallback note).

**Gate-ratchet batch — seven audit finding classes converted into permanent deterministic checks (audit 2026-07-23 milestone M2, gates G-12..G-18).** Per the Tier-1 QA principle (every audit exits by building the gate that would have caught the class automatically): (1) **envelope exhaustiveness + oracle coupling (G-12/TEST-2)** — the new `ir.AllTypes()` registry (itself source-pinned against the `isType()` implementors) makes the push-down envelope pin exhaustive over the whole ir type universe (Bit/Interval/VerbatimType gained explicit fail-closed cases), the ADR-0176 oracle matrix moved to an untagged test file so unit-level gates can see it, and two new bare-`go test` gates assert every admitted column family has a real-PG oracle family and every grammar leaf operator (the new `rowpredicate.GrammarLeafOps` inventory, emitted per-term as `PushdownTerm.Op`) appears in the oracle — widening the envelope or the grammar without oracle cells now fails unit CI, not just review. (2) **shard-coverage cross-grep (G-13/TEST-3)** — `check-shard-coverage.sh` now parses the REAL ci.yml integration matrix and requires two-way set equality with its mirror, so a shard rebalance that drops a package (the exact shape that would silently disable the PAD-SPACE collation matrices) fails the Lint job. (3) **expiry-token gate + perf-matrix coupling (G-18/DOC-5 + G-16/DOC-2)** — `prerelease-triggers.sh` now blames every "unreleased at time of writing"/"pending review/release" marker and flags the ones whose commit already shipped (`git tag --contains`); its first run flagged 10 real stale markers, all corrected in this change (incl. the ADR-0176 markers outlived by v0.99.290); it also warns when perf-technique paths change without a same-delta `perf-parity-matrix.md` edit. (4) **ADR index status parity (G-17/DOC-3)** — a new `internal/docsync` test pins index-row status against each ADR file's own Status header plus the "stays Proposed" self-contradiction regex; its first run caught ADR-0135's file header still saying Proposed for an engine shipped in v0.99.148 (fixed, along with ADR-0136's doubly-stale Proposed status — the `d1-trigger` engine shipped v0.99.149). (5) **SyncSpec↔CLI flag parity (G-14/DEVEX-1)** — a reflective gate enumerates `sync start`'s flags through kong's real model and requires every flag to have a fleet-spec koanf sibling or a commented exclusion rationale; its first run caught that the documented `--notify-smtp-tls` flag never actually existed (kong derived `--notify-smtptls` from the trailing acronym; every doc, error message, and the fleet key said otherwise) — fixed with the documented spelling primary and the old derived one kept as a compatibility alias. (6) **error-codes content equality (G-15/DEVEX-2)** — `TestRegistryDocSync` grew from token presence to cell-level content equality: the doc table's Meaning/Remedy cells are pinned against an in-package mirror and the Class cell against the registry, so a semantics change edited into one home fails CI until the other follows (the stale scope-guard-prose class); the tripwire caught its first real skew on `main` within the release (the decommission chunk's rows merged after the mirror was generated). (7) **integration-shard fail-on-skip belt (TEST-7)** — the five CI shards now run verbose into a teed log and `scripts/check-integration-skips.sh` fails on any skipped test outside an explicit known-legit allowlist, killing the vacuous-green mode where a mid-run docker death converts the rest of a shard into invisible skips (the psverify fail-on-skip belt, generalized).

**Chaos-lite container-restart gate for the CDC retry arc (audit 2026-07-23 TEST-1 / gate G-11, milestone M1-2).** The transient-classification/retry class escaped to live soaks four times inside one delta because all of its coverage was unit pins against hand-transcribed error strings — a driver upgrade that rewords a dial error passes every pin while the real classifier stops matching. Two new per-PR integration tests (`TestStreamer_ChaosLite_*`, riding the existing pipeline streamer shard) now restart the REAL target container mid-CDC with pending source writes, and separately the source container, on both cross-engine legs (MySQL→PG and PG→MySQL), asserting the whole v0.99.286→290 arc as one deterministic gate: same-PID ride-out within the retry budget (`Run` never exits during the outage), warm resume rather than restart-from-scratch (pinned by the PG target table's OID / the MySQL table's CREATE_TIME staying constant, plus single-slot reuse on the PG source), the outage-window source DELETE replayed on reconnect (the D0-4 divergence shape — an idempotent re-snapshot at NOW would leave the deleted row as a silent orphan), and byte-identical convergence afterwards.

**Final audit-2026-07-23 quality batch — three small gates plus a test-comment ground-truth fix (M3-3 / M3-6 tails).** (1) The vacuum-health alerter's tick interval is injectable (zero value = the 60s production constant), and the delta's one previously-unexecuted production goroutine now has a 1ms-cadence lifecycle gate under `-race` — start, ≥2 observed ticks, ctx-cancel, clean-exit assert (TEST-5); the advisory's missing MIGRATE-mode cell is FILED as an explicit gap (roadmap entry + perf-parity-matrix row 26), not implemented (PERF-1). (2) The weekly vstream-pipeline leg gains the filtered-tag-gate's vacuous-green guard: a pre-run `go test -list` count pinned to the documented 40m budget basis (14 tests — fewer means a rename silently emptied the `-run` alternation, more means re-derive the budget in the same PR) plus a post-run top-level-PASS count assert that turns any skip loud (TEST-9). (3) A new `internal/docsync` gate asserts every registered engine that declares a CDC capability is named in the CDC operator guide, with a registry-roster belt against a rotted blank-import set (DOC-6's ratchet). (4) `startShardedVTTestServer`'s "mapped ports are stable across a Docker stop/start" comment was proven WRONG by the chaos-lite work (Rancher Desktop re-allocates ephemeral bindings on restart, and the restart-mid-copy chaos test relies on the returned DSN surviving `restartSource`) — the helper now binds both exposed ports to reserved fixed host ports via the chaos-lite modifier, and the comment states the real contract.

### Fixed

**HIGH (silent loss): filtered replicas now follow the SOURCE ENGINE's own comparison semantics for temporal literals (audit 2026-07-23 D0-5, owner call Q1 — fixes a stale-row defect present since filtered sync shipped in v0.99.276, i.e. v0.99.276–v0.99.290).** A temporal literal finer-grained than the column — a time-of-day on a `DATE` column, or more than 6 fractional-second digits — is resolved differently by every engine, and sluice's client-side CDC evaluator compared it at full precision, disagreeing with the engine-evaluated snapshot leg: on a PG source, `--where "d = '2024-01-01 08:30'"` snapshot-copied the row (PG casts the literal to `date`, truncating the time) and the client evaluator then dropped all of its CDC changes — stale target rows at exit 0, with or without the push-down. `rowpredicate.Compile` now normalizes each temporal literal to the source engine's own coercion, ground-truthed live on all three engines (2026-07-23) and pinned by a new three-engine real-server matrix: **Postgres 16.14** casts the literal to the column's type — DATE truncates time-of-day, and fractional seconds round to µs by PG's own DOUBLE-MEDIATED rule (`rint(strtod(fraction)·10⁶)`, datetime.c), reproduced bit-for-bit via `ParseFloat` + `RoundToEven`; the value-fidelity review's F1 CRITICAL caught the first cut implementing exact decimal half-even instead, which agrees on most boundaries but silently diverges on ~0.1% of 7-digit fractions (`.0001255` → PG `.000125`, exact-decimal `.000126`), so a randomized real-PG fraction sweep now gates the CLASS, not representatives, alongside the hand-picked divergence pair; **MySQL 8.0.46** instead PROMOTES the DATE column to datetime and compares the full instant, rounding extra fractional digits HALF-UP on the exact digits (`.1234565` → `.123457`); **MariaDB 11.8.8** promotes like MySQL but TRUNCATES extra digits (no carry) — three distinct behaviors, threaded engine-neutrally as `ir.TemporalLiteralSemantics` on the collation-resolver seam (the MySQL engine's resolver is flavor-parameterized for the MariaDB split). With the client evaluator source-faithful by construction, the v0.99.290 interim granularity EXCLUSIONS moved back INTO the PG push-down envelope: time-bearing-on-DATE and sub-microsecond literals are eligible again and the real-PG oracle gained granularity equivalence cells (date×time-bearing; timestamp×{7-digit, half-boundary, the double-mediated pair, carry}). Two ratchets close the class edges: `Compile` now REFUSES a finer-than-column temporal literal when no engine lens is available (the zero value — review F4), so "compiled output is engine-granular or engine-normalized" is an invariant of the code, and the `PushdownTerm` granularity flags remain as the classifier's fail-closed belt for terms that bypass Compile. The three-engine matrix also stores rows through real typmod/fsp columns (`timestamp(0)/(3)`, `DATETIME(3)`) read back from the server — pinning that comparison precision is the TYPE's µs resolution, not the declared column precision — and carries NULL rows plus 8/9-digit fractions. RED-before-GREEN: every new agreement pin was run against the pre-fix evaluator on live PG and failed on the boundary cells before the fix landed (both the original normalization and the F1 double-mediated correction).

**HIGH (silent loss): non-finite float AND numeric rows now order with PG's total order in the client `--where` evaluator — the snapshot and CDC legs agree on NaN/±Infinity rows (audit 2026-07-23 D0-6 owner call Q4, plus the review's F2 sibling — float affected v0.99.282–v0.99.290, numeric v0.99.276–v0.99.290).** PG orders NaN above every other value (`'NaN'::float8 > 0.1` is true), so an ordering predicate's snapshot leg copied a NaN row — and the client CDC evaluator, which mapped NaN to UNKNOWN→drop, then dropped its every UPDATE (stale target row) and swallowed its DELETE (permanent orphan), at exit 0. `compareFloat` now implements the total order (NaN greater than every non-NaN, NaN equal to NaN) universally — safe engine-neutrally because PG is the only supported source that can deliver a float NaN (MySQL/MariaDB cannot store one; SQLite stores NaN as NULL). The review found the WORSE sibling the float fix missed: PG's `NUMERIC` also stores NaN (and, PG 14+, ±Infinity in unconstrained columns), `ir.Decimal` values travel as strings, and the exact big.Rat parse mapped `"NaN"`/`"Infinity"`/`"-Infinity"` to UNKNOWN→drop — while numeric sits INSIDE the push-down envelope, so the server delivered the NaN row's changes and the client belt itself destroyed them under the benign-direction DEBUG log. `compareNumeric` now applies the same total order (NaN above everything including Infinity; −Infinity below everything; equality included — numeric allows `=`, float does not). Pinned by two real-PG integration gates that stream NaN/±Inf rows through real logical decoding (ground-truthing the float64/string delivery contracts — previously derived-not-verified) asserting server-verdict == client-verdict per op plus UPDATE-stays-UPDATE / DELETE-stays-DELETE routing on the NaN row, and by two non-finite oracle cells on an unconstrained-numeric family through the pushed prqual. Float ordering stays OUTSIDE the publication push-down envelope — the float leg is a client-leg consistency fix, not a push-down admission.

**VStream sources route engine-coerced temporal `--where` terms through the A0 client-side fallback instead of the server push (review F3).** The pushed VStream filter is evaluated by vtgate's evalengine, not mysqld — and evalengine's coercion of a finer-than-µs fraction or a date-vs-datetime-literal comparison is an unverified surface (the exact class where it already diverged once, on PAD SPACE). Tables whose predicate engages the coercion now stream unfiltered server-side and filter client-side with the engine-faithful predicate, exactly like PAD-SPACE columns (the SL1 single-precision-FLOAT refusal keys on the union); the residual is recorded in ADR-0174's post-landing list with cluster ground-truth deferred to the vitess-cluster-validator. Engine-granular temporal predicates keep pushing server-side unchanged.

**Post-slot publication re-verification closes the concurrent-first-boot window (audit 2026-07-23 D0-7 / M1-3 — affected: v0.99.287–v0.99.290, since the ADR-0175 guard shipped; requires two streams cold-starting simultaneously under one publication name).** Cold start ensures the Postgres publication BEFORE its own replication slot exists, so two simultaneously cold-starting streams sharing a publication name could swap definitions past the ADR-0175 existence guard — neither stream had a slot yet for the guard to refuse against, and the loser silently streamed through the peer's row filter or member set forever. After the snapshot open creates the stream's slot, the pipeline now re-verifies the publication against the exact definition this stream ensured (a transactional probe using PG's own `pg_get_expr` normalization as the judge — read-only, always rolled back), refusing with `SLUICE-E-CDC-PUBLICATION-SCOPE-CONFLICT` before any data moves and abandoning the just-created slot; the racer whose definition the catalog holds verifies clean, so at least one stream always proceeds. Pinned by a deterministic interleaved-ensure race gate on real PG (row-filter swap + member-set swap, both directions asserted) plus a pure-diff unit matrix.

**`--publication-name` is validated at resolve time — a mixed-case or over-length name refuses up front instead of running green through the bulk copy and dying (or silently idling) at the first change (audit 2026-07-23 D0-9 / M1-5 — affected: v0.99.287–v0.99.290, since the flag shipped).** sluice's quoted `CREATE PUBLICATION` preserves case, but `START_REPLICATION`'s `publication_names` argument is downcased by the server (and CREATE silently truncates past 63 bytes with only a NOTICE while stream-time matching is verbatim) — so an unsafe name created one publication and streamed from another: 42704 at the first change on a busy source, a permanently idle green stream on a quiet one, and the ratchet then RECORDED the broken name so every resume repeated it. Now refused with the new coded `SLUICE-E-CDC-PUBLICATION-NAME-INVALID` (lowercase `[a-z0-9_]`, ≤63 bytes — the mirror of PG's own slot-name charset enforcement), before anything touches the source; pinned through the resolve phase per the Bug-180 lesson.

**One shared transient network-shape matcher — the four hand-mirrored classifier lists are gone (audit 2026-07-23 QUAL-1 / gate G-9, milestone M1-1).** The transient transport-shape vocabulary lived in four places (the trigger-CDC poll classifier, the pipeline connect-phase retry, and both engine applier text legs) and had already drifted one release after Bug 199: the Windows dial wordings (`connectex:` / "actively refused" / "connection timed out") never reached the trigger-CDC list, so a `postgres-trigger`-source sync on Windows still exited terminally on a routine managed-PG restart — the exact class the delta had fixed twice, one file over (always a loud, zero-loss exit; an availability gap, not a data risk). All four sites now delegate to the new `internal/nettransient` matcher (one pinned corpus: structured sentinels first, then the text shapes; "no such host", auth failures, DSN parse errors, and coded refusals stay terminal-by-design), which also brings each site the generic shapes it individually lacked (the appliers gain the winsock/TLS-handshake/pool wordings; the pipeline gains `use of closed network connection`). Genuinely site-specific wordings stay local (MySQL reparent/disk-full/read-only, PG server-lifecycle messages), and the D0-3/D0-8 terminal-code shield is untouched — the shared matcher runs only when no structured server error is present, pinned per corpus entry. Corpus-parity change-detectors in all four consumer packages feed every corpus entry through the site's real classifier, so a one-sided addition or a site that stops delegating fails CI instead of shipping as drift. The corpus itself is pinned byte-for-byte, including the ARCH-5 posture decision: a wrapped `context.DeadlineExceeded` deliberately classifies transient (dial/exec deadlines against a restarting peer arrive exactly that way; a genuine shutdown is bounded by the retry loop's ctx-done select), while `context.Canceled` stays terminal.

**Control-table preamble and publication-ratchet reads now ride the connect-phase retry (audit 2026-07-23 ARCH-4, milestone M3-5 — affected: v0.99.288–v0.99.290, since the connect-phase retry shipped; loud).** Sync phases 2–2.8 (ensure control table, clear stale stop signal, the fingerprint ListStreams read, and the recorded-publication read) run on every retry re-establish, but their failures carried the bare schema-apply hint instead of the connect-phase marker — so a network blip during a retry attempt's re-establish was terminal at exactly the step class Bugs 199/200 fixed one phase earlier. All four sites are now marked like the applier-open sites; refusals (stream-id collision, the D0-2 drift refusal, permission faults) stay terminal since the marker only retries positively-matched transient network shapes. Pinned by injected-fault unit tests on each site, RED before the fix.

**PG `ListStreams` gains the legacy-column fallback MySQL has had since v0.32.2 (audit 2026-07-23 QUAL-2 / M1-4).** An upgraded binary's read-only paths (`sync status`/`health`, the live panel, `--dry-run`, `--schema-already-applied`) query the control table without running its ALTER migrations, so against a table a previous release created the widened SELECT errored with SQLSTATE 42703 and took down the observability path — the seam has now been hit by three column additions (slot_name, rows_applied, publication_name). PG now retries with the original column set on an undefined-column error, degrading to empty extras exactly like MySQL; the class is ratcheted by a previous-release-DDL pin on BOTH engines that builds the minimal original table, so every future column addition is auto-covered.

**Scope-conflict refusals no longer quote a PEER stream's row-filter predicate (audit 2026-07-23 SEC-1 / M1-8).** The ADR-0175/0176 refusal composed catalog-side predicate text into its message — and a row filter routinely carries data values (customer identifiers, date cutoffs), so a refusal landing in another operator's terminal disclosed a co-located tenant's predicate. Catalog-side quals are now redacted (the entry names the member and that it carries a row filter); the operator's OWN incoming predicate stays visible. Pinned at the rendering layer and end-to-end in the D0-7 race gate.

**A filtered `--reset-target-data` restart derives its per-stream publication instead of landing row filters on the shared `sluice_pub` (audit 2026-07-23 ARCH-3 / M2-8 — affected: v0.99.290 only).** The ratchet phase reads the control row before coldStart's ClearStream deletes it, so the reset looked like an existing legacy stream and the per-stream-default derivation gate never fired. `--reset-target-data` is now treated as a genuinely new stream for the derivation (a recorded per-stream name still wins — continuity over orphaning); matrix cells added to the ratchet unit pin.

**The filtered-sync before-image preflight runs AFTER `--where` key canonicalization (audit 2026-07-23 D0-10 / M3-2, the carried 07-19 finding).** The engines probe REPLICA IDENTITY / binlog_row_image by exact table name, so a merely case-mismatched `--where` key produced a spurious `SLUICE-E-WHERE-CDC-BEFORE-IMAGE` naming a REPLICA IDENTITY remedy that cannot fix a casing typo. The probe now receives the schema-cased names and a bad key routes to the correct unknown-table refusal; case-folded pins added.

**Push-down classifier hardening (audit 2026-07-23 ARCH-1 / ARCH-9).** `collectPushdownTerms` gains a fail-closed default arm: an AST node the term walker does not recognize (a future grammar construct) now emits an `Unrecognized` term the classifier refuses, instead of silently contributing zero terms and letting an unproven construct push down — pinned with a synthetic node. The text-collation envelope arm additionally refuses a catalog-marked NON-deterministic collation regardless of name (Compile's resolver refusal already prevents such predicates; the envelope no longer rests single-layered on it), and the `timestamp without time zone → ir.DateTime` catalog mapping the envelope's `ir.Timestamp` unreachability comment relies on now has a named change-detector test in the postgres engine.

### Changed

**The Postgres publication lifecycle moved out of `cdc_reader.go` into `publication.go` (audit 2026-07-23 ARCH-6 / M3-1).** The ADR-0175/0176 arc had grown `cdc_reader.go` to 3,339 lines, ~1,050 of them a separable publication concern (ensure/rescope/add-table, the scope-conflict guard family, the transactional probes, the post-slot verification, the coded refusals, cleanup). Mechanical same-package extraction — every symbol, comment, and behavior unchanged, proven by the untouched unit + integration suites; `cdc_reader.go` is back to 2,280 lines and the new file opens with a charter comment mapping the surface.

**The `SLUICE-E-WHERE-PUSHDOWN-DRIFT` remedy now names the PG slot-drop hop (v0.99.290 regression-cycle observation).** The refusal's `--restart-from-scratch` escape first hits the loud slot-exists refusal on a PG source; the hint, registry summary, and error-codes row now say so (drop the stream's replication slot as that refusal instructs, then re-run) instead of leaving the operator to discover the extra hop. Also added: the explicit-`--publication-name`-overrides-recorded ratchet arc is now unit-pinned end to end (WARN naming both names, record update, and no re-push into the source engine — audit TEST-6 / M3-4).

### Dependencies

- Bumped `google.golang.org/grpc` 1.82.0 → 1.82.1 (the GHSA xDS RBAC / HTTP-2 HIGH advisory, dependabot alert; govulncheck reports the vulnerable path is not reachable from sluice code — bumped anyway so the published binaries carry the patched module).

### Documentation

**MariaDB joins both operator migration guides (audit 2026-07-23 DOC-6, 3rd carry).** The flavor has been first-class since v0.99.268 — domain-GTID CDC (ADR-0170), native `uuid`/`inet6`/`inet4` carried faithfully on bulk copy AND the binlog tail (ADR-0171) — but neither `cdc-streaming.md` nor `migrating-legacy-mysql.md` mentioned it. Both now carry MariaDB sections: the CDC guide covers the domain-GTID position/resume shape, the native-type wire subtleties, and the shared retry policy; the legacy guide covers why MariaDB is prime legacy territory (strict mode defaulted on only in 10.2.4), the flavor fingerprint guard's two directions, the native types, and the version-dependent `PAD_ATTRIBUTE` catalog gap (absent through 11.x LTS/12.0, added 12.1 — sluice keys on the `_nopad_` name token), linking the public field note. The class is ratcheted by the new CDC-guide doc-sync gate.

## [0.99.290] - 2026-07-23

The complete ADR-0176 arc (per-stream Postgres publications + the PG 15+ publication row-filter push-down) plus the full remediation of the 2026-07-23 blind audit (`workspace/repo-audit-2026-07-23.md`) — five High findings, three of them in released code, including one CRITICAL silent-loss fix for filtered PG sync.

### Added

**Postgres publication row-filter push-down for `sync --where` on PG 15+ sources ([ADR-0176](docs/adr/adr-0176-pg-publication-row-filter-pushdown.md) chunk 2 — the PG analogue of the VStream filter push-down).** A filtered PG-source sync now pushes its `--where` predicate into the stream's publication as a per-table `WHERE (<predicate>)` row filter, so out-of-scope changes are never decoded or sent — PG's own filter transformation implements the same move-IN → INSERT / move-OUT → DELETE truth table sluice's client evaluator uses, and the predicate text is single-sourced with the snapshot SELECT and verify COUNT renderings so the three server-side evaluation sites cannot drift. Because pushing down makes the server authoritative for delivery (the ADR-0174 A0 hazard shape), the feature ships inside a proven envelope, gated three ways: a conservative eligibility classifier (integer, numeric, boolean, date, timestamp-without-tz, and text/varchar under the default or `"C"` collation; everything else — bpchar, enum, uuid/inet, float, binary/JSON, other collations, and the bool-vs-0/1 idiom that isn't valid PG SQL — streams unfiltered server-side and keeps today's client-side filtering, logged at INFO) whose family list is a change-detector pin that can only widen together with the oracle; a real-PG-16 per-cell equivalence oracle (~41 predicate-shape × value-family cells, all four row-move transitions plus NULLs in every workload) asserting the delivered row set under push-down is identical to client-side-only evaluation; and the client-side evaluator staying ON as a continuous equivalence belt (a client-side drop on a pushed-down table logs at DEBUG — the benign direction). Below PG 15 the emitted DDL is byte-identical to before (client-side-only, no refusal, no flag). The ADR-0175 scope guard composes with it: a second stream's bare rescope that would clear a row filter still refuses, and a filtered stream's own cold restart — whose raw predicate text can't be text-compared to the catalog's `pg_get_expr` rendering — is resolved by a transactional no-op probe (ALTER in a transaction, re-read, commit only when PG itself proves the definition unchanged), so restarts never false-refuse while peer streams exist. Warm resume still never touches the publication — and now reconciles against it (see Fixed).

**Per-stream Postgres publications with a control-state ratchet (the ADR-0176 prerequisite chunk).** The stream's effective publication name is now recorded in its `sluice_cdc_state` row (a new `publication_name` column, `slot_name`'s exact sibling, on BOTH target stores — the control table lives on the target and a PG-source sync can target any engine), and `sync start` ratchets onto it: an explicit `--publication-name` wins (WARNing and updating the record when they differ), a recorded name is reused on warm resume without re-passing the flag, and only a genuinely NEW stream with a `--where` row filter derives a per-stream default (`sluice_<stream-id>`, normalized to a safe 63-byte PG identifier) — every other stream keeps the shared `sluice_pub` default byte-identically, so nothing changes on upgrade. Cleanup parity comes with it: the pre-anchor teardowns that drop a stream's just-created replication slot now drop its per-stream publication too (never the shared default, tolerant of absence), so abandoned cold starts don't accumulate dead publications on the source. The ADR-0175 scope-conflict guard also widens from the member *set* to the full publication *definition*: on PG 15+ two identical table sets where a surviving member carries a row filter or column list the rescope would silently clear now refuse with the same `SLUICE-E-CDC-PUBLICATION-SCOPE-CONFLICT`. `sluice schema add-table` also inherits the record: it now scopes its publication-extend to the stream's recorded publication (previously it always extended `sluice_pub`, which against a per-stream-publication stream would loudly fail — or, when another stream's `sluice_pub` existed, silently leave the new table outside the stream's scope after bulk-copy). Pinned end to end on real PG by new `TestPublicationScope_*` gates (derive → record → warm-resume reuse, and abandon-drops-per-stream-publication-but-never-`sluice_pub`).

### Fixed

**CRITICAL (silent loss, filtered PG sync — affected: v0.99.276–v0.99.289, every release with filtered PG sync): an UPDATE to a non-predicate column of a row whose filtered column is unchanged out-of-line TOAST no longer emits a spurious DELETE on the target (audit 2026-07-23 D0-1).** pgoutput sends an out-of-line TOASTed column as `unchanged-toast-datum` in the UPDATE's NEW tuple whenever a different column changed — even under `REPLICA IDENTITY FULL` — so the decoded after-image omitted the column, the `--where` row-move evaluation read it as UNKNOWN→false, and an in-scope sibling-column UPDATE was mis-classified as a move-OUT: sluice DELETEd a row the source still holds, silently, with `sync status` green (with or without the new push-down — under push-down the server even delivered the UPDATE correctly and the client belt itself destroyed it). The Postgres reader now backfills the omitted columns from the complete RI-FULL before-image on filtered tables (exact by PG's unchanged ⇒ old == new guarantee), and `route()` gains an after-image completeness belt — the `SLUICE-E-WHERE-CDC-BEFORE-IMAGE` sibling, `SLUICE-E-WHERE-CDC-AFTER-IMAGE` — that stops the stream loudly if any partial after-image ever slips past the reader again. Reproduced live before fixing (a 9.6KB `STORAGE EXTERNAL` column + sibling-column UPDATE deleted the target row); pinned by a new end-to-end integration gate (row survives, sibling update applies, TOAST value byte-identical) plus a no-touch workload arc in the push-down oracle so every family cell now also exercises an UPDATE that never sets the filtered column.

**Terminal-code shield in both applier classifiers: a structured server error can no longer be flipped retriable by transient wording quoted in its message (audit 2026-07-23 D0-3/D0-8 — the MySQL silent batch-skip chain affected v0.99.92–v0.99.289; the loud retry-delay class on both engines dates to the v0.42.0 classifier).** Both engines' error classifiers ended with text-fallback legs that scanned the whole error string for transport-transient wording (`reparent`, `not serving`, `connection refused`, `disk full`, ...) — and those legs were reachable even when the chain carried a structured `*MySQLError`/`*pgconn.PgError` whose code had already been judged terminal, because a server error's message routinely echoes row data, key values, and (via the flush wrapper) table names. The MySQL consequence was the worst class sluice has: a plain-INSERT cold-copy batch failing 1062 on a table named e.g. `reparent_history` (or with a colliding value like `planned-reparent-2026-07`) classified RETRIABLE, the byte-identical retry hit 1062 again, and the ADR-0108 tolerate-1062-on-retry wart — whose safety argument assumes a first-attempt 1062 stays terminal — swallowed it as "the rows already landed": **both INSERTs had rolled back, so the entire batch was silently absent while the migration reported success.** On Postgres the blast radius was smaller (PgError messages don't echo row data): a `P0001` audit trigger quoting stored text like "connection timed out", or a 23505 with transient wording, retried a deterministically-failing batch through the full ADR-0038 budget instead of failing loudly at once. Now a matched structured driver error is classified by its code ALONE — the text legs run only when no structured error is present — with the documented structured-code+message AND-gates preserved (MySQL 1105+vttablet framing, 1290+read-only wording, Error 3+ENOSPC; PG XX000+read-only wording). Pinned by a cross-product matrix on both engines: every structurally-terminal code × every transient substring in the text legs must classify non-retriable, and every previously-pinned transient shape stays retriable.

**A failed between-attempt position read no longer forces a destructive re-snapshot (audit 2026-07-23 D0-4 — affected: v0.99.288–v0.99.289; the latch predates them but was unreachable, since a terminal connect exit meant a fresh process always warm-resumed correctly).** The sync retry loop reads the persisted CDC position between attempts to decide cold-copy-phase (force a clean re-establishment) vs CDC-phase (warm resume) — but it discarded the read's ERROR, and both engines report found=false on a failed read, so "the target is down and I could not read the row" was indistinguishable from "no anchor row exists". A routine target restart — the exact scenario the v0.99.288/289 connect-phase retry now survives — could therefore latch `RestartFromScratch` mid-outage, and the first successful reconnect executed a forced fresh cold start ignoring the valid persisted position: in-scope tables dropped and re-copied on native MySQL targets, and on idempotent sources (Postgres/VStream) a re-snapshot whose slot is recreated at NOW, which never replays source DELETEs committed before the new snapshot — silent divergence where a warm resume would have replayed them from retained WAL/binlog. The discriminator now only trusts a SUCCESSFUL read: a failed read leaves the restart latch at its prior value (defaulting to warm resume whenever any successful read observed the anchor this run), and only a successful read proving "no anchor row" may force the re-establishment. Pinned by unit tests on the retry seam: transient apply error + failing position read must warm-resume the next attempt, and a genuine pre-anchor cold-copy failure (successful read, no row) must still force the clean re-copy.

**Warm resume now reconciles the pushed publication row filter with the current `--where` flags — a changed or REMOVED predicate refuses loudly instead of silently under-delivering forever (audit 2026-07-23 D0-2, the ADR-0176 release blocker — never released; closed before the arc ships in this release).** ADR-0176 made `--where` the first flag whose effect is durable source-side catalog state: the pushed predicate lives on the stream's publication and warm resume deliberately never re-ensures it — so restarting with a widened, changed, or removed `--where` left the SERVER filtering on the stale predicate, with rows the new flags admit never decoded or sent, unobservable client-side by construction, persisting across every restart at exit 0 (the removed-`--where` variant produced zero operator-visible signal at all). The pushed subset's canonical fnv64a hash is now recorded in `sluice_cdc_state` as `row_filter_hash` — `publication_name`'s exact sibling on BOTH engine control stores (ADD-COLUMN migration, COALESCE legacy tolerance, byte-exact stored-codec round-trip pins including the empty-subset sentinel) — and `sync start` phase 2.8 compares it on every warm resume of a publication-capable source, refusing drift with the new `SLUICE-E-WHERE-PUSHDOWN-DRIFT` naming the escapes (re-pass the original `--where`; `--restart-from-scratch` to re-snapshot under the new predicate — required for a widened filter anyway; `--reset-target-data`), both of which are themselves exempt from the comparison so the refusal can never block its own remedies. Pinned end to end on real PG: widened and removed `--where` both refuse (RED pre-fix — both silently resumed), same-filter resume stays byte-identical, and the restart-from-scratch escape re-establishes under the new predicate and re-records the hash.

**The publication push-down envelope now excludes temporal literal-granularity mismatches (audit 2026-07-23 D0-5 — never released; closed within the arc).** Postgres resolves a finer-than-column temporal literal by silently truncating or rounding it inside the publication row filter — a time-bearing literal against a DATE column stores as `(d < '2026-01-15'::date)`, and a >6-fractional-digit literal against a microsecond timestamp rounds to µs — while sluice's client evaluator compares the literal at full precision, so the pushed filter and the equivalence belt provably disagree on the boundary (ground-truthed on real PG 16: the date shapes diverge on `<`/`!=`/`NOT(>=)`, the 7-digit timestamp literal matches server-side but not client-side). `rowpredicate.PushdownTerms` now carries per-literal granularity facts (time-bearing; sub-microsecond) and the ADR-0176 classifier keeps such terms out of the envelope — those tables stream unfiltered server-side and keep client-side filtering, the known-correct fallback under both of the audit's D0-5 adjudications (whether `Compile` should additionally normalize or refuse such literals is deferred to the owner — audit Q1). The real-PG oracle also gains its missing op×family cells (bool `IN`/`NOT IN`, timestamp `IN`/`NOT IN`, date `!=`/`NOT IN`/3VL negation — audit TEST-4) plus a permanent granularity-fallback gate that asserts both the live-PG divergence and the classifier exclusion for every granularity shape.

**A dial-time connect failure on the APPLY path now retries instead of exiting with zero retries, on both engines (Bug 200, found by the v0.99.289 post-release regression cycle — always a loud, zero-loss exit; an availability gap, not a data risk).** The v0.99.288/289 connect-phase hardening covered the pipeline's setup/reopen sites — but the engine appliers dial too: when a target restart severs the pool, the next apply's pool acquire (Postgres, pgx `ConnectError` inside `pipelined acquire conn`) or `begin tx` (MySQL) dials fresh into the refused window, and neither applier classifier had a dial-shape leg — so the first apply during a routine target restart exited terminally with zero retries, defeating the retry budget for the most common local transient. Both classifiers now match the positive dial-transient shapes (POSIX `connection refused`, the Windows `connectex:`/`actively refused` wordings, `connection timed out`), and the Postgres concurrent lane's begin site — which returned errors raw, bypassing the classifier entirely — now classifies. Default-deny intact and pinned both ways: a typo'd hostname (`no such host`) and an auth failure inside a connect error stay terminal on both engines. Third sibling-generation of the "classification coverage, not missing retry machinery" class (v0.99.286 trigger transports → v0.99.288 pipeline connect phase → v0.99.289 wordings/pool → this: the appliers' own dials); each generation was caught by the release-N regression cycle within hours of publish.

### Changed

**Audit 2026-07-23 quick-wins (DEVEX-1..4, DOC-1..5, ARCH-2).** The fleet YAML sync spec gains the `publication-name` key — the ADR-0175 refusal's primary documented escape was unexpressible on the fleet surface most likely to trigger it (parse pin added). The error-codes registry, staged-wave guide, and sluicecode summary now state the guard's v0.99.289 existence semantics plus the drop-the-slot escape instead of the stale active-slot wording, and the staged-wave cutover recipe gains an explicit decommission step (drop the finished wave's slot + per-stream publication — a leftover slot pins WAL for the rest of the migration and blocks later differently-scoped cold starts). The below-PG-15 version-gate filter drop now logs at INFO (the preflight already announces the push at INFO, so the drop must be equally visible or the operator believes WAL delivery is server-filtered when it is not — data stays correct either way). The scope-conflict refusal now names the always-valid own-publication escape. Plus doc-drift closures: the filtered-subset guide's "no server-side row filter" claim (falsified by the push-down), perf-parity matrix row 25 (+ the trigger-CDC reduction gap filed explicitly per the perf working agreement), ADR-index rows 0175/0176, roadmap item 65 marked SHIPPED, and the expiry-token sweep.

## [0.99.289] - 2026-07-23

**Retry + guard hardening — closing the windows the last release's fixes still left open.** The ADR-0175 publication scope-conflict guard now protects stopped streams, not just running ones; a managed-PG maintenance restart or standby promotion under a `postgres-trigger` source now reconnects instead of exiting; and the v0.99.288 connect-phase retry gains the two shapes it missed — the Windows dial-refusal wording and a severed pool connection at a checkpoint boundary. Two of the three fixes were found by sluice's own post-release regression cycle and tag CI within hours of v0.99.288 publishing.

### Fixed

**The publication scope-conflict guard now keys on slot EXISTENCE, not activity — closing ADR-0175's documented residual window (present in v0.99.287–v0.99.288).** As shipped in v0.99.287 the guard refused a narrowing publication rescope only while another *active* `sluice_%` replication slot existed, so a conflicting stream whose slot was momentarily inactive — stopped mid-migration, or between retry attempts — could still be silently de-scoped and starve on its next resume. A slot's existence is the durable, source-side claim that a stream holds a scope and intends to resume, so the guard now refuses against any other `sluice_%` slot, active or not; the refusal labels each conflicting slot (active vs inactive) and names all three escapes — `--publication-name` per stream, drain the other stream, or drop the slot if the stream is truly abandoned. The deliberate cost is a conservative refusal against slots whose streams have finished for good — including the previously-benign flow of *sequential* different-scope runs against one source (finish a sync on one table set, start another on a different set): those now refuse until the finished stream's slot is dropped or each run uses its own `--publication-name`, because sluice cannot distinguish "finished forever" from "stopped and about to resume", and the resumed case was being silently starved. A leftover slot pins WAL on the source anyway. Pinned by a new integration test in the ADR-0175 gate: an inactive slot alone must refuse, and the refused attempt must leave `pg_publication_rel` untouched.

**A Postgres server restart or standby promotion under a `postgres-trigger` source now reconnects instead of exiting the sync.** The v0.99.286 trigger-CDC hardening classified network/transport transients on the change-log poll but explicitly deferred the SQLSTATE level: a poll failing with `57P01` (admin_shutdown), `57P02`/`57P03` (crash shutdown / cannot connect now), or a class-08 connection exception — exactly what a managed-PG maintenance restart or failover produces — still surfaced unclassified and terminated the stream, a gap as old as the trigger engine itself (v0.85.0). Those shapes now ride the same bounded retry budget, via a narrow exported predicate on the postgres engine so the trigger engine and the applier can never drift on what "connection-availability transient" means. Deliberately narrower than the applier's set: a missing change-log table (`42P01`) and auth failures stay terminal — retrying those masks an operator error.

Also hardened the slot-loss integration test against a walsender release race (the v0.99.288 tag-CI flake: the test's own `pg_drop_replication_slot` ran before Postgres marked the slot inactive, `SQLSTATE 55006`) — the drop now retries until the walsender lets go. Test-only; no product change.

**Two transient shapes the v0.99.288 connect-phase retry missed now retry (Bug 199, found by the post-release regression cycle within hours of publish).** First, the Windows dial-time refusal wording — `connectex: No connection could be made because the target machine actively refused it.` — matched neither the POSIX `connection refused` text nor the structural `ECONNREFUSED` check (pgx v5 flattens multi-host connect errors), and since the refused window is most of any target restart, the connect-phase retry was effectively inert on Windows for the canonical local transient (v0.99.288 only — earlier releases had no connect-phase retry at all). Second, platform-neutral: a severed pool connection picked up at a checkpoint boundary surfaced pgx's `conn closed` unclassified and exited with zero retries; the Postgres applier classifier now honours pgconn's own `SafeToRetry` contract (the error is guaranteed to have occurred before any data reached the server) plus the `ErrConnClosed` sentinel (unclassified since the apply-retry classifier landed in v0.42.0). Both were loud, zero-loss exits with clean warm resumes — availability gaps, not data risks — and both are the "fixed the representative, missed the siblings" class the regression cycle exists to catch.

## [0.99.288] - 2026-07-22

**Operational resilience, found by running sluice's own multi-day soaks — plus a new proactive target-health advisory family.** A transient network blip while a sync retry re-establishes its connections no longer kills the stream (caught live on the 2026-07-22 scale-soak), a cancelled D1 `--stage-local` stage now reports a stable error identity (the v0.99.287 tag-CI flake), and two new opt-in `--notify-*` rules watch the Postgres TARGET for autovacuum falling behind and transaction-ID wraparound headroom. If you run continuous sync for days at a time, upgrade.

### Added

**Target-side autovacuum advisories: `--notify-dead-tuple-ratio` and `--notify-xid-age` on `sync start` (and the fleet YAML).** A sluice bulk copy into Postgres is a sustained high-write workload, and so is a long CDC catch-up against a high-churn source — exactly the shape where dead tuples outrun autovacuum, with transaction-ID wraparound as the far end of that road. Two new threshold rules in the existing alerter watch the TARGET's own catalog for it: `--notify-dead-tuple-ratio` fires (warning) when the worst user table's `n_dead_tup/(n_dead_tup+n_live_tup)` reaches the threshold — the alert body names the table, the dead/live counts, and when autovacuum last completed there, so "autovacuum is running but losing" vs "autovacuum never reached this table" is answered in the page itself; `--notify-xid-age` fires (critical) when the database's `age(datfrozenxid)` reaches the threshold (Postgres force-stops near ~2.1B; autovacuum normally holds this near 200M). Like the sync-lag alert these need no PlanetScale telemetry — the signal is probed from the target over the connection sluice already holds — and they run through the same edge-trigger + cooldown + hysteresis machinery and the same webhook/Slack/SMTP sinks. Tables below a 1000-dead-tuple floor are ignored so a tiny scratch table can't page on a meaningless ratio. Postgres targets only (a configured rule on another target warns once and stays inert); both rules are off by default and advisory throughout — a probe or sink failure is logged and swallowed, never able to affect the sync. Pinned by an integration test that ground-truths the probe on real PG 16 with manufactured bloat plus a sub-floor all-dead decoy that must not page.

### Fixed

**A transient network failure while a sync retry attempt re-establishes its connections no longer kills the stream.** The retry loop classifies errors by the `ir.RetriableError` wrapper the engines attach inside a flowing attempt — but each attempt first has to reopen the target applier and source readers, and a failure there carried no wrapper, so it returned terminal. Caught live on a multi-day soak: a ~30-second network blip broke both legs of a PlanetScale↔PlanetScale sync at once; the VStream read error was correctly classified and the retry engaged, but the reopen then died at `open target change applier: mysql: ping: invalid connection` and the process exited — even though the same warm resume, run later, drained the backlog in under two minutes. Connect-phase failures are now marked where they arise and, when they carry a positively-matched transient network shape (dead pool connection, reset/refused, timeouts, TLS handshake timeout, the Windows winsock wordings), ride the existing bounded retry budget with exponential backoff. The classification is deliberately narrow and the budget is the loud-failure floor: DSN parse errors, bad credentials, unknown-host, coded refusals, and every unknown shape stay terminal exactly as before, and a target that can never be reached still exhausts the budget and fails loudly. The gap is as old as the retry machinery itself (v0.42.0/v0.46.0), so every prior release with continuous sync carries it — but the failure was always loud (a clean exit with a clear error and a durable resume position), never data loss. This is the connect-phase sibling of v0.99.286's trigger-CDC transient classification — same class (classification coverage, not missing retry machinery), different site.

**A cancelled Cloudflare D1 `--stage-local` staging pass now reports a stable `context.Canceled` error identity.** When staging was cancelled mid-page, `database/sql` surfaced the abort differently depending on which DB operation the cancel landed on — `sql: statement is closed` when the pool reaped the prepared statement, `context canceled` when the exec saw it directly, or a driver-specific message on a `Commit`/`BeginTx`/`Prepare`. All mean "we were cancelled", but callers and the retry classifier need one stable identity, and the nondeterminism surfaced as the v0.99.287 tag-CI flake (a byte-identical build failed an `errors.Is(err, context.Canceled)` assertion that had passed two hours earlier). The fix normalises at the single return boundary: a named-return `defer` wraps the returned error with `ctx.Err()` whenever the context is done and the chain doesn't already carry it, preserving the driver detail — so a DB call added to the loop later can't reintroduce the nondeterminism. This only ever affected which error identity a correctly-aborted stage reported, never whether it aborted safely — no staged file was ever silently truncated. Present since the `--stage-local` staging path landed in v0.99.167; stress-verified 50/50 on the previously-flaky test.

## [0.99.287] - 2026-07-22

**Two independent correctness fixes, both found by looking outward rather than inward.** The first closes a silent divergence on Postgres sources: two concurrent syncs with different table scopes would quietly starve each other, with a healthy-looking `sync status` throughout — the shape you hit staging a migration in waves. The second unblocks SQLite/Cloudflare D1 imports that use the canonical boolean idiom (`INTEGER CHECK (col IN (0,1))`), which sluice was refusing outright. If you run more than one Postgres-source stream against one database, or import from SQLite/D1, upgrade.

### Fixed

**SQLite / Cloudflare D1 sources: a `CHECK (col IN (0,1))` constraint no longer blocks the migration.** SQLite has no `BOOLEAN` type, so the canonical idiom is an `INTEGER` column constrained to 0/1 — and sluice refused it at create-tables with "carries a non-portable SQLite expression … with no provably-equivalent Postgres translation", stopping the migration before any data moved. The expression was in fact perfectly portable; the SQLite→canonical expression translator simply had no `IN` node, so the construct could not be parsed and fell through to the catch-all non-portable refusal. `x [NOT] IN (a, b, …)` now translates verbatim to both Postgres and MySQL, which share SQLite's syntax and its NULL three-valued logic. The genuinely non-portable shapes still refuse: `IN (SELECT …)` (a subquery is outside the provable subset), SQLite's `IN <table>` shorthand, and the SQLite-only empty list `IN ()` (a Postgres syntax error).

**…and `--infer-types` now re-types those constraints along with the column.** When rich-type inference promotes such a column to Postgres `BOOLEAN`, the surrounding CHECK has to be re-typed with it or Postgres rejects the body (`operator does not exist: boolean = integer`). The 0/1 literals compared against a boolean-promoted column now emit as `false`/`true` — covering `=`, `<>`/`!=`, reversed operand order (`1 = flag`), and `IN`/`NOT IN` members. Deliberately NOT rewritten, because each would be a guess: an ordering comparison (`flag > 0`, whose boolean semantics are their own thing) and an out-of-range literal (`flag = 2`, which was never valid against a two-valued column) are left alone so Postgres rejects them loudly. MySQL targets are unchanged — its `BOOLEAN` is `TINYINT` and takes 0/1 natively. The rewritten constraints still *enforce*: the integration pin asserts a non-conforming row is rejected and a conforming one accepted, because a CHECK silently relaxed into a tautology would be worse than the original bug.

**`strftime()` column DEFAULTs on a SQLite/D1 source now translate instead of being dropped.** Only `datetime('now')` / `date('now')` / `time('now')` were recognised; every `strftime(...)` spelling took the loud-WARN drop path, so the migration succeeded but the target column had no default and a DEFAULT-omitting `INSERT` after cutover produced NULL where the source would have supplied a timestamp. The whole-format current-instant spellings now map to Postgres equivalents, preserving precision — these render at second precision whereas Postgres's bare `now()` carries microseconds, so the translation is `date_trunc('second', now())` rather than `now()`. `%s` maps to `floor(extract(epoch from now()))`. Partial formats (`'%Y'` alone) and non-`'now'` bases still refuse and drop loudly: guessing a general strftime→to_char translation is how a DEFAULT silently changes meaning.

Both classes were found by reviewing [planetscale/cli#1299](https://github.com/planetscale/cli/pull/1299) against sluice, and neither ever risked migrated row data — copied rows carry explicit values throughout.

**Concurrent Postgres-source syncs with different table scopes no longer silently starve each other (ADR-0175).** The replication *slot* has always been per-stream and operator-overridable (`--slot-name`), but the *publication* — the table filter `pgoutput` applies, named per stream at `START_REPLICATION` — was a hardcoded `sluice_pub` with no flag, config key, or env var. Each cold start ran `ALTER PUBLICATION sluice_pub SET TABLE <its own tables>`, which replaces the member set atomically, so cold-starting a second stream with a disjoint `--include-table` scope against the same source de-scoped the first: its slot stayed healthy and kept advancing while `pgoutput` emitted nothing for its tables, and `sync status` / `sync health` both reported a current stream. Warm resume never re-asserts scope, so restarting the starved stream did not repair it. This was reachable by anyone staging a migration in waves; it was safe only in the one shipped multi-stream shape (a fleet of same-scope streams), where the rescope is a no-op rewrite. MySQL, PlanetScale, and Vitess sources were never affected — they have no publication.

Two changes close it. First, the load-bearing one: a cold start that would **remove** tables from a publication another *active* sluice replication slot is reading now refuses with `SLUICE-E-CDC-PUBLICATION-SCOPE-CONFLICT`, naming the at-risk tables and the conflicting slot — and refuses *before* mutating anything, so a refused attempt leaves every running stream untouched. Second, `sync start` gains **`--publication-name`**, the sibling of `--slot-name` with the same `sluice_` prefix convention (`--publication-name wave1` manages `sluice_wave1`), which is how you legitimately run several differently-scoped streams off one Postgres source. The default is unchanged, so existing deployments keep `sluice_pub` and upgrade without any change in behaviour — a stream-id-derived default was deliberately rejected because the publication name is sent on every `START_REPLICATION`, including warm resume, and would have broken every running Postgres stream on upgrade.

Only *narrowing* rescopes are guarded: widening or equal-scope rescopes remove nothing and never reach the check, so the fleet shape and the additive `schema add-table` path are unaffected. The regression is pinned end to end by a new integration gate that runs two concurrent Postgres-source streamers with disjoint scopes and asserts both targets receive their own changes — verified non-vacuous by running it against deliberately-disabled fix code, where it fails at exactly the starvation assertion.

### Documentation

- `docs/operator/staged-wave-migration.md` and the sluicesync.com staged-wave guide now document the supported per-wave recipe on Postgres (`--slot-name` **and** `--publication-name` per stream) in place of the interim "run waves sequentially" workaround.

## [0.99.286] - 2026-07-22

**A continuous `sync` now rides out routine transient source-side errors instead of exiting.** Found by a multi-day soak against real PlanetScale and Cloudflare D1: three ordinary network/upstream blips each terminated a healthy long-running stream. Nothing was ever at risk of data loss — every failure was loud and every restart resumed cleanly — but a sync that needs a manual restart after each blip isn't operationally usable. If you run continuous sync for days at a time, upgrade.

### Fixed / Changed

**A continuous `sync` no longer exits on a routine transient source-side error — it reconnects and resumes in process.** Long-running syncs were terminating on ordinary network/upstream blips that should have been retried, because the error never reached the retry loop in a shape it recognized. sluice's pipeline retries a CDC reader failure only when the error is classified as retriable (`ir.RetriableError`), and three real shapes were falling through as terminal — each observed killing a multi-day soak against real infrastructure:

- **PlanetScale/Vitess (VStream):** a long-lived stream drop surfaces as gRPC `code = Internal desc = server closed the stream without sending trailers`. `Internal` is deliberately *not* in the retriable code set (a genuine vtgate fault must stay loud), so the transport-level abnormal-close wordings are now discriminated and classified retriable while a real `Internal` fault still fails fast.
- **Cloudflare D1 (`d1-trigger` / `sqlite-trigger`):** the D1 HTTP transport had no classification at all, so a `TLS handshake timeout` — or a Cloudflare-side `HTTP 500` — ended the stream. Transport failures and the transient status family (408, 429, 5xx) are now retried; a 4xx meaning the *request* is wrong (bad token, missing database, malformed statement) stays terminal so a real misconfiguration still fails loudly.
- **Postgres trigger-CDC (`pgtrigger`):** the same structural gap — a bare poll error with no classification — now classifies transport-level transients. (SQLSTATE-level transients such as `57P01` / `57P03` / `08006` are not yet covered and remain a follow-up.)

No data was ever at risk from these: each failure was loud, the CDC position is durable, and every restart warm-resumed cleanly. The retry budget is unchanged (`--apply-retry-attempts`, default 8), so a genuinely persistent outage still exhausts it and fails loudly rather than spinning forever. The D1 and pgtrigger classifiers are one shared, pinned implementation (`internal/engines/internal/triggercdc`) so the transient set can't drift apart between the trigger-CDC engines.

### Documentation

- New operator guide `docs/operator/staged-wave-migration.md` — moving a database a few tables at a time: the two mechanisms, which one to use on which source engine (they are not interchangeable — see the Postgres caveat), how foreign keys constrain wave ordering, and the one thing sluice deliberately does not do (no target→source write-back).
- ADR-0175 (Postgres publication scope isolation — `--publication-name` + the clobber refusal), ADR-0176 (PG publication row-filter push-down), ADR-0177 (PG publication column lists — capability survey, no adoption), ADR-0178 (an analytical target class — `ir.ChangeSink` as the sibling of `ChangeApplier`).

## [0.99.285] - 2026-07-20

**Dependency and base-image maintenance — no functional change.** Routine updates to third-party Go dependencies and the runtime container's base image. sluice's own behavior is identical to v0.99.284; this release exists so the published binaries and the GHCR runtime image carry the current dependency set and the latest base-OS security patches.

### Dependencies

- Bumped `google.golang.org/grpc` to 1.82.0, the `aws-sdk-go-v2` group (`aws-sdk-go-v2` 1.42.1 + `config` / `credentials` / `service/kms` / `service/s3`), `github.com/klauspost/compress` to 1.19.0, `golang.org/x/crypto` to 0.54.0 and `golang.org/x/sync` to 0.22.0, and `github.com/mattn/go-isatty` to 0.0.22.
- Refreshed the `distroless/static-debian12` base image digest, so the multi-arch GHCR runtime image (`ghcr.io/sluicesync/sluice`) picks up the latest base-OS patches.

### Compatibility

- **No behavior change.** All updates are patch/minor dependency bumps and a base-image digest refresh; every code path is identical to v0.99.284.

## [0.99.284] - 2026-07-20

**Collation and float fidelity fixes for `sync --where` on MariaDB and PlanetScale/Vitess.** Three small, self-contained correctness fixes to continuous filtered sync, from the audit follow-ups after v0.99.283. If you don't use `sync --where`, nothing changes; `migrate --where` (source-evaluated) is unaffected throughout.

### Fixed / Changed
