Pinned by the redaction table, the banner-through-construction path, both fetch-error shapes, and a functional-userinfo-retention pin.
- **`file://` backup URLs now create owner-only 0700 directories and WARN about the residual file posture (loose-permissions hardening; since blob-store URLs landed in v0.16.0, through v0.99.204).** A `file:///` `--backup-target` routes to gocloud's fileblob, whose defaults are 0666 files / 0777 directories — silently looser than the hardened 0600/0700 local store `--output-dir` uses. The URL annotator now defaults fileblob's directory-mode option to 0700 unless the operator set their own, and store open WARNs that the FILES remain world-readable-minus-umask (fileblob exposes no per-file mode option), steering to `--output-dir`. The file posture is surfaced, not silently mutated; existing directories keep their modes.

### Fixed

- **The `--reset-target-data` destructive-confirm gate now has an honest abort contract across json and text modes, a Ctrl-C-able prompt, and a second-SIGINT escape hatch (the json envelope lie since the envelope work landed in v0.99.175, the abort-exits-0 and swallowed prompt Ctrl-C since the confirm gate landed in v0.6.0; through v0.99.204; audit §4.4).** An aborted confirm returned nil, so `--format json` rendered `{"status": "completed"}` for a run that never happened, with the prompt text polluting the one-JSON-object stdout contract. The abort now returns a declined sentinel (generic exit 1), the envelope renders the new status `"aborted"`, and the prompt writes to stderr in json mode — at all three embedding sites: `migrate`, `sync start`, `sync from-backup run` (text-only there). The prompt read also races the signal context so Ctrl-C at the prompt aborts instead of being swallowed by the blocking stdin read, and the signal wiring arms a second-signal escape hatch — the first SIGINT/SIGTERM keeps the graceful drain, a second exits 130 (128+SIGINT) immediately. Pinned by the {migrate, sync start, from-backup} × {json where present, text} × {confirm, abort} matrix through the real command `Run()`, the envelope `"aborted"` shape, prompt ctx-cancel in both orders, and the signal counter via direct invocation (Windows-safe).
- **`sync status --all` now exits non-zero when EVERY configured target is unreachable (the fleet roll-up's whole life, v0.99.132 through v0.99.204).** The per-target failure isolation degenerated when the whole fleet was dark: an empty table plus exit 0 read as "no streams" to any script checking `$?`. Partial reachability keeps exit 0 with the per-target lines; total unreachability returns a plain error (generic exit 1) after the per-target diagnosis lines. Both shapes and the exit code pinned through the real exit-code mapping.
- **D1 `--stage-local` staging honors Ctrl-C and no longer leaks its temp dir (since `--stage-local` landed in v0.99.167, through v0.99.204).** Staging ran on `context.Background()` because engine resolution predated the signal wiring — a Ctrl-C during the potentially minutes-long D1 replica neither stopped the HTTP paging nor released the `sluice-d1-stage-*` temp dir until process kill. Engine resolution now threads the signal-aware context into staging, and the existing error-path cleanup removes the temp dir on cancellation. Pinned: a canceled ctx aborts before any network I/O and leaves no temp dir.
- **`migratestate.Store.Read` is now genuinely read-only — the ADR-0082 legacy-format upgrade moves to the first write path (since the per-table progress rework in v0.99.34, through v0.99.204).** Read performed the one-way legacy-blob upgrade — a whole write transaction — inside a nominally read-only call: it failed under a read-only target user and locked older binaries out of a row the operator only inspected. Read now records a needs-upgrade note; the first write for that migration id runs the unchanged upgrade transaction exactly once under the store mutex, exploding the blob into rows BEFORE flipping the header sentinel so no progress can be lost, and a clear or already-upgraded re-read drops the note so stale blob progress can never replay. Pinned by scripted-driver units (legacy Read issues only the header SELECT; first write runs the byte-identical upgrade exactly once; failure rolls back and retries) plus both engines' real-DB integration pins on ACTUALLY read-only servers (PG `default_transaction_read_only`, MySQL `super_read_only`).
- **Diagnose bundles now write a `sections.json` accounting for every section — written / skipped (reason) / failed (reason) — so a bundle can no longer silently omit a section (since bundles landed in v0.75.0, through v0.99.204).** ~25 section writes discarded their errors, so a failed section vanished with no trace exactly when someone was debugging with the bundle. Per-section failures still never fail the whole bundle (only the manifest and `sections.json` are structural), and the writer encodes BEFORE creating the zip entry, so a failed encode no longer leaves a phantom empty file masquerading as a section. Additive; manifest version unchanged. Pinned by forcing a real section-encode failure through the production path.
- **`appliercontrol` refuses an all-zero sizing config loudly instead of silently degenerating to permanent 1-row apply batches (defensive; the clamp shape dates to the controller's introduction in v0.72.0, through v0.99.204 — no shipped production path could reach it).** Ceiling has no sensible package default (it is the operator's `--apply-batch-size` cap), so the constructor refuses the all-zero shape naming the fields and the way out; deliberate PARTIAL configs keep their documented clamps (pinned). Both production construction sites always pass Floor 1 / Ceiling > 1, so this is a tripwire for future callers, not a behavior change.
- **Sustained sqlite-trigger WAL-checkpoint failures escalate DEBUG → WARN after 3 consecutive failures (since the cadenced checkpoint landed in v0.99.152, through v0.99.204).** A failing cadenced `wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` was visible only at `--log-level=debug`, so a silently growing source WAL had no default-visibility signal. Consecutive failures now escalate to WARN naming the streak; a single routine failure (a writer held the WAL lock this cycle) stays DEBUG and the first success resets the streak. Still non-fatal. Pinned across the boundary in both directions.

### Internal

- **Lint headroom + hygiene (audit §4.4):** the backup-codec envelope parser sat at exactly the gocyclo ceiling and is now split along tag-family seams with verbatim case bodies (byte-identical decode; the existing decode-differential, malformed-envelope, and Bug-74 family-matrix suites pass unchanged); the dead-code trio (`triggerSetupExampleSchema`, `quoteForPrometheusLabelValue`, `LatencyWindow.Reset`) is deleted with its stranded imports; a stale-named mysql file is renamed to match its contents; 25 verb-less `fmt.Errorf` calls become `errors.New` per the house rule (byte-identical messages); `waitForStopComplete`'s comment no longer promises an exit 2 the code never returned (exit 1 is the right taxonomy shape, now pinned so code and comment can't drift); the crash-hook doc no longer names `sync from-backup run` as an embedder; and the envelope-status docs gain the `"aborted"` vocabulary.

## [0.99.204] - 2026-07-09

### Fixed

- **`backup verify --rebuild-catalog` no longer stamps zstd on the rebuilt lineage record regardless of the chunks' actual compression — it sniffs the codec from chunk magic bytes and refuses loudly when it cannot prove one (wrong-heal on the DR path; loud failure, no silent loss; every release carrying the rebuild tool, v0.67.0 through v0.99.203; audit N-14).** A gzip- or none-compressed chain whose `lineage.json` was lost got a rebuilt catalog that LIED — restore trusts the record, so restore/prune/compact died on a bare zstd decode error whose remediation hint pointed back at the very rebuild tool that wrote the wrong record. The rebuilt record's codec is now sniffed from one chunk per manifest and verified to agree across the chain (disjoint deterministic signatures: zstd `28 B5 2F FD`, gzip `1F 8B`, uncompressed `{` — the codec input always begins with the header line's `{`); mixed probes, unknown magic, and truncated chunks all refuse — the durable-record path never guesses. Encrypted chains carry no codec magic on disk (the codec sits INSIDE the encryption envelope), so a keyless rebuild of an encrypted chain now refuses with a steer to `--encrypt`, and a keyed rebuild decrypts one chunk through the same ADR-0152 unwrap/AAD chokepoints restore uses and sniffs the plaintext. The read-only synthetic-root path (verify/prune/broker without `lineage.json`) sniffs best-effort and WARNs naming the default-codec assumption when it cannot probe, so keyless flows like SHA-only verify keep working; restore itself still never sniffs. Pinned per the family discipline across all three codecs (magic table, real-writer bytes, encrypted layout), the refusal shapes, and the DR scenario end-to-end per codec family — backup, delete `lineage.json`, rebuild, restore — including the encrypted leg.
- **Fleet `syncs.yaml` explicit zeros now mean what the flags document (silent config inversion, fleet layer only; the sync command center's whole life, v0.99.132 through v0.99.203; audit N-11).** An explicit `apply-exec-timeout: 0` / `heartbeat-interval: 0` / `max-buffer-bytes: 0` was coerced to the 60s/60s/64MiB defaults, while the matching `sync start` flags document "0 disables" (and `--max-buffer-bytes=0` = no orchestrator cap) and pass an explicit 0 through verbatim — "unset" and "explicit 0" were conflated on the fleet layer only; and an explicit `apply-retry-attempts: 0` (or either backoff key at 0) was silently absorbed into the ADR-0038 default where the CLI refuses the same value out-of-range. The three "0 disables" knobs are now pointer-typed in the spec (key omitted → the `sync start` flag default; a present value, INCLUDING 0, passes through verbatim; YAML key names unchanged), the retry trio reaches the CLI's validator verbatim and refuses with the identical out-of-range message, and the SIGHUP hot-reload fingerprint distinguishes explicit-0 from omitted, so flipping between them restarts the sync. Pinned through the REAL parsers (the Bug-180 lesson): the {omitted, explicit 0, non-zero} matrix through `loadFleetConfig`, the retry refusal through the load path with the CLI-computed message, and a kong-parity pin proving `sync start` and the fleet keys produce identical Streamer values.

### Changed

- **The `postgres-trigger` change-log is on an index diet and the capture trigger's PK list is baked at setup time — ~39% faster insert churn on the source under capture (1850 ms → 1133 ms per 200k rows), change-log index footprint 16 MB → 4.4 MB (audit N-16; both write shapes date to the engine's introduction in v0.85.0).** Setup created two indexes on `sluice_change_log` that no engine query has ever read — `sluice_change_log_id_idx ON (id)`, an exact duplicate of the BIGSERIAL PK's implicit index, and `sluice_change_log_table_idx ON (schema_name, table_name, id)`, re-verified unused against the full query inventory (poll, anchor, the v0.99.202 settle-clamp, prune/auto-prune batches, stats/recount, teardown — all key on `id` and `txid` alone) — both maintained on EVERY captured source DML for zero read benefit, on precisely the managed (Heroku-class) tiers where the operator pays for source IOPS. Setup no longer creates them and emits idempotent `DROP INDEX IF EXISTS` for both names, so any pre-existing install converges on its next setup re-run (no migration machinery — pre-1.0). The capture trigger also re-derived the table's PK column list from a `pg_constraint`/`pg_attribute` catalog join per fired row while ADR-0066 §3's own TG_ARGV bake sat vestigial; the bake is now real (setup loads each table's PK columns in conkey order, `CREATE TRIGGER` carries them as a JSON array in `TG_ARGV[0]`, composite PKs unchanged), with a loud stale-bake guard: if a baked PK column stops matching the row image (e.g. after a post-setup ALTER of the PK), the trigger refuses the write naming the remedy — re-run trigger setup — never rows keyed on the wrong columns. Pinned by DDL-shape unit pins plus integration pins for fresh setup and pre-v0.99.204 convergence, composite-PK end-to-end across all three payload modes, and the stale-bake loud refusal.
- **The MySQL schema phases no longer pay one catalog round trip per index/FK — the three per-object information_schema probes are chunked IN-list batch reads (audit V-1).** Index verification (the `SLUICE-E-INDEX-MISSING` net), the detect-then-skip probe before index builds, and the per-FK constraint check now share one `table_name IN (…) AND <name> IN (…)` catalog read per ~400 wanted objects with a Go-side set compare, instead of one serial round trip per object. On a vtgate/PlanetScale target every probe fans out through the cluster, so a hundreds-of-index schema paid hundreds of serial RTTs per DDL/verify phase — a 200-index schema now verifies in one metadata query. Refusal shapes byte-identical (existing pins pass unchanged), detect-then-skip still probes at attempt time so the ADR-0108 reparent-retry replay never double-creates, every value rides a parameter (or the flavor's ADR-0153 interpolated equivalent), and one named wart is pinned: the Go-side compare case-folds both sides, matching the case-insensitive comparison the old DB-side `=` probes did under information_schema's collation and `lower_case_table_names` targets. Ground-truthed on real MySQL and real vtgate. Perf-parity matrix row 19 declares the per-mode coverage.
- **The VStream cold-copy snapshot drain — the engines' last unbuffered `ir.Row` channel — is now buffered (64) with batched lock pops (16 rows per acquisition, one cond broadcast per batch instead of per row) (audit P′-1).** Removes a client-side ceiling on faster PlanetScale tiers. Byte-budget backpressure is unchanged by construction: the debit still happens at pop time under the same lock, so the bounded-memory cap-wait and the cross-table loud refusal are untouched (both pins pass unchanged); the buffer merely holds ≤ 64+16 already-debited rows in flight — the same bounded-overshoot class every buffered tee carries.

### Internal

- **Testing-guard hardening + a real kmsverify suite (audit N-17).** The run-filter coverage guard now auto-discovers the build-tag axis set from `*_test.go` `//go:build` lines and fails CI Lint on any tag with neither a manifest axis nor a documented exemption — a future `integration && newtag` suite can no longer type-check, pass both coverage guards, and run in no workflow — and cross-checks every manifest regex verbatim against the workflow file its leg names. The shard guard's matching now mirrors ci.yml's actual non-recursive package listing (bare entries no longer cover subtrees), pinned by an always-run self-test asserting `internal/pipeline` does NOT cover `internal/pipeline/migcore` — previously an integration test under a pipeline subpackage could pass the guard while running in no shard. The kmsverify scaffolding went from all-skip to four real tests against a localstack KMS container + real PG (round-trip, chain extension, missing-key and wrong-key loud refusals) on a weekly + dispatch extended-suites leg under `-race`; only the IAM access-denied case remains an honest skip (localstack community edition doesn't enforce IAM; unit-pinned instead). Both guard fixes negative-proven with staged fake escapees.

## [0.99.203] - 2026-07-08

### Fixed

- **MySQL-source FLOAT columns are no longer silently precision-rounded on read pages that travel the text protocol (HIGH; silent precision loss — exit 0, zero WARN; MySQL/PlanetScale/Vitess sources; every release, v0.1.0 through v0.99.202; discovered by ADR-0153's fidelity matrix).** Any read page issued WITHOUT bind parameters travels MySQL's text protocol (COM_QUERY), and MySQL's FLOAT→text conversion does NOT round-trip single precision (ground-truthed on 8.0.46: a stored float32 8388608, verifiably exact, renders "8388610") — so sluice received an already-rounded value and landed the wrong number on the target. Affected pages, precisely: the arg-less full-table scan `ReadRows` (small/keyless tables, the backup and binlog-snapshot row readers) and the FIRST unbounded page of every cursor-paged read (its LIMIT is inlined; subsequent bound pages went prepared/binary and were always exact). DOUBLE was never affected — shortest-round-trip printing, now pinned. Fix: single-precision FLOAT reads through the projection `(col * 1E0) AS col` — an exact, sign-preserving, version-universal promotion to DOUBLE whose text form round-trips — at the single projection seam every SQL row reader shares (`selectColumnExpr`, the same seam as the temporal CAST; full-scan, chunked, backup-snapshot, binlog-snapshot readers all inherit it). Deliberately NOT `CAST(col AS DOUBLE)` (8.0.17+ syntax; no floor excludes 5.7-compatible sources like Aurora MySQL 2.x) and NOT `col + 0E0` (IEEE −0.0 + 0.0 = +0.0 drops the sign; multiplication preserves it). Pinned failing-first (the raw pre-fix SELECT provably returns the rounded value — doubling as a retire-the-fix canary) and mutation-verified: the float32 torture matrix (2^23, ±MaxFloat32, denormals, −0.0) bit-exact through the real full-scan reader, cross-checked bit-identical against the binary-protocol chunked reader, DOUBLE/NULL legs, and a vtgate promotion-parsing leg with the −0 sign intact.
- **Two interpolation-encoder divergences the ADR-0153 fidelity matrix forced, fixed on both protocols and all flavors before the flip shipped:** float64 −0.0 now encodes as the string `'-0'` — the interpolated literal `-0` parses as unary-minus-on-unsigned-zero (plain +0), silently dropping the IEEE sign bit the binary protocol preserves — pinned with ABSOLUTE stored-text assertions on every leg (bulk, CDC, vtgate) against a raw binary-protocol control, including type-free on the applier's cache-miss/unknown-column branches; and NaN/±Inf now refuse loudly with `SLUICE-E-VALUE-UNREPRESENTABLE`, naming the column, before the driver sees the value — previously an interpolated NaN drew an Error-1054 shape the schema-drift self-healer retried for ~30 minutes instead of failing fast (the data was never writable either way; no published release carried the interpolated path).

### Changed

- **PlanetScale/Vitess MySQL flavors now default to client-side statement interpolation (`interpolateParams=true` semantics) on write-path connections, collapsing 2 round trips per parameterized statement (COM_STMT_PREPARE + COM_STMT_EXECUTE, prepared fresh per call) to 1 COM_QUERY (ADR-0153; audit N-15a).** Measured A/B on real PlanetScale PS-10 at ~100 ms client RTT: bulk copy −33% (bulk_copy phase), CDC burst drain −26%, CDC steady-state per-flush p95 −105 ms ≈ 1.04× the median RTT (the smoking gun: the entire gap is the hidden PREPARE), and a ~35% higher per-lane trickle flush ceiling (~2.4 → ~3.3 flushes/s); absolute deltas scale with RTT. The decision is made once at the DSN parse choke point, so every derived connection (applier lanes, shard-discovery/purged-GTID probes, snapshot pools, resnapshot re-parses) inherits it; one INFO line announces the resolved protocol. Explicit DSN `interpolateParams=` always wins, true or false (explicitness detected on the RAW DSN — the driver's parser collapses "explicitly false" with "absent"; `WithDatabase` re-materializes an explicit false on derived DSNs). Vanilla MySQL keeps the binary protocol by default, with `interpolateParams=true` as the documented high-RTT DSN opt-in (docs/throughput-tuning.md). Every guard keys on the RESOLVED interpolation state, never the flavor, so a vanilla opt-in gets identical protections: unsafe collations AND charsets (big5/cp932/gb2312/gbk/sjis/gb18030 families — trailing-byte 0x5C injection hazard) make the DEFAULT step aside with a WARN (every `charset=` occurrence scanned, any unsafe entry disqualifies — the driver's own check reads only the collation and misses `charset=` entirely), while explicit-true + unsafe is a loud parse-time refusal, the collation list pinned two-sided against the driver's; NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES needs no guard — ground-truthed correct (the driver switches escapers on the server status flag; pinned byte-exact under a real NBE session, and the pin flips the posture to refusal if a driver upgrade ever changes it); packet headroom is pinned at both legs (the ~1 MiB ADR-0150/0139 statement targets sit far under every max_allowed_packet, and at the true boundary the driver's `ErrSkip` transparently falls back to the prepared path). Row-data READ sessions deliberately keep the prepared/binary protocol on all flavors (the FLOAT fix above is why: the prepared path's fidelity never depends on the server's text formatter). Fidelity evidence per the Bug-74 family discipline, pinned BEFORE the flip: the full driver-value family matrix (microsecond temporals, DECIMAL(20,6)/(65,30) extremes, DOUBLE extremes/denormals, VARBINARY/BLOB across all 256 octets, 4-byte UTF-8 with quotes/backslashes/NUL, BIGINT (UNSIGNED) extremes, BIT/SET/ENUM/JSON/GEOMETRY, zero-date substitutes, per-column NULLs) byte-exact under BOTH protocols on both write paths (bulk batched INSERT + CDC coalesced apply) on real vanilla MySQL, plus a third-codec vtgate leg on a real vttestserver; the end-to-end wire pin observes Com_stmt_prepare = 0 on the default path through the engine registry. Perf-parity matrix row 18 declares the per-mode coverage.

## [0.99.202] - 2026-07-08

### Fixed

- **The `postgres-trigger` snapshot→CDC cold-start handoff no longer silently loses the changes of a transaction that was still open when the anchor query ran (HIGH; silent loss class — exit 0, zero WARN; present in every release of the engine, v0.85.0 through v0.99.201; epoch-INDEPENDENT and distinct from v0.99.201's xid8 fix; live-confirmed 2026-07-08).** A change-log row INSERTed by a still-open transaction is MVCC-invisible to the anchor query, so the anchor's `MIN(id)-1` hold-down arm could land ABOVE that transaction's already-allocated lower id — its changes were in neither the bulk copy (the transaction hadn't committed at the snapshot) nor the `id > anchor` CDC tail. This is the residual v0.99.201's notes documented and deliberately did not bundle; the v0.86.1 Bug-94 anchor rework and v0.99.201's xid8 fix each narrowed the cold-start gap class but could not close this hole. Fix, in `OpenSnapshotStream` after the anchor query on the pinned snapshot connection: export the snapshot's visibility horizon (`pg_current_snapshot()::text`), assign a fresh txid upper bound on the pool (`pg_current_xact_id()` — a freshly ASSIGNED xid, the same own-xid bound logical-decoding slot creation waits on), poll fresh snapshots until every pre-bound transaction settles (5-minute budget, then a loud refusal naming the stuck txids and the `pg_stat_activity` remedy), then clamp the anchor below `MIN(id)` of change-log rows not `pg_visible_in_snapshot` in the copy's horizon; a quiescent source exits on the first poll, no added latency. Stated honestly: the roadmap's `pg_snapshot_xip`-only wait sketch was proven UNSOUND during implementation — snapshot xmax is latestCompletedXid+1, so the only running transaction can sit at/above xmax outside every xip list (live-probed: snapshot `744:744:` with `backend_xid=744` idle-in-transaction); the shipped wait unions snapshot xmin with xip under the assigned bound, and a pin freezes the unsound shape out. Pinned Phase-A failing-first and mutation-verified (both loss pins fail with the clamp disabled): a real-handoff engine test, a real-Streamer cold-start row-missing-on-target repro, the wedged-writer loud-refusal test at the at-/above-xmax shape, and the quiescent-source first-poll-exit test.
- **A PlanetScale storage-grow reparent landing mid-`ALTER … ADD INDEX` no longer aborts the migrate — both MySQL index-overlap paths ride the transient out (loud failure, `--resume` recovered, no data risk; the vanilla concurrent-worker path since the overlap landed in v0.99.30 through v0.99.201, the Vitess/PlanetScale serial build path since it landed in v0.99.199 through v0.99.201; the Postgres overlap has carried this ride-out since v0.99.120).** Both paths called `buildTableIndexes` bare, so a reparent mid-ALTER killed the migrate on exactly the platform where reparents happen, while the writer's own transient classifier went unused there. The fix mirrors the PG shape in-engine: a pure `retryIndexBuildWithReparent` policy reusing the ADR-0108 cold-copy reparent envelope (same attempt/backoff variables, wall-clock bound, loud exhaustion) and `classifyApplierError`; the worker path re-acquires a fresh pinned `*sql.Conn` after a transient, the serial path rides the pooled `*sql.DB`'s implicit reacquire. Replay is double-create-safe because `buildTableIndexes` re-probes detect-then-skip (Bug 131) — a partially-applied or committed-but-unacked combined ALTER is skipped, never re-emitted — and build-then-mark is unchanged (`IndexesBuilt` fires only after a table's build truly succeeds). Pinned by the PG-mirror policy set (rides-transient / terminal-unchanged / reacquire-rides-budget / loud-exhaustion / ctx-cancel) plus wiring pins through `BuildTableIndexesFromChannel` on both paths and both VStream flavors, including the no-double-create-after-mid-ALTER-retry pin; perf-parity-matrix row 17 declares the per-mode coverage. Live-validated on real PlanetScale post-merge (migrate with unique/plain/composite index families: all indexes land, `SLUICE-E-INDEX-MISSING` verification green).

### Security

- **Encrypted backup chunks are now cryptographically bound to their backup identity and recorded position, closing chunk splice/replay/reorder/substitution by a store-level adversary (ADR-0152, manifest FormatVersion 5; encrypted backups provided confidentiality ONLY since envelope encryption landed in v0.22.0, through v0.99.201 — while SECURITY.md's shared-storage framing and two in-code comments implied integrity that did not exist).** Chunk encryption was AES-GCM with nil AAD, the manifest's SHA-256 entries are adversary-writable, and per-chain mode (the default) shares one CEK across the chain — so any valid ciphertext chunk decrypted cleanly wherever a rewritten manifest pointed it, and the restore exited 0. Under a FormatVersion-5 manifest every chunk carries GCM AAD binding the ciphertext to the manifest's identity prefix (CreatedAt/engine/kind — deliberately NOT the BackupID, which hashes the EndPosition unknown at chunk-write time; row-chunk index/path allocation moved from flush to open so the writer knows the path it binds) plus the chunk's recorded path, and change chunks additionally bind their list ordinal (replay order is semantic; row chunks deliberately don't bind it — a table's rows are a set and the ADR-0149 range workers legitimately append out of index order). A tampered, spliced, reordered, or cross-backup-replayed chunk fails the auth check at open, before any row or event is emitted, naming the class. The chain CEK's wrap is likewise identity-bound through the new `crypto.BoundEnvelope` surface — AWS KMS `EncryptionContext` (service-authenticated, CloudTrail-visible), GCP KMS AAD, GCM AAD on the passphrase wrap — routed through two lineage chokepoints (`WrapChainCEK`/`UnwrapChainCEK`) gating on the OWNING manifest's recorded version, never try-both (try-both would convert the integrity check into a silent downgrade oracle); Azure Key Vault deliberately does NOT implement it (KeyWrap has no context parameter; faking one client-side would claim integrity the service cannot provide — pinned as a non-implementation; Azure's splice resistance rests on the chunk AAD, the load-bearing defense everywhere). Stamping is proportional (Bug-116 discipline): only encrypted manifests get v5; plaintext backups keep their versions; resuming a pre-v5 encrypted in-progress full keeps the prior version and unbound shape (its kept chunks were written unbound). Honest boundary, documented in ADR-0152 + SECURITY.md: the manifest is UNSIGNED, so whole-backup rollback and change-list tail truncation by a store adversary remain out of scope until a signed-manifest design lands. Pinned by golden derivation pins (the AAD strings are on-disk contract), the e2e tamper matrix (content swap with SHAs fixed up / entry reorder / same-CEK cross-backup replay all refuse; untampered restores), the BoundEnvelope accept/refuse matrix over all three implementing families (Bug-74 discipline, fakes upgraded to enforce context/AAD equality), and the pre-v5 compat restore proving old chains restore byte-identically.
- **Azure Key Vault chains no longer break on restore when key auto-rotation is enabled: the wrap-time key version is pinned at wrap and recorded in the manifest's KEKRef (audit N-9; Azure KMS chains since the envelope landed in v0.34.0, through v0.99.201).** The unwrap targeted the vault's LATEST key version and discarded the WrapKey response `KID`, while the code comment claimed Azure recovers the wrap-time version from blob metadata — that is AWS/GCP behavior; Azure key-wrap ciphertext carries none. Enabling rotation (a common vault policy) made restores of older chains fail with a misleading "wrong key or tampered". Now the GetKey preflight resolves the operator's unversioned key URL to a concrete version, WrapKey responses reinforce the pin, `ResolvedKEKRef` records the VERSIONED key URL in `ChainEncryption.KEKRef` (per-chain and per-chunk modes), and the unwrap retargets the envelope at the recorded wrap-time version via the new `ChainKEKRebinder` surface — advisory by contract (the ref comes from an unauthenticated manifest): a malformed/foreign ref WARNs and keeps the current target, whose unwrap then fails loudly if wrong. Pre-v0.99.202 chains (unversioned KEKRef) keep the latest-version behavior plus a once-per-envelope WARN naming the rotation hazard and the recovery (pass the wrap-time version explicitly). `backup compact`'s encryption-binding tuple now includes KEKRef, so Azure segments wrapped under different key versions refuse to merge (loud, correct: their unwrap targets genuinely differ). Pinned by the version-pin/ResolvedKEKRef/rebind matrix including a simulated auto-rotation, with the fake reproducing Azure's real no-version-metadata semantics.
- **Restore now actually performs the chunk-header and SchemaHash checks two in-code comments claimed existed (hardening; the checks were absent in all prior releases).** `Restore.streamChunkRows` validates every chunk header's column set against the manifest schema's non-generated columns (set equality, source-shaped, pre-retarget; the first non-test caller of `ChunkReader.Header()`) and refuses with the table, chunk, and both column deltas before any row is emitted — for plaintext and encrypted chunks alike, on single-full restore, every chain segment, and the broker's reset leg. `ChainRestore.verifySchemaHashes` recomputes and compares every link's recorded `SchemaHash` before anything lands on the target — honestly a corruption check, not tamper-proofing (the hash lives in the same unsigned manifest as the schema; the field's comment now says so). Write-side companions: fulls now record `SchemaHash` (previously only incrementals/streams did) and the item-51 no-DDL sequence-refresh swap re-stamps the hash over the swapped schema, closing the wart the old comment predicted; one named carve-out WARNs and proceeds for pre-v5 manifests carrying standalone sequences (old writers legitimately swapped sequence state without re-hashing — refusing legitimate old chains on the DR path would be worse than the narrow miss); v5+ manifests get no carve-out. Pinned by the renamed-column header refusal (SHA fixed up), the SchemaHash mismatch refusal firing before any target phase, and the carve-out pin.

### Internal

- **New pipeline-level VStream-target index-overlap e2e pin (audit T′-1).** The overlap wiring had `-race` unit pins and vanilla e2e pins, but no test drove the pipeline's flavor routing end-to-end against a VStream-flavor target — the routing where the v0.99.199 silent-index-loss bug lived (the engine-level vstream pin pre-fills the completed-tables channel by hand). `TestMigrate_VStreamTarget_IndexOverlap_EngagesAndBuildsAll` boots a real vttestserver as the migrate TARGET plus a vanilla MySQL source and runs a real 8-table migrate through `Migrator.Run`, asserting every expected secondary index lands (information_schema ground truth, independent of the `SLUICE-E-INDEX-MISSING` net), the overlap genuinely engaged on the VStream branch (index-build-start before last copy-complete, via the seams), and no back-pressure/deadlock under a deliberately slow serial builder. The name matches the extended-suites vstream-pipeline `-run` filter and the coverage guard passes (negative-proofed).
- **Audit finding N-5 ("SHOW CREATE recovery decoders are not NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES-aware") REFUTED live; the invariant is now named and pinned (zero behavior change).** Phase-A ground truth on MySQL 8.0.46 and 8.4.10: `SHOW CREATE TABLE` and information_schema `COLUMN_TYPE` print literals with a FIXED escape set in all four {default, NBE} read-session × creation-session cells — `NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES` changes only the PARSE direction, never the PRINT direction — so the unconditional decode is correct in every reachable mode, and the audit's proposed fix (threading sqlMode into the decode) would itself have corrupted recovered bytes. Landed: a live byte-exact integration pin over every literal-bearing decode family × {default strict, NBE} reader sessions, reached through the real `--mysql-sql-mode` plumbing (the Bug-180 layer) and failing loudly if a future MySQL ever prints mode-dependently; the "mode-independent print discipline" invariant named at the decode sites so the one-sided-looking asymmetry is never "completed" into a real bug; and the documented print escape set corrected to the observed one.

## [0.99.201] - 2026-07-08

### Fixed

- **The `postgres-trigger` engine's safety-lag hold-back and snapshot→CDC handoff anchor now compare in the 64-bit xid8 domain, closing a latent silent-data-gap on Postgres clusters whose XID epoch is ≥ 1 (HIGH; silent loss class — exit 0, zero WARN; the hold-back affected v0.85.0 through v0.99.200, the anchor v0.86.1 through v0.99.200; fires only once a cluster's lifetime txid count crosses 2^32, routine on long-lived busy PG).** Both queries compared the change-log row's system `xmin` — a 32-bit, epoch-less `xid` — against `pg_snapshot_xmin(pg_current_snapshot())` — a 64-bit, epoch-carrying `xid8` (the in-code comment treated the cast as a JSON-precision detail, not a cross-domain comparison). At epoch 0 the domains coincide and everything worked; past 2^32 lifetime txids the poll's hold-back predicate becomes ALWAYS true — the watermark advances past an in-flight transaction's already-allocated change-log ids and its changes are silently skipped when it commits (the exact overlap-commit gap the predicate exists to prevent) — and the anchor's `>=` arm NEVER matches, so `COALESCE` degenerates to `MAX(id)`, resurfacing the Bug-94 cold-start gap. Live-confirmed on a `pg_resetwal -e 5` epoch-bumped PG 16 (old predicate emitted a committed row while an older txn with a lower change-log id was still open; old anchor returned `MAX(id)`). Fix: both queries now compare the change-log's existing `txid` column — `pg_current_xact_id()::text::bigint`, recorded by the capture trigger, xid8 on BOTH sides — which is what ADR-0066 §2's txid rationale intended all along; `txid` has been NOT NULL since the engine's first release, so existing installs need no `ALTER` and behavior at epoch 0 is unchanged (verified live). A KNOWN pre-existing epoch-INDEPENDENT residual (an in-flight txn's already-allocated row is MVCC-invisible to the anchor query) is documented in ADR-0066 and deliberately not bundled. Pinned by SQL-shape unit tests plus an epoch-bump integration test (real PG 16, `pg_resetwal -e 5`, gates on snapshot xmin > 2^32, pins hold-back-under-overlap and the anchor landing below the unsettled row), mutation-verified against the reverted queries.
- **A typo'd `schema-changes:` value in the fleet `syncs.yaml` now refuses loudly at config load instead of silently enabling source-DDL forwarding (silent config inversion; present since the fleet sync command center landed in v0.99.132, through v0.99.200).** The sole consumer treats every non-`refuse` string as "forward" (case-insensitive), and the fleet path plumbed the value with only an empty-default — kong's enum protected `sync start --schema-changes` only — so `schema-changes: refused|off|no` passed the unknown-key guard and forwarded source DDL against explicit operator intent. `validateSchemaChanges` now refuses anything outside {`forward`, `refuse`} at spec load, naming the sync, the value, and the allowed set; a same-class sweep confirmed it was the fleet spec's only unvalidated string field. Pinned through the real fleet-config load path on a YAML fixture (the Bug-180 lesson), including the omitted-key default.
- **`--include-table` / `--exclude-table` no longer deterministically fails with 42P01 when a standalone sequence's owner table is excluded (loud failure, no data risk; present since standalone sequences became first-class in v0.99.175, through v0.99.200).** `ApplyTableFilter` pruned `schema.Tables` only, so `schema.Sequences` passed through with `OwnedByTable` intact and the Postgres writer emitted `ALTER SEQUENCE … OWNED BY <excluded_table>.<col>` against a table the filter left uncreated — blocking the shipped copy-table-subset guide's path for any re-optioned serial whose owner falls outside the subset. The fix is in the engine-neutral filter (every caller — migrate, sync cold-start, backup, restore, diff, preview, verify, multidb — inherits it): a sequence whose owner fails the filter is dropped with a WARN naming the sequence and its excluded owner. Dropping the WHOLE sequence rather than just the ownership is deliberate — `OWNED BY` ties its lifecycle to the excluded column, so carrying it unowned would silently change its semantics; unowned sequences always pass through. Pinned by unit tests over both filter directions plus a PG→PG integration test that reproduced the pre-fix 42P01 with the fix reverted.
- **`SLUICE_*` environment variables can now set underscore-keyed config fields, which were previously SILENTLY ignored (silent config drop; the overlay bug is present since the config layer landed in v0.1.0, each affected field from the release that added it, through v0.99.200).** The env overlay replaced EVERY underscore with a dot, so `SLUICE_KEYSET_SOURCE` resolved to `keyset.source` ≠ `koanf:"keyset_source"` and was dropped — likewise `include_tables`, `exclude_tables`, `namespace_map`, `expression_mappings`; the docs' one worked example (`SLUICE_EXTENSIONS_ALLOW`) was the only shape that worked, and `keyset_source` (a secrets pointer) is exactly what operators put in env. The mapping is now built by reflection over the Config struct's koanf tags (flat keys keep underscores, nested keys map to dotted paths, map-typed keys take the variable's tail as the map key); block-structured keys stay YAML-only. An unrecognized `SLUICE_*` var is skipped with a loud WARN naming it and the valid keys — WARN-and-skip rather than a hard error, deliberately, because legitimate `SLUICE_`-prefixed names exist outside the config file (kong bindings `SLUICE_SOURCE`/`SLUICE_TARGET`/`SLUICE_NOTIFY_*`, exempted, and operator-chosen `env:` secret holders) whose names cannot be enumerated. Also fixed: comma-separated env values now actually split into slice fields (explicit hook mirroring the fleet loader — koanf's default left `"a,b"` as a one-element slice). Pinned by unit tests over the mapping, the WARN-and-skip path, the exemptions, and the comma split.

### Security

- **The native MySQL binlog CDC replication stream now inherits the source DSN's TLS posture instead of ALWAYS riding plaintext (silent TLS downgrade; present on the native `mysql` flavor's binlog CDC path in every release through v0.99.200; VStream/PlanetScale/Vitess flavors were always TLS-by-default and are untouched).** The `BinlogSyncerConfig` was built with no `TLSConfig` at all, so a `tls=true` DSN encrypted every `database/sql` query connection while the replication stream — every replicated row — went cleartext with no warning (loud failure only if the server enforced `require_secure_transport`). The fix clones go-sql-driver's own DSN-resolved TLS config onto the binlog syncer, so the stream gets exactly the transport the query connections negotiate, including a REGISTERED custom `tls=` config's CA pool and client certs. Mapping: absent/`tls=false` → plaintext as before plus a once-per-stream-open WARN; `tls=true` → verifying TLS (ServerName from the DSN host, MinVersion floor TLS 1.2, matching the VStream path); `tls=skip-verify` → encrypted-unverified, WARN on every stream open (the `vstream_insecure_tls` precedent); `tls=preferred` → go-mysql has no try-TLS-then-plaintext mode, so the stream keeps the skip-verify TLS half and REFUSES the plaintext-fallback half (loud open failure against a no-TLS server; set `tls=false` explicitly if plaintext is intended); an unregistered custom name was already refused by `ParseDSN` and is pinned so a driver change can't reopen the hole. Readers built without a DSN (the zero value) are byte-identical to pre-fix. Pinned by a unit mapping table through the real parser plus the transport-WARN matrix, and ground-truthed on real MySQL 8 with self-signed certs (skip-verify streams rows over TLS + warns; `tls=true` refused on BOTH the query and binlog connection, proving the syncer enforces the config; default plaintext still streams + warns).
- **An encrypted backup chain can no longer be silently extended with plaintext chunks when the chain-root manifest is transiently unreadable (silent-plaintext-extension on an error path; present since chain-extension encryption alignment landed in v0.22.1, through v0.99.200).** `ChainRootEncryption` returned nil on ANY store read error and both consumers (`backup incremental`, `backup stream`) branch nil = "parent chain is plaintext" — so a transiently unreadable or corrupt root manifest either silently extended an encrypted chain with plaintext chunks at exit 0 (no `--encrypt`), or emitted the wrong "parent chain is plaintext" refusal actively steering the operator to drop `--encrypt`. It now returns an error: a read/parse failure propagates and both consumers abort with "cannot determine parent chain encryption state (refusing to assume plaintext)", while a genuinely absent root manifest keeps the plaintext-chain semantics. Pinned by lineage unit tests (failing Exists/Get, parent-carried fast path, absent root, plaintext root) and consumer tests across {incremental, stream} × {`--encrypt`, plaintext}; encrypted-chain integration suite re-run green.
- **Argon2id KDF parameters read from a backup manifest are now bounds-checked before key derivation, closing a pre-auth memory/CPU bomb on restore (untrusted-manifest hardening; present since envelope encryption landed in v0.22.0, through v0.99.200).** Params were copied verbatim from `manifest.json` and fed to `argon2.IDKey` BEFORE anything about the manifest is authenticated (the KEK the auth tags depend on is itself derived from them), so a tampered `memory_kib: 4294967295` asked for ~4 TiB and an unbounded iteration count spun for hours. The single chokepoint constructor now refuses params above named bounds — Memory ≤ 2 GiB, Iterations ≤ 64, Parallelism ≤ 32, Salt ≤ 1 KiB — naming the param, value, and bound; legitimate sluice-written chains can never trip them (the only construction site is the fixed 64 MiB / 3 / 4 / 16-byte default and there is no flag to raise it). Pinned by at-bound and over-bound matrices proving the refusal fires before `argon2.IDKey`.
- **Webhook, Slack, and PlanetScale-telemetry URLs — which carry the credential in the URL itself — no longer leak into WARN logs through transport errors (credential-in-logs exposure; webhook/Slack since the metrics alerter landed in v0.99.108, the telemetry signed-URL legs since v0.99.95, through v0.99.200).** Both `url.Parse` (inside request building) and `client.Do` wrap failures in `*url.Error`, whose `Error()` embeds the full URL, and the alerter WARN-logs the error verbatim — so any transport failure printed the secret (a webhook/Slack URL's path IS the credential, per sluice's own redaction code). notify's shared `postJSON` now routes every request-build and transport error through `diagnose.SafeParseError` (the v0.99.197 helper) and names the sink; the same-class sweep covered both legs of the PlanetScale telemetry client, whose scrape URL carries `sig`/`exp` query params (a bearer credential until expiry). Pinned by tests forcing real transport errors against secret-bearing URLs and asserting the secret never appears in the error string.
- **Go toolchain bumped to 1.26.5 for GO-2026-5856 (`crypto/tls` standard-library fix).** govulncheck flagged the vuln as reachable from every TLS-using path in the shipped binary — the SMTP notifier, blob-store chunk IO, the metrics HTTP server, and (as of this release's binlog fix) the binlog CDC syncer. CI and the release build read the version from `go.mod`, so the single bump moves everything.

### Changed

- **`postgres-trigger` setup now refuses PostgreSQL < 13 at preflight (was PG < 9.4).** `pg_current_xact_id()` (capture trigger) and `pg_current_snapshot()` (poller) are PG 13+ and have been required since the engine's first release — a PG 12 source previously passed setup and then failed EVERY write to every triggered table at first DML. The refusal is now loud and early; nothing that worked is dropped.

### Internal

- **CI/workflow hardening (audit N-13 + hygiene).** `build-prebaked-images.yml`: `packages: write` moves from workflow-level to only the publishing bake job, and the `pull_request` path becomes a separate build-only job (read-only token) — previously a same-repo PR fully published the prod-tag images every CI integration job boots, before review; fork behavior unchanged (already read-only). `dependabot-automerge.yml`: `timeout-minutes` added and the major-bump comment step's `steps.meta.outputs.*` interpolations move out of the `run:` script into `env:` vars, so a crafted dependency name can no longer inject shell syntax into a write-scoped `pull_request_target` job. `psverify.yml`: checkout/setup-go SHA-pinned to release.yml's exact pins (it handles live PlanetScale DSN secrets). The vitess/pg version matrices' dispatch `inputs.run` filter now reaches `go test` via `env` instead of `run:` interpolation.
- **Docs truth sweep from the 2026-07-08 audit cross-check.** `docs/dev/development.md` (v2 golangci-lint install path, Go 1.26.x, corrected hook/make-gate descriptions), the MySQL flavor list gains Vitess in CLAUDE.md/README, README `--help` block gains `trigger teardown` + `schema add-table`, CONTRIBUTING's release-flow section rewritten to match actual practice (draft-review gate + five-check publish gate), `docs/architecture.md` module tree updated, operator docs added for `--skip-foreign-keys` / `--control-keyspace` (managed-services) and `--analyze-after` (throughput-tuning), and `docs/dev/roadmap.md` shipped quick-wins marked DONE plus the deferred cross-check findings filed as self-contained entries.

## [0.99.200] - 2026-07-07

### Changed

- **On a PlanetScale / self-hosted Vitess MySQL target, the secondary-index build now OVERLAPS the bulk copy instead of running as a serial post-copy batch — a pure timing improvement, correctness-identical to v0.99.199 (Vitess/PlanetScale MySQL targets only; no flag, default, format, or data-path change).** v0.99.199 fixed the silent secondary-index loss on Vitess/PlanetScale by draining the completed-tables channel to completion and then building every table's indexes serially AFTER all copies finished (drain-all-then-build). v0.99.200 keeps that exact correctness but starts the work earlier: the index builder (`buildEachAsCopiedSerial`) — already running concurrently with the copy pool under one errgroup, consuming the completed-tables channel buffered to the table count — now builds each table's secondary indexes AS that table's copy completes, so the index build of the already-copied tables overlaps the copy of the tables still copying rather than firing in one batch at the end. It remains a single-loop consumer, so there is still exactly ONE `ALTER … ADD INDEX` in flight at any moment — no concurrent ALTERs against vtgate (concurrent ALTERs fighting vtgate's online-DDL / Safe-Migrations queue, not ALTER-during-copy, was why ADR-0080 declined the overlap for Vitess) — and because the channel is buffered to the table count the copy producer is never back-pressured by a slow build. The build reuses the same `indexBuildJobsForTables` + `buildTableIndexes` machinery as `CreateIndexes`, so the emitted SQL (inline-skip set, alphabetical order, one combined `ALTER TABLE … ADD INDEX a, ADD INDEX b` per table, idempotent detect-then-skip) is byte-identical, and it stays build-then-mark: a table's `IndexesBuilt` fires only AFTER its own build succeeds, so a mid-build failure leaves the later tables unmarked and a `--resume` rebuilds them. The `SLUICE-E-INDEX-MISSING` verify net is unchanged and the vanilla-MySQL concurrent index path is byte-identical (only the Vitess/PlanetScale branch changes). Measured on real PlanetScale PS-10 (6 tables × 200k rows, 4 secondary indexes each): the overlap hid roughly 10s of index-build work under the copy for a ~11.6s / ~3.6% faster total migrate — the per-table ~2.5s builds fired immediately after each table's own copy instead of in a ~13s serial batch at the end, leaving only the last table's build unhideable. Stated honestly, at that scale the copy dominates (~95% of the total on a CPU-bound PS-10) so the index build is only a ~4% slice and that bounds the win; the speedup grows on faster tiers (copy shrinks, index build is a larger fraction) and on larger or more-heavily-indexed datasets (more index-build time to hide). It is the same work as v0.99.199, just started earlier — no throughput regression. Pinned by a new unit test asserting the overlap property (a table's indexes build before the channel closes — which times out against the old drain-all behavior) plus the existing vttestserver Vitess integration coverage confirming every index family still lands for both migrate and sync cold-start.

## [0.99.199] - 2026-07-07

### Fixed

- **A `migrate` or `sync` cold-start into a PlanetScale / self-hosted Vitess MySQL target now builds every secondary index instead of silently building none (CRITICAL; silent schema loss — exit 0, zero WARN; regression present in v0.99.30 through v0.99.198).** v0.99.30's ADR-0080 extended the overlapped (during-copy) index build to MySQL targets. That path correctly DECLINES to run concurrent `ALTER … ADD INDEX` against a Vitess/PlanetScale flavor — concurrent ALTERs fight vtgate's online-DDL / Safe-Migrations queue — but the branch it declined into was a no-op (`drainTablesFiringCallback`) that drained the completed-tables channel and marked every table `IndexesBuilt=true` WITHOUT building anything, and a false code comment claimed the post-copy whole-schema `CreateIndexes` ran as a fallback when on the overlapped branch that fall-through was unreachable. So on a Vitess/PlanetScale target every secondary index — plain, unique, composite, and FK-backing — was silently dropped (the target came out with PRIMARY keys only), which also defeated the v0.99.198 `--skip-foreign-keys` "keep the columns indexed" guarantee and turned joins into full scans. Before v0.99.30 MySQL didn't take the overlapped path, so a Vitess migrate built indexes via the sequential `CreateIndexes` and they landed — a regression, not a long-standing gap. Fix, on both migrate and the sync cold-start (they share `BuildTableIndexesFromChannel`): the Vitess/PlanetScale gate now drains the completed-tables channel to completion (never backpressuring the copy producer), then builds each table's indexes SERIALLY post-copy — one table at a time, no concurrent ALTERs against vtgate, byte-identical SQL to `CreateIndexes` (one combined `ALTER TABLE … ADD INDEX a, ADD INDEX b` per table = a single InnoDB scan). It is build-then-mark: `IndexesBuilt` fires only AFTER a table's build succeeds, so a mid-build failure leaves the unbuilt tables unmarked and a `--resume` rebuilds them. This restores the pre-v0.99.30 behavior for Vitess (which never had the overlap — that was plain-MySQL-only, so there is no throughput regression versus how Vitess always behaved), and the vanilla-MySQL overlapped path is byte-identical. Pinned by unit tests (the Vitess path now emits an `ADD INDEX` per table and observes build-then-mark ordering) plus a vttestserver Vitess-target integration test asserting every index family lands for migrate and sync cold-start.
- **New loud-failure safety net `SLUICE-E-INDEX-MISSING` (refusal, exit 3), flavor-agnostic across all MySQL flavors, so this silent-loss class can never recur unnoticed on any engine.** A new optional `ir.IndexVerifier` / `SchemaWriter.VerifyIndexes` surface is wired into the orchestrator immediately after the index phase on both the migrate + fast-parallel cold-start path (`runBulkCopyPhases`) and the serial cold-start path (`runBulkCopyWithOpts`): after the build, sluice probes `information_schema` for exactly the set the build targeted (reusing `indexBuildJobsForTables`, so the inline-emitted keys a `CREATE TABLE` already carries are never false-flagged) and refuses loudly — naming the missing `table.index` list — if any expected secondary index is absent. It is cheap (one catalog read per expected index) and general; engines without the surface are a no-op. Pinned by a `VerifyIndexes` unit matrix that raises the code when an index is missing.

## [0.99.198] - 2026-07-07

### Added

- **`--skip-foreign-keys` (opt-in, on `migrate` and `sync start`): skip creating foreign-key constraints on the target while keeping each skipped FK's referencing column tuple indexed (new capability; off by default, no data-path change).** The primary use case is a target with limited or no FK support — Vitess / PlanetScale sharded keyspaces (cross-shard FKs don't work and FKs are opt-in per database), or any target where FKs are managed out-of-band — so an existing database that HAS foreign keys can be migrated or synced onto it WITHOUT first stripping the FKs from the source. To preserve join performance, for every FK sluice would have created it synthesizes a plain non-unique backing index over the referencing columns in FK-column order, but ONLY when no existing target index already covers those columns as a left-prefix (a PK or secondary index whose leading columns already match is left alone — never a redundant index). This matters most on a MySQL target, where MySQL auto-creates an FK's backing index ONLY when the FK is created, so a naive skip would leave the column unindexed and slow every join through it. The transform runs once on the finalized schema before any DDL phase and applies to both the migrate FK phase and the sync cold-start schema-apply path (and suppresses the multi-database deferred cross-database FK pass); it is mutually exclusive with `--allow-degraded-fks` (opposite intents — refused loudly at validate if both are set). The change is never silent: each skipped FK is reported on its own log line (table, referencing columns, and the synthesized backing index or the existing index that already covered them) plus a summary count of FKs skipped / indexes synthesized / columns already covered. Composite FKs index the full tuple in FK-column order; partial (WHERE) and expression indexes are correctly NOT treated as covers, while a MySQL prefix-LENGTH index IS; re-running is idempotent because the synthesized indexes ride the existing `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS` / detect-then-skip path, so a `--resume` never double-creates them. Unset, behavior is byte-identical to before. Two documented scope boundaries: the fleet `sync run` (`syncs.yaml`) surface does not carry the flag yet (follows the `--skip-views` precedent — easy to add on demand), and a table added mid-stream via live add-table is not covered (only `migrate` and the sync cold-start create FKs). Pinned by a left-prefix-cover unit matrix (exact / PK-prefix / composite-order / too-narrow / partial / expression / MySQL prefix-length), a same-columns de-dup pin, a mutual-exclusion refusal pin, an over-long-name hash-fallback pin, and a cross-engine integration test (PG→MySQL: target has zero FKs, an already-indexed FK column is not duplicated, an un-indexed FK column gets a synthesized index, all rows copied).

## [0.99.197] - 2026-07-07

### Security

- **An invalid-URI DSN no longer leaks its password (or any userinfo token) into sluice's error output or logs (credential-in-logs exposure; LOW likelihood — only an INVALID-URI DSN triggers it — but a real credential disclosure; long-standing, present at the postgres DSN-parse site since v0.1.0, at pgtrigger since v0.85.0, and at fleettui since v0.99.137, all through v0.99.196).** Go's `net/url.Parse` embeds the ENTIRE raw input — including a DSN's `user:PASSWORD` userinfo — verbatim in its `*url.Error` string (`.URL` field), so a DSN that fails to parse as a URI (e.g. a password carrying an un-encoded control or special character) produced an error like `parse "postgres://user:PASSWORD@host…": net/url: invalid control character in URL`, password included. sluice wrapped that error with `%w` at several DSN-parse sites and let it propagate, so any surface that printed it — the `sync status --all` unreachable line, a `migrate`/`sync`/`verify` failure, a captured log — disclosed the credential. A valid DSN parses cleanly and was already redacted (userinfo + query string stripped, including the v0.99.194 `dsnEndpoint` work), so only a malformed DSN reaches this path — but a malformed DSN can carry a real secret, making it the same credential-in-logs class the v0.99.194 redaction targeted, reached through the parse-error door. Fix: a new `diagnose.SafeParseError` helper unwraps the `*url.Error` via `errors.As` (reaching it even after a caller re-wraps with `%w`) and returns its `.Err` field — the credential-free failure reason — dropping the `.URL` field that carries the DSN. Applied at every DSN-parse site that propagates the error: `internal/engines/postgres` (`parseURIDSN`, `withReplicationParam`, `withSchemaURI`), `internal/engines/pgtrigger` (`parseURIDSN`), and `internal/fleettui` (`NormalizeURL`, which also stopped echoing the raw connect address that can carry basic-auth userinfo). A whole-tree grep confirmed no other in-class sites (the `err == nil`-guarded parses never touch the error; blobcodec's query-string echoes carry a documented no-secret invariant; the Azure KMS key ID is a public resource locator). Valid-DSN handling is byte-identical — only the parse-ERROR branch is sanitized. Pinned at three layers: a `diagnose` unit test (ground-truths the raw error contains the secret, then asserts the sanitized one does not but keeps the reason), a postgres engine-site test at `parseURIDSN`, and a `fleettui` `NormalizeURL` test.

### Changed

- On a malformed URI DSN (or a malformed fleet-TUI connect address), the parse-failure error message now shows the underlying reason (e.g. "net/url: invalid control character in URL") without echoing the DSN/connect string. Valid DSNs and all data paths are unchanged.

## [0.99.196] - 2026-07-07

### Fixed

- **A PlanetScale/Vitess source no longer risks silently missing an entire sharded keyspace's shards when `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` under-reports (silent-partial-copy risk on a multi-keyspace source; present on the default discovery path since auto-discovery became the default in v0.99.191, and reachable via explicit `vstream_auto_discover_shards=true` back to when VStream multi-shard discovery landed in v0.1.0).** sluice's Vitess shard discovery relied solely on `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS`, and that query — live-confirmed on real PlanetScale — can PERSISTENTLY omit a fully-SERVING sharded keyspace's shards: a database with a default unsharded keyspace plus two 4-shard keyspaces had `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` drop one of them entirely for 6+ minutes (never converging) while `SHOW VITESS_TABLETS` and `SHOW KEYSPACES` listed all three complete throughout. Per keyspace the omission is all-or-nothing (0 or all N shards), but at the database level the result is incomplete-not-empty — the connected keyspace can be present-and-complete while a sibling keyspace is silently dropped from the inventory. The v0.99.194 retry-on-empty only re-runs discovery when the *connected/target* keyspace comes back empty, so a present target with a dropped sibling never triggered it, and a cold-copy / CDC / backup that drew its shard set from that incomplete inventory would have silently copied zero of the omitted keyspace's shards — exit 0, missing data. Fix: `discoverAllShards` now runs BOTH `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` and `SHOW VITESS_TABLETS` and returns the per-keyspace UNION of serving shards (distinct `(keyspace, shard)` where `State=SERVING`, accepting any serving tablet type so a momentary reparent gap doesn't drop a shard), never dropping a shard either source reports, and WARNs loudly naming any keyspace where the two disagree (a shard recovered-via-tablets, or a `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` shard with no serving tablet — kept in the set, flagged possibly-unstreamable). The cross-check is additive, not a new hard dependency: if `SHOW VITESS_TABLETS` is unavailable (older vtgate, insufficient permissions) discovery falls back to the `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS`-only result with a WARN, byte-identical to before. Both consumers inherit it: shard enumeration (cold-copy + CDC, via `discoverShards` / `resolveVStreamShards`) and the `--control-keyspace` sharded-target auto-detect (`discoverUnshardedKeyspaces`). It went uncaught because it requires a source with multiple keyspaces where a *secondary* sharded keyspace is the omitted one; a single-keyspace sharded source — the common case, and what every prior sharded test used — is unaffected (a directly-targeted omitted keyspace still fails loud with "returned no shards", never silently). Column parsing is by name (reorder-safe; a missing `Keyspace`/`Shard`/`State` column is a loud error, not a silent mis-parse). Pinned by pure reconcile/parser unit tests seeded with the exact observed omission plus a vttestserver integration test proving the union equals the `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` result on a healthy cluster with no spurious WARN.

## [0.99.195] - 2026-07-07

### Fixed

- **A `backup full` of a PlanetScale/Vitess (VStream) source now anchors the chain at the real COPY-completion position instead of an empty one, so a subsequent `backup incremental` chains off the full's exact position rather than starting from CDC's "current" (bounded, WARN-loud change-loss window; regression present in v0.99.3 through v0.99.194 on VStream backup chain roots, sharded or not).** The VStream backup snapshot's `Position` is captured at open time, where it is the zero value: ADR-0071 (first shipped v0.99.3) made the COPY pump a concurrent goroutine that finalizes the terminal VGTID only after the backup row sweep drains every table, so reading `Position` at snapshot construction recorded an EMPTY `EndPosition`. A `backup incremental` then could not chain off that empty anchor and fell back to CDC's current position — so any source write between the full backup finishing and the incremental opening its stream fell into an uncaptured gap. This is the identical race `#243` fixed on the cold-start→CDC handoff (at v0.99.68), overlooked on the backup path, which carried it continuously. Fix: `irbackup.Snapshot` gains an optional `FinalizePositionFn`; the VStream snapshot sets it to join the copy-completion barrier (`WaitCopyComplete`) after the row sweep drains and read the finalized VGTID, and `recordEndPosition` records that as `EndPosition` instead of the zero-at-open value. General to all VStream backup chains, not sharded-specific. Postgres and vanilla MySQL leave the finalizer nil and are byte-identical — their open-time position (Postgres exported-snapshot LSN, in-transaction GTID) is authoritative and recorded unchanged. Hidden because the only prior `recordEndPosition` coverage mocked a non-empty position; pinned now by `recordEndPosition` unit tests plus vstream/vttestserver integration on both an unsharded (`finishCopy`) and a sharded (`finishCopyAutoShard` stitched-min) chain root, each proving open-time-empty vs finalized-non-empty and a WARN-free incremental resume.

### Added

- **`restore --control-keyspace` routes a chain restore's CDC control tables to an unsharded sidecar keyspace, unblocking a chain restore into a *sharded* PlanetScale/Vitess target (new capability; a sharded-target chain restore previously aborted mid-replay with `Error 1105 … does not have a primary vindex`; loud failure, no data risk).** A chain restore (a full plus one or more incrementals) replays CDC change chunks, and its incremental leg calls `ChainRestore`'s `EnsureControlTable`, which creates the three internal control tables (`sluice_cdc_state`, `sluice_cdc_schema_history`, `sluice_shard_consolidation_lease`) in the target's data keyspace; a sharded Vitess keyspace rejects those vindex-less tables, and `restore` had no way to relocate them. The new `--control-keyspace <name>` mirrors `sync start --control-keyspace` (v0.99.193): it routes the control tables to a separate unsharded keyspace — auto-detected when the target is sharded and the flag is unset (a loud refusal that names the candidates if zero or several unsharded keyspaces exist), or explicit to override the auto-pick. It reuses the existing `applyControlKeyspace` / `ResolveControlKeyspace` / `WithControlKeyspace` stack as a fifth caller alongside the `sync start` / `sync stop` / `sync status` / `sync health` family, so the control-keyspace-configured target engine is the exact one that reaches `ChainRestore`'s `OpenChangeApplier`. A single FULL restore writes no control tables, so the flag is inert there; an unsharded target, a non-Vitess engine (Postgres, SQLite/D1), or an unset flag is byte-identical to before. This completes the v0.99.193 sharded-target story for the backup/restore path — `sync` got the sidecar treatment then, `restore` gets it now. Pinned by unit coverage over the resolve-and-apply path (auto-detect, explicit override, inert on a single-full / non-Vitess target).

## [0.99.194] - 2026-07-06

### Added

- **`--control-keyspace` is now a per-entry `control-keyspace` field in the `syncs.yaml` fleet config, closing the v0.99.193 known limitation.** v0.99.193 added `--control-keyspace` and its sharded-target auto-detect to the single-target `sync` commands but not the fleet paths; it is now a per-entry field threaded through `sync run` and `sync status --all` via the same `applyControlKeyspace` chain, with identical semantics — auto-detect the unsharded sidecar keyspace when unset and the target is sharded, override when set. The `status --all` de-dup key includes the control keyspace so distinct sidecars don't collapse into one bucket. A fleet with a sharded PlanetScale/Vitess target can now be driven end-to-end from `syncs.yaml`.
- **Per-entry `allow-cross-shard-merge` and `inject-shard-column` fields in the fleet config, so a sharded→sharded fleet move can opt into the cross-shard collision guard instead of refusal-looping (this path never worked in the fleet before — the flags had no fleet equivalent since the sync command center landed in v0.99.132).** They mirror `sync start`'s `--allow-cross-shard-merge` / `--inject-shard-column` (ADR-0048 / Bug 152) and are threaded onto the streamer exactly as the single-command flags are (reusing the shared `parseInjectShardColumn`): `inject-shard-column` is the structural NAME=VALUE discriminator (per-shard column + composite PK so cross-shard rows land disjoint), `allow-cross-shard-merge` is the globally-unique-key override. A load-time `validateCrossShard` guard refuses a per-entry entry that sets *both* — they are mutually exclusive in effect (the discriminator makes the override inert), so the curated config surface refuses the contradiction loudly, naming the stream-id, rather than let an operator believe an inert override took effect — and validates the `inject-shard-column` NAME=VALUE shape at config-load rather than at first cold-start.
- **The fleet loader now rejects an unknown or typo'd `syncs.yaml` key with a loud load failure (`ErrorUnused`) instead of silently dropping it.** A misspelled `allow-cross-shard-merge`, or a `sync start` flag that isn't part of the curated fleet subset, now fails the config load loudly instead of being ignored with no effect. Every key documented in the `syncs.yaml` samples (ADR-0122 / ADR-0126, the docs, and the `fleet-operator` skill) was verified to be a real `SyncSpec` / `RestartConfig` field first, so this only rejects genuinely-unsupported keys. This is the one behavior change for existing configs — a `syncs.yaml` that carried a previously-silently-ignored unknown key now fails to load.

### Fixed

- **A freshly-created sharded PlanetScale/Vitess keyspace that transiently reports 0 shards from `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` no longer aborts the sync (loud failure, no data risk; the default-path transient is present in v0.99.191–v0.99.193, and reachable via explicit `vstream_auto_discover_shards=true` back to when multi-shard auto-discovery landed in v0.1.0).** A just-provisioned keyspace can return an empty shard set for the target keyspace before the vtgate's serving graph settles — the query succeeds, the keyspace just isn't in the result yet — and sluice treated that first empty read as fatal (`shard auto-discovery for keyspace … returned no shards`), forcing operator relaunches. Discovery now retries on an empty result *for the target keyspace only* through a shared `discoverAllShardsForKeyspace` wrapper: bounded (4 attempts) with a short capped exponential backoff (500ms base, 2s cap), ctx-aware so a cancel aborts promptly. The non-empty first read returns immediately (fast path, no added latency), and a genuinely-absent keyspace stays empty past the budget so the existing loud refusal fires unchanged — the retry only buys the transient case a few chances, never masking a real absence. Both consumers ride it: the reader's `resolveVStreamShards` enumeration and the v0.99.193 `--control-keyspace` auto-detect. Pinned by a `retryShardDiscoveryOnEmpty` unit matrix plus real-2-shard-PlanetScale integration coverage.
- **A healthy-but-idle PlanetScale source no longer gives up after ~6 minutes (loud failure, no data effect — an idle source warm-resumes cleanly either way; present since the two-phase liveness watchdog landed in v0.99.10, on idle/low-traffic sources over an endpoint that sends no idle heartbeats, i.e. the public PlanetScale vstream endpoint).** An idle source hits the ~45s Phase-2 progress timeout each reconnect cycle, and the streamer's bounded-retry give-up counter (`--apply-retry-attempts`, default 8; ADR-0038) resets *only* when the persisted CDC position advances — which an idle source, processing zero events, never does — so it climbed to the cap and the sync gave up after roughly six minutes though nothing was wrong. A new optional engine surface `ir.LivenessProgressTimeoutError` (engine-neutral, checked via `errors.As`, mirroring `TransactionKilledError`) marks *only* the established-then-idle Phase-2 timeout (`vstreamProgressTimeoutError`), never the Phase-1 never-established liveness timeout or any open/connection error. `runWithRetry` neither increments nor resets the counter for a marked idle timeout, so a truly idle source survives indefinitely while a stream that can *never* establish still accumulates toward the budget and fails loudly; a real failure interspersed with benign idle timeouts keeps its accumulated count. The same exemption is applied to the backup rollover loop. Pinned by streamer-retry unit tests plus the mysql-side classifier test that only the Phase-2 constructor sets the marker.
- **`sync run --dry-run`, the shared-target-budget WARN, and `sync status --all`'s unreachable-target line no longer print DSN passwords (credential-in-logs leak, no data effect; present for the go-sql-driver DSN form since the fleet sync command center landed in v0.99.132).** `printFleetPlan` now routes source/target DSNs through `diagnose.RedactDSN`, and `dsnEndpoint` — the coarse host:port extractor feeding the shared-target WARN and the `status --all` unreachable line — now parses the go-sql-driver form (`user:pass@tcp(host)/db`, the common MySQL/PlanetScale shape) down to a bare `host:port` (strip userinfo, pull the addr out of `net(addr)`, drop the db) instead of falling through to `return dsn` and printing the raw connection string with its password. A genuinely-unparseable DSN is now redacted (`<redacted-dsn>`) rather than returned raw. Pinned by `TestDSNEndpoint` over the exact PlanetScale go-sql-driver form.

## [0.99.193] - 2026-07-06

### Added

- **`--control-keyspace` routes sluice's three CDC control tables to an unsharded sidecar keyspace, so `sync` now works against a *sharded* PlanetScale/Vitess target (new capability; unblocks a sharded→sharded region move that every prior release rejected before any rows copied; loud failure, no data risk).** Vitess requires a primary vindex on every table in a sharded keyspace, but sluice's internal control tables (`sluice_cdc_state`, `sluice_cdc_schema_history`, `sluice_shard_consolidation_lease`) have none, so a sync against a sharded *target* keyspace previously aborted at `CREATE TABLE` with `VT09001: … does not have a primary vindex` before a single row copied. A new `--control-keyspace <name>` places those three tables in a separate *unsharded* keyspace instead: control-table statements are keyspace-qualified (backtick-quoted `` `ks`.`sluice_cdc_state` ``, so PlanetScale's hyphenated default-keyspace names are accepted) and the `information_schema` lookups are scoped to that keyspace, while the data still flows into the sharded keyspace. The flag is threaded through `sync start` / `sync stop` / `sync status` / `sync health` and `schema add-table`. **For a sharded target with the flag unset, sluice auto-detects the default unsharded sidecar keyspace** — it enumerates the vtgate's keyspaces (one `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS`, classifying a keyspace as unsharded when it reports the single `-` shard) and, when the data keyspace is sharded, selects the sole unsharded keyspace that isn't the data keyspace: exactly one candidate is used automatically, and zero or several is a loud refusal that names the candidates and asks for `--control-keyspace`. A shard-discovery failure falls back to the unchanged bare-name behavior (a genuinely sharded target then still fails loudly at `CREATE TABLE`, never silently). One honest semantics note: on a sharded target the CDC position write still rides the same transaction as the data write, but that transaction is now a cross-keyspace, best-effort (non-2PC) commit — acceptable because a sharded apply is already cross-shard/non-atomic and keyed idempotent apply makes a torn resume a safe no-op (live-proven: a hard crash mid-CDC warm-resumed from the sidecar-persisted position with zero duplicates, zero gaps, source md5 == target md5). **Unsharded MySQL/PlanetScale/Vitess targets and every non-Vitess engine (Postgres, SQLite/D1) are byte-identical to before** — control tables stay in the data keyspace on a single-keyspace transaction; the feature engages only for a sharded MySQL/PlanetScale/Vitess target, with no data-path or format change. Deferred: the fleet paths (`sync run`, `sync status --all`) do not yet carry `--control-keyspace` (needs a per-entry `syncs.yaml` config field); single-target `sync` commands are covered. Pinned by the pure `selectControlKeyspace` decision matrix and the unset-path byte-identity unit test, validated live on a real 2-shard PlanetScale target (auto-detect found the hyphenated default keyspace with no manual setup; clean `status`/`stop`; warm crash-resume md5-identical), with the `-race` Integration matrix (concurrent-apply path) green on the tagged commit before tag.

## [0.99.192] - 2026-07-06

### Fixed

- **Pointing the vanilla `mysql` driver at a PlanetScale endpoint now refuses loudly up front (exit 3, `SLUICE-E-DRIVER-HOST-MISMATCH`) instead of failing obscurely partway through a run (UX/foot-gun fix; no data effect; the obscure mid-run failure was present in every prior release).** A `*.connect.psdb.cloud` / `*.private-connect.psdb.cloud` host needs the `planetscale` driver: the vanilla `mysql` flavor uses binlog CDC and `LOAD DATA` cold-copy, both blocked by Vitess/PlanetScale, so `--source-driver mysql` / `--target-driver mysql` against a PlanetScale host previously connected and then failed deep in the run with a confusing low-level error. A new preflight — the optional `ir.DSNValidator` engine surface (modeled on `ir.ShardDiscoverer`), implemented by the mysql engine and consulted for both the source and the target at the very top of migrate and sync — now inspects the DSN host alone, before any reader/writer opens, and the vanilla flavor refuses a PlanetScale host with a role-prefixed coded error naming the host, explaining that binlog CDC and `LOAD DATA` are Vitess-blocked, and hinting `pass --source-driver planetscale` / `pass --target-driver planetscale` (source checked before target). No effect on non-PlanetScale hosts or on the `planetscale`/`vitess` flavors (the correct drivers for that host), and no data-path or format change. Pinned by unit matrices over the mysql `ValidateDSN` (both PSDB suffixes × all three flavors, plus no false positives on normal/garbage/socket/empty DSNs) and over the pipeline preflight (coded refusal, role prefix, source-before-target, no-op without the surface).

## [0.99.191] - 2026-07-06

### Fixed

- **A genuinely *sharded* PlanetScale/Vitess source cold-copy now discovers the real shard layout and copies every row from every shard, instead of failing closed with zero rows (loud failure — no silent loss; pre-existing since VStream multi-shard support landed in v0.1.0).** On a sharded source keyspace the VStream snapshot cold-copy (`sync start`'s snapshot→CDC handoff) built its VGTID from whatever `resolveVStreamShards` returned, and with no explicit `vstream_shards` and auto-discovery off that list silently defaulted to `["-"]` — the *unsharded* convention — so the copy asked vtgate for a keyspace-wide `{Shard:"-"}` VGTID that a sharded keyspace has no such shard for. vtgate rejected it (`FailedPrecondition "… shard provided in VGTID, -, not found …"`) and the cold-copy delivered *zero* rows. The cross-shard-collision preflight discovers the real shards independently (via the engine's `DiscoverShards`), so it correctly saw `[-80,80-]` while the copy assumed `["-"]` — guard and copy disagreed — which is why even passing `--allow-cross-shard-merge` (which clears the collision guard) didn't help: the copy still built the wrong keyspace-wide VGTID and copied nothing.
