Fixed by making shard auto-discovery the **default**: whenever `vstream_shards` isn't pinned, sluice reads the real layout via a plain, parameterless `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` — a single `-` for an unsharded keyspace (byte-identical to the old `["-"]` default) and every shard for a sharded one — and discovery returning zero shards is now a loud error, never a silent `["-"]` fallback. Discovery is deferred to stream-open (moved out of reader construction) so building a reader stays connection-free; an explicit `vstream_shards=…` still pins/overrides and skips the query, and `vstream_auto_discover_shards=true` is retained for back-compat (it now names the default). Complements v0.99.190, which fixed the shard-discovery query itself (the `LIKE ?` bind parameter vtgate rejected) for unsharded databases. Pinned by the re-worked `resolveVStreamShards` unit matrix (the no-explicit default now routes to discovery — proven by the discovery-wrapped error, impossible if it still short-circuited to `["-"]`) and, authoritatively, by the new build-tagged integration test `TestVitessReshard_ShardedColdCopy_AllShardsCopied`, which reshards a keyspace 1→2 and cold-copies all 200 rows from both shards of a real 2-shard `vitess/lite` cluster with no `vstream_shards` set (FailedPrecondition gone). The `-race` Integration matrix — including the vstream and engines-mysql suites — was green on the tagged commit before tag.

## [0.99.190] - 2026-07-06

### Fixed

- **PS→PS `migrate` / `sync start` on an *unsharded* PlanetScale database no longer refuses with a spurious shard-discovery failure (significant; loud refusal, no data risk; pre-existing since VStream multi-shard auto-discovery landed in v0.1.0).** Shard auto-discovery ran `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS LIKE ?`, but the bind parameter reached vtgate as a `:v1` placeholder it cannot parse (`syntax error … near ':v1'`), so `discoverShards` errored — and the cross-shard-collision preflight treats an un-determinable shard layout as "refuse rather than risk a silent cross-shard overwrite." The result: on an ordinary *unsharded* PlanetScale database, where there is no sharding and nothing to merge, sluice refused the very first command of any migrate or sync unless the operator passed `--allow-cross-shard-merge` (which short-circuits the preflight before discovery runs), turning that flag into a de-facto required workaround for a query bug on exactly the databases where its real purpose — opting into a genuine cross-shard merge — does not apply. Fixed by running a plain, parameterless `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` and filtering to the target keyspace client-side: there is no bind variable for vtgate to choke on, and an unsharded keyspace returns a single `<keyspace>/-` row, so the preflight sees one shard and proceeds. `--allow-cross-shard-merge` is no longer needed on unsharded databases and now applies only to genuinely sharded sources (its intended purpose); the client-side keyspace filter is also now the primary filter — a self-hosted vtgate can serve several keyspaces — not a defensive backstop. Pinned by the reader-error classification unit tests, with the `-race` Integration gate (including the vstream suite) green on the main commit before tag.
- **`sync stop --wait` no longer reports a false drain timeout when tearing down a VStream source (minor; no data effect; present on VStream sources since the `sync stop --wait` graceful-drain feature, v0.9.0).** Stopping a VStream stream on `sync stop` (or Ctrl-C / outer-context cancel) surfaces from the gRPC `Recv` as a gRPC Canceled status (or DeadlineExceeded), which `errors.Is(context.Canceled)` did not match — so the engine-neutral streamer treated the clean teardown as a terminal error, skipped clearing `stop_requested_at`, and `sync stop --wait` reported `did not complete drain within …` even though the stream had already stopped. `classifyReaderError` now normalizes a gRPC Canceled/DeadlineExceeded status to the corresponding standard `context` sentinel (the raw gRPC status stays on the chain via `%w` for diagnostics), so the streamer recognizes the clean stop and completes the drain handshake. No data effect — the stream always stopped; only the `--wait` completion signal was wrong. Pinned by a unit matrix asserting both Canceled and DeadlineExceeded normalize to their `context` sentinels, are not classified retriable (a cancel is intentional, not transient), and keep the underlying gRPC status reachable.

## [0.99.189] - 2026-07-06

### Fixed

- **`sluice slot drop` without `--yes` now refuses loudly (exit 3, `SLUICE-E-CONFIRMATION-REQUIRED`) instead of prompting — which on any non-TTY silently aborted a destructive command (agent-contract fidelity; low severity, no data loss; surfaced by the Tier-1 agent-skills live validation).** AGENTS.md guarantees every sluice command is non-interactive ("no prompts, ever"), but `slot drop` without `--yes` printed an interactive `Drop replication slot "…"? [y/N]` confirmation via `confirmDestructive`. On a non-TTY — any agent, CI job, or script — the read hit EOF, was read as "no", printed `aborted`, and returned exit 0: a destructive command silently no-op'ing, exactly the failure the "refuse loudly" tenet exists to prevent (the caller believed the slot was dropped when it was not). `slot drop` without `--yes` now returns a `ClassRefusal` coded error — the new `SLUICE-E-CONFIRMATION-REQUIRED` code (exit 3) — naming the slot with the hint `pass --yes (or -y) to confirm`, and the refusal fires fail-fast *before* any source connection is opened. `--yes`/`-y` clears the gate and proceeds exactly as before; `--if-exists` and `--force` are unchanged, and `confirmDestructive` is retained (the `trigger` teardown path still uses it). Pinned by two unit tests: without `--yes`, `Run` returns the exit-3 coded refusal naming the slot without reading stdin (no database needed); with `--yes` it clears the gate and reaches the engine-resolution path.
- **`backup full`'s `next_steps` hint now points at `--from-dir`, the flag `backup verify` actually accepts.** The success envelope suggested `sluice backup verify --output-dir <BACKUP_DIR>`, but `backup verify`'s flag is `--from-dir` (`--output-dir` is a usage error there), so the binary's own suggested next command failed for anyone who copy-pasted it. The hint now emits `--from-dir`; the identical wrong string was corrected in the envelope test that pinned it.

### Changed

- **Three Tier-1 agent skills hardened from the same live-validation pass (docs/skills only; no shipped-binary change).** `fidelity-verify` now states that cross-engine `verify` is count-only and a clean count is not a value-level fidelity guarantee (closing a false-FAITHFUL reading); `sluice-error-triage` now handles the generic-`code`-with-the-real-cause-in-`message` shape, adds a wrong-hint warning and a codeless-failure branch, and lists the full connect-error codes; `backup-chain-operator` corrects `incremental` (no `--format`), `prune` (no `--encrypt`), and the `slot drop` invocation (positional name, `--yes` for non-interactive confirm rather than an interactive prompt). Agent guidance only — the sluice binary behaves identically.

## [0.99.188] - 2026-07-06

### Fixed

- **MySQL ENUM/SET labels are now decoded with MySQL's full string-escape set, so a label containing a control byte lands byte-exact instead of as a wrong allowed-value set (medium-low; silent schema corruption; pre-existing since v0.1.0; the sibling sweep after the v0.99.186/187 BINARY-`DEFAULT` thread).** `parseEnumOrSet` reads the allowed-value list out of `information_schema.columns.COLUMN_TYPE` but decoded only two escapes — a doubled quote (`''`→`'`) and a doubled backslash (`\\`→`\`) — while `COLUMN_TYPE` re-escapes control bytes inside a label with MySQL's *full* single-backslash escape set (`\0 \b \t \n \r \\` plus the doubled quote). The two-escape shortcut passed every other escape through literally, so an enum label whose real value contains a control byte — e.g. `ENUM('nul_x\0y')`, label `nul_x` + NUL + `y` — was carried into the IR as the literal 8-character string `nul_x\0y` (backslash + `0`), a *wrong* allowed-value set on the target enum/domain (silent schema corruption, exit 0; rare because control chars in enum labels are uncommon). Each label is now decoded with the same MySQL string decoder v0.99.187's binary-default recovery uses (`decodeMySQLQuotedString`), refactored onto a shared escape-aware scanner (`scanMySQLQuotedString`) that also makes the comma/quote *split* escape-aware — a label containing an escaped comma, escaped quote, or `\0` can no longer mis-split the list. A well-behaved ASCII enum decodes byte-identically to before. Cross-engine, a genuinely NUL-bearing label round-trips byte-exact MySQL→MySQL and *loud-fails* at apply MySQL→Postgres (PG `text` cannot hold a NUL) — correct loud-failure, not silent loss. Pinned across the family (Bug-74 discipline): a unit matrix over every escape × ENUM and SET × comma-in-label × empty label × NUL-label-mid-list × unchanged plain-ASCII enum, plus MySQL→MySQL integration round-trips for escaped labels and a byte-exact NUL label.
- **MySQL table `COMMENT`s that contain a NUL byte are now recovered faithfully from `SHOW CREATE TABLE` instead of the NUL-truncated `information_schema` value (cosmetic; low; pre-existing since v0.1.0).** `information_schema.tables.TABLE_COMMENT` C-string-truncates a table comment at its first `0x00` byte, exactly as `COLUMN_DEFAULT` does and unlike `COLUMN_COMMENT` (which does not truncate, so column comments are left alone), so a comment carrying a NUL was read short (or empty for a leading-NUL comment). The reader now recovers a non-empty comment from the `COMMENT='…'` clause of `SHOW CREATE TABLE`, decoded with the same escape-aware decoder — the same silent-truncation class as the binary-`DEFAULT` fix, but cosmetic, hence low severity. The extra read is amortized onto v0.99.187's per-table `SHOW CREATE` recovery pass: a table needing *either* a binary-default recovery or a comment recovery is fetched exactly once, and the common case (no binary default, empty comment) pays no extra query; a `SHOW CREATE` that cannot be re-read keeps the possibly-truncated comment with a WARN. Pinned by a clause-parse unit matrix (NUL-bearing, doubled-quote, newline/tab escapes, no-comment, and the two false-match traps — a column-level `COMMENT '…'` must not be matched, and a table comment whose text contains `COMMENT=` still resolves the real option), the amortization-gate unit test, and a MySQL→MySQL integration round-trip over a NUL-bearing / plain / no-comment trio. The audit that produced both fixes also *cleared* the high-severity `information_schema` suspects — generated-column expressions (`GENERATION_EXPRESSION`), string/`VARCHAR` defaults, `CHECK` clauses, and view definitions are all faithful — confirming there is no "wrong computed values" class and that v0.99.187's binary-only scoping was correct.

### Internal

- **`release.yml` no longer auto-pushes the winget manifest (`skip_upload: "true"`).** winget is submitted to `microsoft/winget-pkgs` manually and selectively, and the goreleaser auto-push to the `sluicesync/winget-pkgs` fork intermittently flaked on the GitHub API (a 500, then a 403 "Timed out validating rule", e.g. during v0.99.187) — which would fail the whole release *after* the binaries, GHCR image, and homebrew/scoop taps had already published. The manifest is still generated (into `dist/winget/` for a manual fork PR when a version warrants one); it just never blocks a release. No shipped-binary or install change for brew/scoop/container users.

## [0.99.187] - 2026-07-06

### Fixed

- **MySQL `BINARY`/`VARBINARY` column DEFAULTs that contain a NUL byte are now recovered byte-exact from `SHOW CREATE TABLE` (Finding C; completes the v0.99.186 fix; pre-existing since v0.1.0; silent data corruption on Postgres and SQLite/D1, loud only on the same-engine MySQL CREATE-reject subset).** `information_schema.COLUMN_DEFAULT` is a text column and MySQL C-string-truncates a binary column's literal default at its first `0x00` byte before storing it there, so the metadata read is lossy for *any* binary default containing a NUL: `BINARY(1) DEFAULT 0x00` and `BINARY(4) DEFAULT 0x00FF00FF` both come back as the bare `0x` (empty), while `BINARY(2) DEFAULT 0x2700` comes back as the well-formed but short `0x27`. v0.99.186 taught the reader to translate binary hex-literal defaults byte-exact but still sourced the bytes from `information_schema`, so it carried those truncated values as the emitted default: the bare-`0x` cases fell through as a wrong `'0x'` literal (Postgres stored `\x3078`, the two ASCII bytes of "0x"), and the truncated-but-well-formed cases were the more dangerous ones — `hexLiteralDefault` *accepted* `0x27` and every emitter rendered a value silently short of the real default. Nothing in `COLUMN_DEFAULT` alone distinguishes a truncation from a genuine short value, so the class cannot be detected from the metadata. The reader now flags every binary-family column whose default `information_schema` renders as a hex literal and, after the column scan, re-reads its authoritative default from `SHOW CREATE TABLE` (once per affected table, cached), which reports the byte-exact stored default in one of the two forms MySQL emits there — a `0x<hex>` literal when any byte is ≥ 0x80, else a single-quoted escaped string (escape set ground-truthed on MySQL 8.0: `\0 \b \t \n \r \\` plus a doubled `''`, every other byte < 0x80 raw) — both decoded to exact bytes and re-encoded to the same `hexLiteralDialect` `DefaultExpression` v0.99.186's emitters already consume (MySQL bare `0x…`, Postgres `'\x…'::bytea`, SQLite/D1 `X'…'`, with the existing fixed-width `BINARY(N)` NUL-padding, idempotent on the already-padded `SHOW CREATE` value). A default `SHOW CREATE TABLE` is unavailable for, or whose clause is neither form, is DROPPED with a loud WARN naming the column (a DEFAULT-less target column is safe; a wrong-bytes default is not) — never silently carried. A trailing-NUL default on a *fixed* `BINARY(N)` was accidentally saved before by width-padding; the genuinely-corrupted shapes are leading-NUL, `VARBINARY`-with-NUL, and mid-NUL-with-trailing-data. Pinned across the family (Bug-74 discipline): reader unit pins over the hex and escaped-string decoders (every emitted escape form incl. tab and literal backslash) and the per-column `SHOW CREATE` extraction (prefix-collision + `COMMENT`-safety), plus MySQL→MySQL / MySQL→Postgres / MySQL→SQLite integration round-trips over leading/mid/trailing-NUL, mid-NUL-with-data, `VARBINARY`-mid-NUL, NUL-padded-narrower, and the v0.99.186 well-formed no-regression case — each verified red without the fix (reproducing the `\x3078` corruption) and green with it.
- **A pathological column literally named `` `x DEFAULT 0xAA` `` can no longer mislocate the DEFAULT keyword inside its own name (value-fidelity review follow-up).** The `SHOW CREATE` per-column extractor now searches for ` DEFAULT ` only *past* the matched backtick-quoted column name, so a column name that itself contains ` DEFAULT ` can't cause bytes to be parsed out of the name instead of the real default clause. Pinned end-to-end together with the tab/backslash escape-decode path.

### Internal

- **Dump-parity oracle: index body-equivalence pairing pass + MediaWiki corpus coverage (Finding B).** The restore-parity comparator keys statements by object identity (verb + kind + name), but sluice deliberately qualifies Postgres index names (`pgIndexName`, GitHub #26 — PG index names are schema-global where MySQL's are table-scoped), so over corpus schemas with short table-scoped index names every secondary index read as a false only-in-sluice / only-in-oracle divergence (why the corpus oracle had been pinned to Chinook). A second pass now pairs the leftover unpaired `CREATE INDEX` statements by a name-independent body signature (uniqueness + everything from `ON` onward), pairing only when a signature is unique on both sides so a genuinely dropped/added/body-changed index still surfaces; enabling the MediaWiki PG→PG corpus surfaced two other documented non-loss classes (serial→identity, typed-literal integer defaults) that are allowlisted/canonicalized with tight class globs citing `docs/type-mapping.md`. Test/CI-only.
- **`migrate-preflight` agent skill hardened after live validation (docs/skills only).** Adds the required `--target-driver` (previously an exit-80 blocker), corrects the dry-run JSON vs schema-preview risk surface, and adds a "what preflight cannot see" value-hazard box (binary-default check + exit-code-vs-message note). No shipped-binary behavior change.

## [0.99.186] - 2026-07-05

### Fixed

- **MySQL BINARY/VARBINARY column DEFAULTs now round-trip correctly to MySQL, Postgres, and SQLite/D1 (pre-existing since v0.1.0; loud failure on MySQL, silent data corruption on Postgres and SQLite/D1).** MySQL 8 reports a binary column's literal default in `information_schema.COLUMN_DEFAULT` as a bare hex literal — `BINARY(14) NOT NULL DEFAULT '19700101000000'` comes back as `0x3139373030313031303030303030` (with `EXTRA` empty, i.e. a literal, not a generated expression). The MySQL reader carried that verbatim as a plain string default, so the writer re-quoted it as the *string* `'0x3139…'` — a 30-char value on a 14-byte column. The failure mode differed by target, and the two cross-engine directions were **silent**: same-engine MySQL→MySQL rejected the re-emitted `CREATE TABLE` loudly with `Error 1067 (Invalid default value)`, but **MySQL→Postgres and MySQL→SQLite/D1 exited 0 and stored a corrupted default** — the ASCII text of the `0x…` literal instead of the intended bytes (e.g. `BINARY(8) DEFAULT 'hi'` landed the six bytes of the string `0x6869` rather than `hi` + 6 NUL bytes). This was confirmed against the shipped v0.99.185 binary: MySQL→MySQL `Error 1067`, but MySQL→PG and MySQL→SQLite both silently mis-stored the default. Net effect: a table with a binary-column literal default (e.g. MediaWiki's `BINARY(14)` timestamps) could not be migrated at all to MySQL, and was **silently given wrong default values** on Postgres and SQLite/D1. The reader now recognizes a well-formed `0x<even-hex>` literal *only when the column is a binary-family type* (`BINARY`/`VARBINARY`) and tags it as a hex-bytes default (`hexLiteralDialect`); the gate on column type is deliberate — a genuine `VARCHAR DEFAULT '0x1234'` reports the identical surface form and must stay a quoted string. Each writer renders the tagged default in its own dialect: MySQL bare `0x…`, Postgres `'\x…'::bytea`, SQLite/D1 `X'…'`. Fixed-width `BINARY(N)` fidelity is preserved across the width-agnostic BYTEA/BLOB targets: MySQL reports the un-padded literal but stores it right-padded to the column width and re-pads on INSERT, while BYTEA/BLOB cannot re-pad, so the Postgres/SQLite writers now zero-fill the payload to the declared fixed-BINARY width (`VARBINARY` is never padded) — without this the default-applied row would land 2 bytes on Postgres/SQLite vs 8 on MySQL, a silent divergence. `BLOB` is unaffected (MySQL forbids a `DEFAULT` on `BLOB`). Pinned across the family (Bug-74 discipline): reader unit pins over `BINARY`/`VARBINARY` at multiple widths, NUL-bearing and uppercase-hex, plus `VARCHAR '0x…'`-stays-a-string and truncated/odd-length fall-through; writer unit pins on each emitter's bare/`::bytea`/`X'…'` arm; and MySQL→MySQL, MySQL→Postgres, and MySQL→SQLite integration round-trips that create the table (the former 1067 site) and assert the default-applied bytes are byte-identical to the MySQL source across exact-width, VARBINARY, and NUL-padded shapes — each pin verified to fail at baseline and pass with the fix. Documented residue: a non-UTF8/leading-NUL default that MySQL itself mangles in `information_schema` (truncated `0x` with no digits) is unreadable from that path and falls through to the verbatim literal — a MySQL limitation, not sluice's.

### Internal

- **MySQL `mysqldump` restore-parity oracle (roadmap item 51 Phase 2) + real-world corpus suite (item 52).** The oracle round-trips a migrated MySQL target and diffs it against `mysqldump` output — it is what surfaced the BINARY/VARBINARY default defect above; the corpus suite adds dump-parity and sample-row verify legs plus a scheduled weekly corpus job. Test/CI-only, no shipped-binary behavior change.
- **Cookbook recipes are now executable tests pinned against the released binary (item 53).** The harness runs the documented recipes end-to-end and caught several stale-recipe doc bugs — the bidirectional-cutover zero-downtime model, the redaction-keyset nested-YAML shape, and a fabricated quickstart dry-run banner — all fixed here. The skills↔CLI doc-sync CI guard (item 56) was extended to catch subcommand drift, not just top-level flags.

## [0.99.185] - 2026-07-05

### Fixed

- **`--encrypt-mode` omission now actually inherits an existing chain's mode from the CLI (Bug 180; completes the v0.99.184 Bug 179 fix).** v0.99.184 added an "inherit the chain's mode when `--encrypt-mode` is omitted" branch, but it was unreachable from the CLI: the flag carried a kong `default:"per-chain"` and `buildBackupEncryption` collapsed `""`→per-chain, so an omitted flag arrived as an explicit `per-chain`. Extending a `per-chunk` encrypted chain while omitting the flag was therefore *refused* (`--encrypt-mode=per-chain conflicts with the chain's mode per-chunk`), and the tool's own hint ("omit `--encrypt-mode` to inherit it") could not be satisfied — the operator had to pass `--encrypt-mode=per-chunk` explicitly. Loud and zero-loss (the un-restorable *class* stayed closed — a mode mismatch is refused at build, never silently un-restorable), but it defeated the release's headline behavior and printed remediation. Fixed at all three points where the mode was prematurely defaulted: the kong flag drops its per-chain default and admits the empty value so omission resolves to `""` (distinguishable from an explicit `per-chain`); `buildBackupEncryption` forwards `""` instead of collapsing it, leaving the inherit/default decision to the orchestrator; and `setupChainEncryption` writes the resolved mode back so the chunk-writer agrees with the manifest on a full-*resume* inherit. A fresh full with an omitted flag still defaults to `per-chain` (unchanged), and an explicit conflicting mode is still refused loudly. Pinned *through* the kong parser (not by setting the field directly — the Bug-74 lesson: the earlier programmatic pin greened a code path no CLI user could reach): an omitted `--encrypt-mode` parses to `""`, explicit values are preserved, an invalid value is rejected by the enum, and `buildBackupEncryption` forwards `""`.

## [0.99.184] - 2026-07-05

### Fixed

- **Encrypted-chain segments now inherit the chain's encryption mode; a conflicting `--encrypt-mode` is refused at build (Bug 179; un-restorable-backup class, loud, pre-existing since the chain-encryption feature).** An encrypted backup chain must use one encryption mode (`per-chunk` vs `per-chain`) for every segment, but the chain-extending orchestrators resolved it inconsistently: `alignEncryption` inherited the parent chain's mode for the chain-CEK derivation, while the sibling `resolveChunkCEK` read *this* invocation's `--encrypt-mode` (defaulting `per-chain`) for writing chunks. A `per-chunk` chain extended by a mode-omitted `backup incremental` — or a `backup stream` rollover — therefore built and `verify`d cleanly but was **un-restorable**: restore resolves a single chain mode/CEK from the root full, meets the segment's per-chain-written chunks, and aborts loudly (`chain CEK is unset`, exit 1, only the full's rows land). There is no silent loss (it fails loudly at restore) and it required an operator to omit or mismatch `--encrypt-mode` mid-chain, but a chain that verifies yet cannot restore is a trust defect. `alignEncryption` is now the single authority for a segment's mode: it inherits the chain's mode when `--encrypt-mode` is omitted (so chunk-writing agrees with the chain) and refuses loudly at build time when an explicit `--encrypt-mode` conflicts with the established chain — turning an un-restorable chain into an impossible-by-inheritance or refused-at-the-earliest-point outcome. Fixed in both `IncrementalBackup` and `BackupStream`; pinned across the family (both orchestrators × parent per-chunk/per-chain × omitted-inherit / explicit-match / explicit-conflict-refuse) including a resolve-chunk-CEK-after-inherit assertion that directly closes the mode-source split. The same guard was extended to the **full-backup resume** path (`setupChainEncryption`): resuming a crashed in-progress full with a changed `--encrypt-mode` is now inherited-if-omitted / refused-if-conflicting, closing the identical un-restorable shape at the chain root. A chain built with a consistent `--encrypt-mode` on every segment was always restorable and is byte-identical. **One intentional tightening:** a per-chain chain root extended by an explicit `--encrypt-mode=per-chunk` segment happened to be restorable before (restore keys off per-chunk `WrappedCEK` presence); it is now refused at build, enforcing the one-mode-per-chain invariant rather than relying on that fragile coincidence.

## [0.99.183] - 2026-07-05

### Internal

- **Backup/restore/chain carved out of the flat `internal/pipeline` root into two new sub-packages (audit 3.7b, completing the A-1 pipeline decomposition).** Building on v0.99.182's `migcore` core, the ~9.8k-LOC backup/restore/chain-lineage cluster is decomposed out of the flat root in three cycle-safe steps: (1) the shared substrate both the cluster and root's streaming/broker orchestrators used bidirectionally — manifest I/O, the lineage catalog, and the backup-encryption envelope — moved DOWN into a new `internal/pipeline/lineage` package (that mutual reach — the cluster calling root's `readManifestAt` while root called the cluster's `loadLineageCatalog`/`BackupEncryption` — was the import cycle blocking the split; pulling the substrate below both sides breaks it); (2) the remaining shared helpers the cluster borrowed from root (the table filter, target-schema configurators, DDL-phase/views runners, row-redaction + PK helpers, apply-concurrency resolvers, copy/reader helpers, the reparent observer, the reconcile bound, and the chain-resume preflight) moved into `migcore`; (3) the now import-clean cluster — the `Backup`/`Restore`/`ChainRestore` orchestrators with their table pools, chain compaction/pruning, and the dump-parity oracle — lifted into a new `internal/pipeline/backup` package, with `cmd/sluice` and root repointed onto it. Every new edge is one-directional and verified acyclic (`backup` → {`lineage`, `migcore`, `blobcodec`}, never root; neither `lineage` nor `migcore` imports root). The flat root shrank ~8.6k non-test LOC (~46.9k → 38.4k). Pure move: every backup/restore/chain-restore/broker round-trip is byte-identical, proven across the same- and cross-engine integration suites, with `git mv` history preserved where whole files moved.
- **First funlen/gocyclo lint-ceiling ratchet turn — 210→180 lines, 60→45 complexity (audit 3.5).** With the decomposition landed, the fourteen functions that then exceeded the tighter ceilings were split by extracting cohesive, named sub-operations (not arbitrary line chunks): `BackupStream.Run`'s 355-line rotation loop into a linear setup helper plus explicit empty-rollover/commit paths (retiring its standing `//nolint:funlen`), `captureWindow`'s in-flight chunk-writer state onto a small `changeChunkBuffer` value, the Postgres `translateType` type-family switch into per-family dispatchers, and the schema-reader column/index populators, the two `cmd` composition roots, and the remaining long backup/migrate/applier functions into named steps. The ceilings drop to 180/45 with a single re-justified pre-existing suppression (the flat ~60-field `Streamer{}` CLI composition literal — a one-to-one flag→field mapping indirection would only obscure). Behavior-preserving: the concurrency-adjacent splits (`BackupStream.Run`, `captureWindow`, `copyChunk`, `RunOneBatch`) keep exact goroutine/channel/defer/return semantics — resource-cleanup defers stay in the owning function — and the full unit + `-race` integration suites are green.

## [0.99.182] - 2026-07-05

### Internal

- **Shared trigger-CDC core extracted behind a dialect seam (audit A-2).** `pgtrigger` and `sqlite-trigger` (and, by transport substitution, `d1-trigger`) duplicated the trigger-CDC position codec and the change-log prune-batching engine. The genuinely-identical protocol — the `{"last_id":N}` position token codec and the keyset-batched prune loop with its bookkeeper/time-budget — now lives once in `internal/engines/internal/triggercdc`, parameterized by a small dialect contract: the accepted engine-name family is a dialect-provided list (so `pgtrigger` accepts `{postgres-trigger}`, `sqlite-trigger` accepts `{sqlite-trigger, d1-trigger}` per the Bug-166 fix, and a future trigger engine widens its own list with no codec change), and the per-transport prune batch size is a parameter. The **divergent** semantics were deliberately kept per-engine — pgtrigger's contiguous-committed-prefix + xmin safety-lag snapshot anchor versus sqlite's single-writer `MAX(id)` anchor — because merging them would be a silent-loss regression, not a dedup. Byte-identical behavior; every existing pgtrigger/sqlite-trigger/d1-trigger unit and integration pin passes unchanged, with the shared codec's family-acceptance now pinned as a class (1-, 2-, and synthetic 3-engine families × round-trip/cross-accept/foreign-refuse).
- **Shared migration-engine core extracted to `internal/pipeline/migcore` (audit A-1, first structural carve).** The flat `internal/pipeline` package had backup/restore/streamer layered on top of a shared migration engine that migrate also uses, with root reaching back into backup — a bidirectional coupling that blocked decomposing the package. The engine-neutral core now lives downstream in `migcore`, imported by pipeline-root and importing nothing from it (verified acyclic): the chunk-boundary planners (MIN/MAX-divide + sampled-keyset) and PK-orderability logic, the copy-parallelism gate + AIMD backoff + connection-budget resolver, the grow-gate coordinator, the schema-diff + cross-engine-supportable checks, the reparent tracker, and the `RunSummary`/`WrapWithHint`/phase-constant surfaces (~3k prod LOC out of root). The copy *orchestration* (welded to resume-state, migration-state, redaction, shard-stamping) deliberately stays in root — only the neutral core moved. This unblocks the backup/restore carve (a later chunk). Pure move: chunk/gate/budget/grow-gate/reparent behavior is byte-identical, proven by the same- and cross-engine parallel-copy, resume, and backup/restore integration round-trips across the new package boundary.

## [0.99.181] - 2026-07-05

### Fixed

- **MySQL LOAD DATA bulk copy hung indefinitely under `NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES` (Bug 178; liveness, zero silent-loss; latent since v0.10.0).** A MySQL-target migrate over the LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE fast path (`local_infile=ON`, the default) could hang forever — rows streamed, then no `phase complete`, no error, zero rows committed, process wedged — when `NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES` (NBE) was active on the target connection (via `--mysql-sql-mode` or a DSN `sql_mode` param). Root cause: `buildLoadDataStmt` spelled the field/escape/line framing as backslash-escaped SQL string literals (`'\t'`/`'\\'`/`'\n'`), which the server parses under the session sql_mode; under NBE `'\\'` is two backslash bytes, tripping MySQL's "ESCAPED BY must be a single character" check and aborting the statement with Error 1083 BEFORE it requests the infile stream — so the driver never invoked the registered reader, nobody drained the `io.Pipe`, and the encoder goroutine deadlocked on its buffered pipe write (a fast exec error became an indefinite hang). The framing is now emitted as `sql_mode`-invariant hex literals (`X'09'`/`X'5C'`/`X'0A'` — byte-identical output, NBE-invariant parse), and `writeLoadData` closes the pipe reader before awaiting the encoder so ANY pre-transfer statement error surfaces loudly instead of deadlocking (defence in depth). Batched-INSERT (`local_infile=OFF`) was always immune. Pinned by a unit assertion on the hex framing plus a real-MySQL integration test that drives the writer with NBE active across the framing torture class (literal backslash, backslash+field/line terminator, `\N`-looking and `\0`-looking values, real NUL, real NULL) under a hard deadline so a regression re-hangs as a FAIL, not a hung process.

### Internal

- **Wire-format + storage leaf extracted to `internal/pipeline/blobcodec` (audit A-1 down-payment).** The backup chunk codec, fast-JSON chunk writer, change-chunk reader/writer, blob store, compression codec, and local-FS store (~3.3k prod LOC + ~2.5k test LOC) moved out of the flat `internal/pipeline` package into a new leaf sub-package that imports only the IR backup contract — a cycle-free first slice of the pipeline decomposition (finding A-1), shrinking the root package and de-risking the larger backup/restore carve. Pure move: chunk/manifest/codec formats and the backup→restore round-trip are byte-identical (proven by the existing LocalStore + MinIO integration round-trips across the new package boundary); `git mv` history preserved.

## [0.99.180] - 2026-07-04

### Added

- **Per-instance engine options replace the process-wide mutable engine globals (audit A-4).** The CLI set the MySQL session `sql_mode`, zero-date policy, VStream/native copy-table-parallelism overrides, VStream preserve-skew override, and SQLite default date-encoding as process-global mutable state (`mysql.SetSessionSQLMode`/`SetZeroDateMode`/`Set{VStream,Native}CopyTableParallelismOverride`/`SetVStreamPreserveSkewOverride`, `sqlite.SetDefaultDateEncoding`) — so a fleet `sync run` hosting multiple syncs in one process could only carry ONE value of each knob across all of them (the CLAUDE.md zero-value-trap class; June's A-5 `SetApplicationID`→`ir.ConnectionLabeler` fix is the template). They are now plumbed as per-engine-instance options applied at engine resolution: `mysql.Engine` gains `WithSQLMode`/`WithZeroDate`/`With{VStream,}CopyTableParallelism`/`WithVStreamPreserveSkew`, `sqlite.Engine`/`d1Engine` gain `WithDateEncoding`, and the CLI sets them per sync via structural optional interfaces (`applyEngineOptions`) at every value-carrying command plus the fleet `buildStreamerFromSpec` — keeping engine-specifics out of the neutral `ir` package. `sql_mode` injection moved from DSN parsing into the `openDB` chokepoint, the DDL-emit backslash policy now rides a per-writer `mysqlEmitter`, and the reader zero-date fold happens at construction. Every option's zero value reproduces today's default, so the CLI-flag > DSN-param > default precedence and the unset-flag common case stay byte-identical. Pinned by per-option precedence unit tests, a whole-emit-tree backslash pin across both sql_modes, and `applyEngineOptions` CLI tests; MySQL-engine integration suite green. Deferred (flagged in-code): the sqlite-trigger/d1-trigger CDC change-decode path does not yet receive the engine's `--sqlite-date-encoding` default (its decoder folds an unset DSN to ISO — loud refusal on mismatch, never silent-wrong; the DSN-param path is unaffected).
- **A MySQL DSN `sql_mode` param that disagrees with `--mysql-sql-mode` on NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES now WARNs at connect.** sluice's DDL emitters decide whether to double string-literal backslashes off the configured session sql_mode, but a DSN-level `sql_mode` param wins on the actual wire (the two-tier override); when the two disagree on the NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES bit, the emitter would escape a backslash-bearing DEFAULT/ENUM/COMMENT/expression literal against the wrong mode. The `openDB` sql_mode-injection chokepoint now WARNs once per connect — naming both values and the NBE bit on each side — when an operator-supplied DSN `sql_mode` disagrees. It never blocks (the DSN override is legitimate; align `--mysql-sql-mode` to fix), fires only when the operator set the DSN param (sluice's own injected mode agrees by construction), and is silent for both-agree and no-param cases. Pinned over both disagreement directions, both agreeing regimes, the no-param case, and the NBE classifier across quoted/mixed-case/comma-list forms.
- **`--stage-local` D1 imports share the single-page prefetch fetcher (ADR-0151, audit P-2 residual).** The stage-local materializer's page loop was still strictly sequential while the bulk-read path already prefetched one page ahead (v0.99.178); `stageD1Table` now drives the same fetcher goroutine over an unbuffered handoff, hiding one HTTP RTT per 1000-row page behind the local insert (exactly one page of read-ahead, memory bounded at one extra page). Contract preserved and pinned at the staging seam: byte-identical request order/bounds (incl. the > 2^53 bound), in-sequence loud failure of a prefetched page, a cancelled fetch reaps the goroutine and surfaces loudly (never a silently truncated staged file), and overlap proven deterministically by holding the staging file's write lock while observing the page-2 request.

### Changed

- **The reverse-direction MySQL-source backslash surface (MySQL literals → PG/SQLite targets) was audited and found faithful by construction; now locked by regression pins (SEC-1b; audit-clean, not a fix).** Phase-A ground truth on real MySQL 8.0 (both sql_mode regimes): the MySQL reader hands writers raw value bytes (COLUMN_DEFAULT/COLUMN_COMMENT/TABLE_COMMENT arrive decoded from information_schema; `parseEnumOrSet` decodes the escaped ENUM/SET COLUMN_TYPE), and the PG/SQLite `quoteSQLString` correctly leave backslash literal — PG pins `standard_conforming_strings=on` and SQLite has no backslash escapes, so neither target may double backslashes. No mis-emission exists; unit pins over the {plain, trailing, doubled, quote-adjacent} backslash matrix on both writers plus MySQL→PG and MySQL→SQLite cross-engine integration pins (copied row, target-side DEFAULT-applied row, PG enum labels, SET-default array member, comments — all exact source bytes) prevent a future reader/writer change from silently breaking it.

### Internal

- **CI/test hardening (no released-binary behavior change).** `-race` added to the chaos and reshard extended-suites legs (budgets bumped ~1.5×), a dispatch-only ddlfixture leg added and wired into the run-filter-coverage guard, and `check-shard-coverage.sh` made two-way so a stale shard prefix left after a package removal fails Lint (with a self-tidying `DEFENSIVE_PREFIXES` list). A differential httptest pin now reproduces live D1's code-7500 char-class-GLOB rejection for the `--infer-types` class (v0.99.167), and the zero-test `d1-trigger` package gains a registration/capabilities/write-surface smoke test.
- **Structural/quality debt paydown.** The generative round-trip fuzz harness (registry/generator/oracle, ~1.5k LOC of per-dialect DDL-emission strings) moved out of the shipped orchestrator compile unit into `internal/pipeline/internal/fuzzgen` (pipeline-archive fuzzgen ctor symbols 12→0; dialect knowledge no longer leaks into the neutral pipeline package). The keyless-insert at-least-once WARN prose was hoisted to `appliershared.WarnKeyless` (assembled log lines kept byte-identical per engine). `internal/engines/sqlite` gained a consolidated `capabilities_assert.go` and `internal/pipeline` a mechanical extension-catalog lock-step test (drift in either direction now fails a test instead of a user's migrate). The per-cell D1 `typeofAlias` build was hoisted to a per-table alias slice (allocation, behavior-identical), and two genuinely dead import-pin vars were dropped.

## [0.99.179] - 2026-07-04

### Fixed

- **A refused PG-source `sync start` cold-start no longer orphans the replication slot it just created (Bug 177; loud failure, zero data risk; latent in v0.10.0–v0.99.178 — every release with the populated-target cold-start refusal).** The cold-start opened the snapshot (which atomically creates the logical replication slot) BEFORE the target-side preflights, and every refusal/failure exit merely closed the stream — deliberately leaving the slot, since a CONSUMED stream's slot is the CDC resume anchor. But an unconsumed cold start has no anchor: the orphaned slot pinned source WAL until manually dropped, and the `SLUICE-E-COLDSTART-TARGET-NOT-EMPTY` hint's own preferred recovery (`--reset-target-data`) then failed on `replication slot "sluice_slot" already exists` until an un-hinted manual `sluice slot drop`. `ir.SnapshotStream` gains an optional `Abandon()` teardown ("this cold start will never consume the stream") that the PG engine implements as close-everything-then-`pg_drop_replication_slot` (the repl conn closes first — the creating walsender holds the slot acquired); every pre-anchor exit in the sync cold-start (target-writer open failures, connection-budget/RLS/shard/populated-target refusals, bulk-copy failure, the CDC-anchor write itself failing) now abandons instead of closes, on the single-database AND multi-database/multi-schema paths. The boundary is the anchor write: once the CDC position is durably persisted, warm-resume depends on the slot and teardowns keep today's Close. Engines whose opens create nothing durable (MySQL binlog, VStream) have no AbandonFn and Abandon degrades to Close — byte-identical. A failed slot drop is LOUD (WARN with the manual `pg_drop_replication_slot` command + surfaced error). The sync-mode refusal hint's recovery (a) now also names the slot-drop step for the shape the fix cannot reach (a hard-killed process can't clean up). Pinned by unit tests (Abandon dispatch/fallback/error propagation; the gate-preflight refusal invokes Abandon and a passing preflight leaves the stream open) and real-PG integration tests (Abandon drops the slot + re-open succeeds while Close still preserves it; the full Bug-177 shape end-to-end — refusal → zero slots on the source → `--reset-target-data` recovery succeeds first try).

## [0.99.178] - 2026-07-04

### Added

- **Byte-targeted ~1 MiB INSERT batching on the MySQL bulk-load path (ADR-0150, roadmap item 54).** The batched-INSERT bulk loops (plain + idempotent, shared by the ADR-0097/0102 parallel fan-outs) flushed on a fixed 500-row cap, producing 50–100 KB statements on narrow rows — and on Vitess/PlanetScale, which has no LOAD DATA, that path IS the bulk-load path, so WAN imports paid 10–20× more round trips than necessary (the one remaining lever the 2026-07 perf research found on the PS write side). A shared `insertBatcher` composer now accumulates to an estimated ~1 MiB statement byte target (the pscale-dumper size, equal to the CDC applier's ADR-0139 coalescing constant; estimated via `ir.ApproximateRowBytes`, no double-encoding), demotes the row cap to a 10,000-row safety ceiling, and bounds placeholders at 60,000 (MySQL's prepared-statement parameter count is 16-bit); an oversize single row ships alone (never split, never refused — `max_allowed_packet` stays the loud bound). Statement COMPOSITION is the only change: every value still binds through the same `prepareValue` codec (byte-identical wire encoding, the Bug-74 property), and each flush remains one autocommit statement = one transaction, so the ~20 s Vitess killer, AIMD, connection budgets, grow-gate, and reparent-retry are untouched (a retried batch is the same single atomic INSERT, just bigger). Reaches migrate (PS/Vitess always; vanilla on the LOAD DATA fallback + all idempotent/resume/chunked writes), sync cold-start COPY, and the restore/chain-restore/broker full-restore legs; LOAD DATA, CDC steady-state apply (already 1 MiB via ADR-0139/0140), and other engines byte-identical (perf-parity matrix row 3 updated). Companion hint: one INFO per writer on hosted-PlanetScale targets that writes are tier-CPU-bound, not connection-bound (ADR-0116) — a larger tier/Metal is the lever, not more parallelism; self-hosted `vitess` excluded, zero-value-safe gate. Pinned by unit tests (byte-target primary, ceilings, placeholder clamp, oversize, flush-boundary exactness, ≥10× fewer statements vs the 500-row baseline) plus real-MySQL integration legs (value-matrix round-trip through >500-row statements; a >1 MiB row ships alone byte-exact).
- **Single-page prefetch on the `d1` query-API reader (ADR-0151, roadmap item 54).** The live-D1 reader's page loop was strictly sequential — fetch page N over HTTP, decode, stream, THEN request N+1 — even though N's last key (the only input N+1 needs) is known the moment N arrives, so every 1000-row page paid one avoidable WAN round trip of dead time (the dominant per-page cost against the Cloudflare API). A single fetcher goroutine now issues page N+1's request while page N streams, handing pages over an UNBUFFERED channel — exactly one page of read-ahead (memory bounded at ≈ one extra page), never an N-deep pipeline (the chain-replay read-ahead shape from v0.99.176). Contract preserved and pinned by httptest units: requests byte-identical (order + exact-text string bounds incl. > 2^53), a failed prefetched page surfaces IN SEQUENCE after the prior page's rows, a ctx-cancelled fetch reports the cause via `Err()` instead of a clean silently-truncated read (a hazard the naive handoff would have introduced — named and pinned), the fetcher is reaped on every return path (no goroutine leak), and the LIMIT/OFFSET fallback prefetches through the same loop. Reaches both D1-source read paths — `migrate --source-driver d1` and the `d1-trigger` snapshot leg. RTT-hiding only; within-table D1 chunking stays a deferred follow-up. Landed through the `-race` integration gate (concurrency chunk).

### Fixed

- **Latent >65,535-placeholder failure on very wide tables at the old 500-row cap (loud failure; latent in every release since v0.1.0, never reported in the field).** The batched-INSERT path bound rows × non-generated columns placeholders per statement with no parameter bound, so a full 500-row flush on a table with ≥132 bindable columns exceeded MySQL's 16-bit prepared-statement parameter limit and the server refused the statement — a loud abort with exit ≠ 0, not silent loss, reachable only on the batched path (PS/Vitess bulk, MySQL idempotent/resume writes). The new 60,000-placeholder bound clamps the per-table row ceiling so any column count composes valid statements. Nothing to re-verify. Pinned by `TestInsertBatcher_PlaceholderBoundClampsWideTables`.

### Changed

- **`--max-buffer-bytes` on the MySQL batched bulk path now only LOWERS the effective per-statement byte target** (see Added): a value below 1 MiB tightens the trigger, preserving ADR-0028's bound-the-accumulation intent; a larger value leaves the ~1 MiB target in charge (previously moot — the 500-row cap always bound first). Applier-side ADR-0028 byte caps untouched.
- **Fuzz round-trip harness: the four SQLite directions + a tightened cross-engine oracle (dev-facing, test-integrity only).** `allDirections()` gains sqlite→postgres/mysql (ADR-0128/0129) and postgres/mysql→sqlite (ADR-0134) with storage-class-aware value domains (>2^53 ints, boundary-byte blobs, MySQL-TIMESTAMP-window temporals), every exclusion a cited documented asymmetry, and a central `expectedOutcome()` treating ADR-0134 emit refusals as loud-refuse = PASS / rows-in-target = FAIL; no sqlite→sqlite (writer re-canonicalisation would defeat the same-engine text oracle — value identity stays pinned by the hand-authored fixtures). The cross-engine no-faithful-columns regime now FAILS on a row-count skew — previously uncompared for ALL cross-engine directions, so the tightening benefits the existing PG↔MySQL directions too. All 8 directions green locally at the CI smoke budget; workflow budget raised accordingly.
- **Pre-commit hooks self-heal golangci-lint's stale-worktree cache phantoms** (clean cache + retry once on failure; real findings reproduce on the retry). Dev-tooling only.

## [0.99.177] - 2026-07-04

### Added

- **`backup full` gains within-table read chunking (ADR-0149, perf-parity gap #1, roadmap item 54).** A corpus dominated by ONE huge table backed up single-stream while migrate split the same table into PK ranges. The backup table pool now reuses migrate's chunk machinery verbatim: an eligible table's worker plans N disjoint half-open PK ranges pre-stream (single integer PK → MIN/MAX divide; other orderable/composite PKs → sampled keyset) and streams them concurrently, each range paged with a SQL-side inclusive upper bound (the ADR-0096 exactly-once contract), every range reader minted against the SAME exported snapshot through the pool's importer factory — consistency identical to the single-reader sweep by construction. New flags mirror migrate: `--bulk-parallelism` (0 = auto min(8, NumCPU) budget-split; 1 = off) and `--bulk-parallel-min-rows`; the table × within product is bounded by the source connection budget via one run-wide gate with tail work-stealing, and the split satisfies the cross-table axis FIRST (swapped vs migrate) so the resolved `--table-parallelism` is byte-identical to prior releases and a single-huge-table corpus hands the within axis the full remaining width. Eligibility is deliberately narrow — shareable-snapshot path (PG lazy-import), chunkable PK, above threshold; MySQL-eager FTWRL, the non-snapshot fallback, SQLite, and VStream stay single-stream with one loud INFO naming the reason (cited deliberate absences in the parity matrix). Includes the never-ANALYZEd estimator trap, backup edition (the v0.99.176 migrate-side class): the snapshot's pinned primary reader now runs an exact `COUNT(*)` on a fresh off-snapshot connection for the chunk decision (bounds and row streams stay pinned), with importer-minted range readers opted in via the new `ir.ExactCountEstimateOptIn` surface; sync cold-start minting stays declined (ADR-0079 v1.1 unchanged, pinned). Layout/resume unchanged: chunk-file naming byte-compatible, per-table atomic index allocation (collision-free + gapless; the manifest chunk list is a set — verified no downstream ordering assumption), up to ranges−1 extra partial-size chunk files per chunked table, Bug-135 re-stream-from-scratch posture and the manifest anchor untouched. Pinned by unit tests over every fallback clause + the budget split + concurrent index allocation, and PG integration legs (int-PK, collation-sensitive keyset, composite PK, per-chunk-CEK, cancel-mid-table + resume) all run never-ANALYZEd so each doubles as the estimator-trap e2e.

## [0.99.176] - 2026-07-04

### Added

- **MySQL sync cold-starts are parallel by default on the native-binlog flavor (perf-parity gap 3, roadmap item 54).** A MySQL user's first `sync start` cold-copy ran ~4× slower than the `migrate` they had just run because migrate defaults cross-table auto:4 while the sync cold-copy defaulted serial. The native cold-copy now defaults to auto min(4, tables) (clamped at 32; CLI override > DSN param > default), consistency-identical to serial by construction — the FTWRL-coordinated N-snapshot takes ONE global cut and ONE binlog position (no stitch), and the serial opener already attempted the same FTWRL on every cold start. `--copy-table-parallelism=1` (or DSN `copy_table_parallelism=1`) is the serial opt-out; FTWRL-denied sources keep the loud serial fallback. VStream deliberately stays serial-by-default — the stream count is not persisted in the position token, so a flipped default would silently re-derive a different table→stream partition for a resumed job across the upgrade boundary (a miss/double-copy silent-loss class) — and the sequential VStream cold-copy now INFO-names `--vstream-copy-table-parallelism`; trigger flavors log their serial cold-copy likewise.
- **migrate's parallel Postgres readers now share ONE exported snapshot.** Each parallel bulk-copy reader previously observed its own MVCC view (the documented consistency window on a live source); when the source is PG, the exporting connection now runs `pg_export_snapshot()` under REPEATABLE READ and every cross-table pool pair and within-table chunk reader imports that snapshot — a strict consistency upgrade with no replication prerequisites (no wal_level, no REPLICATION privilege, no slot). The pin releases the moment the copy ends (never spanning the index/constraint phases), export failure (e.g. hot standby) falls back LOUDLY to independent readers rather than failing a previously-working migrate, and non-PG sources are byte-identical (capability-gated). Chunk-count DECISIONS deliberately run off-snapshot with an exact-COUNT fallback for never-ANALYZEd tables, while chunk BOUNDS and row streams stay snapshot-pinned (the estimate/bounds consistency split, documented in-code). Pinned by an integration test proving post-export rows are invisible to all parallel readers and zero idle-in-transaction sessions remain at release time.
- **`--analyze-after`: opt-in post-migration statistics refresh on all three target engine families.** After constraints and views complete, migrate runs a per-table refresh — PG `ANALYZE`, MySQL `ANALYZE TABLE` (queried, not exec'd, so error result-rows are caught), SQLite `ANALYZE` — because a freshly bulk-loaded table has stale planner statistics until autovacuum catches up (pgcopydb runs VACUUM ANALYZE by default for the same reason). Advisory by contract: strictly post-success, per-table failures WARN naming the table and never fail the run, engines without the surface get one loud WARN, default off (zero-value-safe), dry-run plan carries `analyze_after`.
- **Replay-path throughput: buffered channels + one-chunk read-ahead (perf-parity gaps 2/4/6).** Restore's chunk-group row channel, chain-restore's incremental change channel, the broker's apply channel, and migrate's redact/shard-stamp tees were unbuffered rendezvous hops while migrate's discipline is a 64-slot bounded buffer everywhere — all now carry the standard 64 buffer. Chain replay additionally overlaps fetching chunk N+1 with applying N via a single fetcher goroutine handing verified chunks over an unbuffered channel (exactly one chunk of read-ahead, never an N-deep pipeline). Behavior-neutral except throughput: apply order strictly preserved, per-chunk fetch retry untouched, and durable positions/cursors only ever advance AFTER the consumer applies. Landed through the `-race` integration gate. The broker's 30 s poll-cadence copy loop is deliberately not extended (noted in the parity matrix).

### Fixed

- **Chain restore re-syncs identity sequences at the chain tail (silent-loss class on keyless tables; affected v0.17.0–v0.99.175, every release with chain restore).** `SyncIdentitySequences` ran only inside the base full's restore phase; nothing re-synced after incremental links applied higher ids, so a chain restore left every identity column's sequence at the base full's max — loud `23505` on the first post-restore insert into constrained tables, silent duplicate ids on keyless ones. Live-reproduced pre-fix. `ChainRestore.Run` now re-syncs at the tail beside the v0.99.175 standalone-sequence re-prime — the newest link's schema snapshot, retargeted and filtered like the base full's restore, through the engine-neutral `SyncIdentitySequences` surface (real on PG, documented no-op on MySQL/SQLite), data-derived MAX+1, idempotent, under the reparent retry; the broker's `--reset-target-data` reset leg inherits through `ChainRestore.Run`. Pinned by an integration test (full at max id 2, links apply 3/4, first insert must draw 5) verified failing pre-fix with the exact live 23505.
- **Temporal precision round-trips faithfully: bare stays bare, explicit `(0)` stays `(0)`, and SQLite→MySQL temporals no longer silently truncate fractional seconds (restore-parity TRIAGE #3; truncation shape affected v0.99.141–v0.99.175, catalog shapes effectively every prior release).** The IR had no precision-unspecified state for temporals, so (1) a bare PG `timestamptz`/`timestamp`/`time` materialized as `timestamp(6) …` in the target catalog, (2) the emitter's 0→bare collapse silently WIDENED an explicit `timestamptz(0)` (and MySQL `datetime(0)`, genuinely 0) to the 6-behaving bare form — accepting fractional seconds the source would reject — and (3) SQLite temporals landed on MySQL as bare `DATETIME` (≡ precision 0), silently truncating fraction-bearing migrated values to whole seconds. `ir.Time`/`ir.DateTime`/`ir.Timestamp` gain `PrecisionUnspecified` mirroring the decimal `Unconstrained` precedent: the PG reader keys declaredness off `pg_attribute.atttypmod` (information_schema cannot distinguish bare from explicit `(6)`), including temporal ARRAY elements; unspecified emits bare on PG and materializes `(6)` on MySQL; a KNOWN precision always emits, including `(0)`; SQLite→MySQL now lands `DATETIME(6)`/`TIMESTAMP(6)` with a value-level pin preserving source microseconds. The live-DDL comparison lens collapses bare≡(0)≡(6) on BOTH sides of the schema-change classifier (canonical bare form), so a bare temporal can never surface a phantom altered-column mid-sync. Append-only wire field, no format bump — old backups decode their materialized `(6)` as explicit `(6)` and restore byte-identically. Pinned per the family×shape discipline ({time, timetz, timestamp, timestamptz} × {bare, (0), mid, (6)} + array elements, wire round-trips, PG→PG `format_type` integration matrix).
- **UNIQUE constraints land on Postgres targets as constraints, not demoted unique indexes (restore-parity TRIAGE #4; affected effectively every prior release for PG targets).** A source `CONSTRAINT x UNIQUE (cols)` landed as `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX` — near-equivalent for enforcement but it broke `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT x` and diverged the pg_dump catalog. `ir.Index` gains a constraint-backed marker (set only by the PG reader from `pg_constraint`); the PG writer's index phase (live, preview, overlapped-build) skips those entries and the constraints phase adds them via `ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT` — ALL uniques before ANY FK, names VERBATIM (prefixing would break `ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT`), idempotent resume (detect-then-skip), `INCLUDE` carries, expression entries refuse loudly (sluice-bug condition), and the keyless inline promotion is preserved without a duplicate ADD. MySQL/SQLite targets keep the documented plain-unique-index asymmetry. Backup-wire carry pinned so a future marshal change cannot reopen the demotion through the restore door; end-to-end pinned by a real `ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT` upsert on the migrated target. The restore-parity oracle's TRIAGE ledger is now EMPTY — every remaining divergence is a cited, documented decision.
- **Bug 176: the double-quoted-token refusal now carries `SLUICE-E-EXPR-BACKSLASH-LITERAL` and exit 3 (affected v0.99.175 only).** The v0.99.175 SEC-1 refusal of SQLite double-quoted tokens bound for MySQL fired loudly but exited 1 uncoded, while the same release's `docs/operator/error-codes.md` declared that code covers the shape — a machine-contract inconsistency agents branching on code/exit-3 missed. Found same-day by the v0.99.175 regression cycle; the refusal now honors the shipped contract and the pin asserts the code rides the error.

### Changed

- **Native-MySQL sync cold-copy default: serial → auto min(4, tables)** (see Added). Operators who relied on a serial cold-start set `--copy-table-parallelism=1` (or the DSN param). VStream and trigger flavors unchanged.
- **Temporal catalog rendering on new targets** (see Fixed): bare stays bare, `(0)` stays `(0)`, SQLite→MySQL lands `(6)` — values identical or MORE faithful, never less. Upgraded backup chains may log a one-time phantom `alter_table` delta on the first DDL-free window post-upgrade (no-op replay, self-heals after one segment); an OLD binary restoring a NEW manifest cross-engine to MySQL emits bare `DATETIME` (silent fractional-second rounding) — upgrade restore-side binaries first.
- **Performance research + cross-mode parity program:** `docs/research/perf-gap-analysis-2026-07.md` (the PG→PG pgcopydb software gap is small; the big structural remainder is inline-PK maintenance vs post-load parallel PK sort), the living `docs/dev/perf-parity-matrix.md` (11 accidental gaps found; gaps 2/3/4/6 closed this release), flat-file-sources research (`docs/research/flat-file-sources.md`, roadmap item 55), roadmap item 54 backlog, and a TAP_GITHUB_TOKEN expiry runbook note.

## [0.99.175] - 2026-07-04

### Added

- **Standalone Postgres sequences are carried as first-class schema objects (restore-parity oracle finding #1; backup FormatVersion 4).** The PG reader previously collapsed every `nextval()` column default into an identity column, so explicit `CREATE SEQUENCE` objects, shared sequences, re-optioned serials, and app-driven sequences were silently not carried — a shared sequence became independent per-table identities (breaking cross-table uniqueness for future inserts), sequence options were lost, and `nextval('myseq')` on the target failed loudly because the sequence didn't exist. The reader now classifies `nextval()` defaults three ways — identity stays identity, the exact factory-serial shape keeps the documented serial→identity modernization, everything else reads into the new `ir.Schema.Sequences` with full options (AS type / START / INCREMENT / MIN / MAX / CACHE / CYCLE), `OWNED BY` binding, and live position (`last_value`/`is_called`) — and the PG writer creates each sequence and primes its position atomically in ONE transaction before tables, so post-migration `nextval()` continues exactly where the source would. Re-primes are forward-only and direction-aware everywhere (resume, an operator-pre-created sequence, chain-restore at the chain tail from the newest link's end-of-window positions, and `cutover`, whose prime walk now covers standalone sequences with the identity-column decision tree; CYCLE sequences get a named skip). Cross-engine targets refuse loudly (PG→MySQL-family in `checkCrossEngineSupportable`, SQLite/D1 in the writer), naming the sequence and the remedy. Backups whose schema carries standalone sequences stamp the new **FormatVersion 4** so pre-v0.99.175 binaries refuse-before-touch instead of restoring sequence-less; stamping is proportional (a sequence-free backup keeps its old version and stays readable by older binaries). `ComputeSchemaHash` canonicalizes sequence positions away so end-of-window position refreshes are hash-invisible and DML can never masquerade as a schema change. Pinned by value-level ground truth (first post-migration insert continues at the source's next value across migrate/resume/chain-restore/cutover, including a descending sequence) and the full option/ownership matrix. Affected: every release before v0.99.175 for PG sources with non-factory-shape sequences.
- **`--format json` result envelopes on the primary verbs + JSON `--dry-run` plans.** `migrate`, `sync start`, `backup full`, and `restore` can emit exactly one terminal JSON envelope on stdout (status `completed`/`refused`/`failed`, elapsed, engines, per-table stats, resume affordance, error with code/hint, next-step suggestions), and `migrate --dry-run --format json` / `sync start --dry-run --format json` emit the plan as `{command, dry_run, plan}`. Text mode is byte-identical; slog stays on stderr; every registered DSN is scrubbed from the rendered envelope via the diagnose redactor. Errors before pipeline engagement classify as `refused`, after as `failed`, and a refusal-class coded error reclassifies the envelope to `refused` even post-engagement so status stays consistent with exit code 3. A new top-level `AGENTS.md` documents the agent operating surface (command taxonomy, env-first credentials, dry-run-first workflow, envelope/plan/log/error-code map).
- **Stable error codes (`SLUICE-E-<DOMAIN>-<SLUG>`) with structured hints, and an exit-code taxonomy (new `internal/sluicecode` registry, `docs/operator/error-codes.md`).** Every error class that already carries an operator hint or named remedy now also carries a frozen machine-matchable code — seeded with the 9 runtime hint classes plus 5 named refusals (cold-start target-not-empty, PG extension not enabled, zero-date, NUL byte, SEC-1 backslash/double-quote literal) — surfaced as `code`/`hint` attributes on the terminal `--log-format json` ERROR record, on the sync supervisor's permanent-failure record, and in the JSON envelopes. Exit codes: 0/1 unchanged; **2** = config-file load/parse error; **3** = named refusal (kong `ExitCoder`); 80 documented as kong's own usage-error code. `verify`/`diff`/`sync-health` keep their long-standing per-command contracts. Registry↔docs consistency is test-enforced in both directions.
- **PG→PG restore-parity oracle (roadmap item 51).** New integration harness (`TestMigrate_DumpParity_PGKitchenSink`): sluice migrates a kitchen-sink schema into target A, version-matched `pg_dump|psql` restores into target B, and `pg_dump --schema-only` of both targets is compared as statement sets keyed by object identity — never line-diffed. Every divergence must match an allowlist entry carrying a reason + citation; undocumented gaps are marked TRIAGE and banner-logged. Its first run surfaced the standalone-sequence collapse, the per-column COLLATE drop, and the default-rendering gaps fixed in this release; enum, domain, EXCLUDE, stored generated columns, FK actions, partial/expression indexes, and COMMENTs verified at full parity on the first run.

### Fixed

- **SQLite/D1 expression DEFAULTs no longer land as silently wrong literal values (silent-loss class; affected v0.99.141–v0.99.174).** SQLite's `PRAGMA table_info` strips the outer parens off a parenthesised expression default, so `DEFAULT ('a' || 'b')` is reported as `'a' || 'b'` — and the reader's endpoint check swallowed it as the WRONG literal `a' || 'b`, which landed on any target as a mangled string default with exit 0, routing around every expression-boundary refusal. The reader now lexes candidate string literals for well-formedness (malformed shapes classify as expressions, never a guessed literal), and the PG/MySQL writers route portable expression bodies through the ADR-0133 translator, so `DEFAULT ('a' || 'b')` now EVALUATES to `ab` on the target (integration-proven insert-and-read on real PG and MySQL). Review closed five sibling classes in the same pass: MySQL's verbatim-carry fallback no longer forwards parseable-but-divergent bodies (`upper('a') || 'b'` parsed as logical OR → default `0`; `7/2` → `3.5` vs SQLite's `3`; `7.5 % 2` → `1.5` vs `1`) — anything outside the proven-faithful residue set (`translate.SQLiteExprMySQLDefaultVerbatimSafe`) is warn-dropped table.column-named; `0x…` hex literals are refused to MySQL (context-dependent binary string — bytes `\x1a` where SQLite stores `'26'`; PG keeps hex); the SQLite writer re-parenthesises expression defaults, fixing a loud syntax error on every functional default written to a SQLite/D1 TARGET (affected v0.99.146–v0.99.174); bare `TRUE`/`FALSE` on a declared-boolean PG column now translates faithfully (non-boolean stays warn-drop, preserving the PG DEFAULT arm's never-abort contract); and the MySQL reserved-word requote no longer runs on verbatim residues (`DEFAULT "order"` previously got backticks injected INSIDE the double-quoted string — silent-wrong; residues are carried byte-verbatim). DEFAULTs are non-data metadata: migrated rows were never affected — only rows inserted on the target after migration used the wrong default. Backups taken pre-fix carry the mangled default as captured (cannot retro-repair); post-upgrade the corrected rendering can surface as a schema-hash change (drift → re-snapshot), not corruption.
- **Per-column `COLLATE` now carries to Postgres targets; cross-engine collations are a named WARN-drop policy (silent-fidelity class; affected effectively every prior release — the IR has carried per-column collation since the v0.11 line, and the PG emitter never rendered it).** `region_code text COLLATE "C"` landed bare on PG→PG, silently changing sort/comparison order and collation-dependent index/constraint behaviour. The PG emitter (`emitColumnDef`/`AlterColumnType`) now renders `COLLATE "<name>"` for PG-dialect collations, mirroring pg_dump's omission rule by construction (the reader only stores explicit non-default collations), including through domain-typed columns. Cross-engine, collation names do not translate: foreign-dialect collations are dropped with a WARN naming table + columns + collations (dialect inferred by the charset-paired rule, `translate.CollationDialect`). Also fixes the latent PG→MySQL hard failure where a PG-dialect collation forwarded verbatim died mid-create-tables with raw `Error 1273 "Unknown collation: 'C'"` — now a named WARN-drop; MySQL→MySQL keeps the full CHARACTER SET / COLLATE round-trip, and SQLite's previous silent drop becomes the same WARN. Ground-truthed by a PG→PG integration pin (target `attcollation` = `"C"` and `ORDER BY` matches the source's byte order on a seed that sorts differently under `en_US`).
- **SEC-1: MySQL-bound SQLite expressions refuse backslash string literals and double-quoted tokens; `quoteSQLString` is backslash-safe; MySQL ENUM/SET labels decode at the read boundary (silent classes).** (1) The ADR-0133 translator forwarded `'…'` literals verbatim; SQLite treats a backslash as an ordinary character while MySQL's default sql_mode treats it as an escape introducer — worst case a trailing `\` swallows the closing quote and shifts expression text into string position (the DDL-position-escape class). Backslash-bearing literals bound for MySQL DDL are now refused loudly (gencol/CHECK preflights + the DEFAULT verbatim-carry arm; index expressions WARN-skip); PG stays deliberately permissive, with `standard_conforming_strings=on` now pinned by construction on both pgx pool funnels. (2) SQLite's double-quoted-string misfeature tokenized as an identifier, sailed through as a verbatim node, and MySQL re-lexed `"…"` as a string literal with escape semantics — silent value corruption in gencol position, a vacated predicate in CHECK position; any `"`-delimited token bound for MySQL is now refused regardless of backslash content. Gencol/CHECK/index carry positions affected v0.99.144–v0.99.174; DEFAULT positions v0.99.141–v0.99.174. (3) `quoteSQLString` emitted DefaultLiteral values, ENUM/SET labels, and column/table COMMENTs with backslashes undoubled, so MySQL's lexer silently decoded them — latent since the MySQL writer's inception, material only for backslash-bearing values; it now doubles backslashes keyed off the session sql_mode (skipped under `NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES`, where doubling would itself corrupt; the v0.97.1 domain-regex pre-doubling is removed to avoid double-doubling). Ground-truthing on real MySQL 8.0 showed ENUM/SET `COLUMN_TYPE` arrives ESCAPED from information_schema, so `parseEnumOrSet` now decodes doubled backslashes at the read boundary — fixing the latent MySQL→PG doubled-enum-label drift (MySQL→MySQL previously round-tripped only by accidental escape symmetry).
- **Trigger-CDC change-log pruning is batched with a per-tick time budget (affected: v0.99.174 for the `--auto-prune-change-log` sidecar; v0.99.151–v0.99.174 for the operator-run `sluice trigger prune` stall class).** The monolithic `DELETE … WHERE id <= cut` stalled the single-writer SQLite source for its whole duration, was one long WAL-burst transaction on PG, and on D1 could blow the per-query CPU ceiling — after which the failed tick retried an even BIGGER delete next interval, so the sidecar prune could fall behind permanently. Both trigger engines now reap in bounded keyset steps (20k local/PG, 2k D1) with a 30 s per-tick budget on the sidecar path (partial tick resumes next cadence; the CLI prune runs to completion), the per-tick `COUNT(*)` is replaced with rows-affected arithmetic plus an hourly true recount, and pgtrigger's sidecar reuses one pooled connection across ticks (sqlite-trigger deliberately keeps per-tick opens — a held writable connection would pin the WAL read-mark, Bug 167). The cut-watermark invariant is unchanged and pinned across batching states: no DELETE's upper bound ever exceeds the durably-applied cut. No silent-loss exposure at any point.
- **The `d1-trigger` change-log poll batch is clamped to D1's `/query` response cap (affected v0.99.149–v0.99.174).** The shared 10,000-row poll LIMIT could overflow Cloudflare's ~1 MB response cap on a catch-up poll (change rows carry full before/after JSON images). The executor seam now declares a per-transport poll ceiling (D1 = 1,000; local SQLite keeps 10,000) and the pump's full-batch fast-repoll keys off the clamped size, so catch-up throughput is not cadence-throttled. Pinned via the httptest D1 mock (the poll SQL D1 receives carries `LIMIT 1000`).
- **SEC-2: crash/operator bundles mask bare-secret CLI flags.** `RedactCLIArgs` only recognised the DSN-bearing flag family, so `--planetscale-metrics-token`/`-id`, `--notify-webhook`, `--notify-slack`, `--notify-smtp-password`, and `--encryption-passphrase` values survived verbatim into `sluice diagnose` bundles via `os.Args`. They are now masked whole (`<redacted>`) in both `--flag value` and `--flag=value` forms. Operators who shared bundles generated with those flags on the CLI should rotate the captured secrets.
- **SEC-3: D1 HTTP client + SMTP credential hygiene.** The D1 client now path-escapes account/database ids into the query URL (a DSN id carrying `/`/`?`/`#` can no longer re-point the request at a different API path) and bounds response reads at 8 MiB with truncated error echoes; SMTP notification `Validate` now warns loudly when plain/login credentials are configured with `--notify-smtp-tls=none` (warn, not refuse — trusted-local-relay configs keep working).
- **The backup chain-compact orphan sweep now surfaces per-file delete failures.** `sweepRootSegmentArtifacts`/`sweepSegmentSubdir` discarded every `store.Delete` error, so a persistently failing delete leaked backup-store disk with zero diagnostic trail; failures are now joined and named in the existing post-commit WARN. Compaction still never fails on sweep errors (the catalog swap has already committed).
- **CI: the `-run`-filter escapee class is closed by a generalized coverage guard.** Three vstream-tagged tests plus one chaos-tagged test (the Bug-146 purged-GTID reactive-cold-start pin) compiled vet-clean but ran in NO workflow — build-tag additivity compiled them, but their names escaped their jobs' `-run` regexes (the same class as the 11 postgis tests hidden through v0.99.53). `scripts/check-run-filter-coverage.sh` replaces the postgis-only guard with a (tag; -run regex; package scopes; job) manifest for every filtered axis, wired into the Lint job and both pre-commit hooks; the four escapees are renamed/covered and re-run green. Test-integrity only — no released-binary behavior change.

### Changed

- **Exit codes 2 and 3 are carved out of the generic-failure bucket** (see Added: error codes). Scripts checking `exit != 0` are unaffected; scripts branching on `exit == 1` specifically should account for config errors now exiting 2 and named refusals exiting 3.
- **The weekly Vitess version matrix now runs the full `TestVitessCluster` suite** (previously Bug-27 geometry only; leg timeouts widened 30→45 m), and **Dependabot gains a 7-day cooldown** on all three ecosystems (auto-merge no longer lands a fresh upstream release within hours of publication; security updates stay immediate).
- **Docs truth sweep:** `docs/architecture.md` module tree regenerated from the real repo, CLAUDE.md state-of-play rewritten maintenance-proof, stale ADR-range/CI comments de-numerified, roadmap item 45 stamped SHIPPED (v0.99.129), dead lint excludes dropped, and a new `--log-format json` operator-docs section. Plus `docs/dev/branch-protection.md` corrected to the live six required checks (re-applying protection from the old five-check payload would have silently un-required the vstream suite), local pre-commit gates now run both CI coverage guards, and the prebaked-image workflow's checkout action is SHA-pinned.

## [0.99.174] - 2026-07-03

### Added

- **SQLite/D1 → canonical expression translator for carried generated-column / CHECK / index bodies (ADR-0133 follow-up).** ADR-0133 carries SQLite generated columns, CHECK constraints, and partial/expression indexes into the IR tagged dialect `sqlite`, but emitted them verbatim — so any non-portable SQLite construct loud-failed at target CREATE-TABLE time. sluice now translates the **provably-safe subset** to the PG/MySQL dialect (`internal/translate/sqlite_expr.go`): `+ - *`, comparisons, `AND/OR/NOT`, `IS [NOT] NULL`, `||` (→`CONCAT` on MySQL), PG-only `/` (integer-division matches SQLite), `abs`, `coalesce`, `ifnull`→`COALESCE`, `nullif`, `length` (→`CHAR_LENGTH` on MySQL), 1-arg `trim`/`ltrim`/`rtrim`, `substr` (literal start ≥ 1), `min`/`max` (MySQL only — `LEAST`/`GREATEST` propagate NULL like SQLite there), `cast AS text`/`real`, `cast AS numeric` (PG only), and the current-instant keywords. A generated-column or CHECK body outside the allowlist is now **refused loudly at schema emit** (table/column-named) rather than emitted verbatim — because a syntactically-valid-but-divergent operator (`%`, MySQL `/`) is silently accepted by the target with a wrong result; a partial/expression index outside the allowlist WARN-skips (a performance object, not correctness). The allowlist is deliberately conservative: an independent value-fidelity review caught six silent-corruption vectors in the first cut (`cast AS numeric`→MySQL `DECIMAL(10,0)` rounding, `min`/`max`→PG `LEAST`/`GREATEST` NULL divergence, `cast AS integer` truncate-vs-round, `%` non-integer divergence, `substr` negative-start, MySQL `/`·`%` verbatim-accept) — all excluded/made-loud, and pinned by src==dst *value*-ground-truth integration tests on real PG + MySQL (the target-recomputed stored generated column equals SQLite's computed value) plus loud-refuse pins for every excluded construct. `strftime`/`julianday`/`unixepoch`/date-time-with-args and other divergent functions remain a documented follow-up.

- **Automatic in-stream change-log pruning for trigger-CDC (`--auto-prune-change-log`, ADR-0137 Phase B).** Phase A shipped the operator-run `sluice trigger prune`; Phase B makes it automatic. When `--auto-prune-change-log` is set (opt-in; default off), a streamer sidecar prunes the source `sluice_change_log` on a cadence (`--auto-prune-interval`, default 5 min) up to the TARGET's durably-applied frontier minus `--auto-prune-keep` (default 1000), so a continuous `sqlite-trigger` / `d1-trigger` / `pgtrigger` sync bounds its own change-log growth without a cron. It reads the durable position via `ChangeApplier.ReadPosition` (the same cdc-state row `sluice trigger prune` reads) and hands it to the source engine's new `ir.ChangeLogPruner`, which decodes it with its own codec and computes `cut = appliedLastID - keep` — **never above the durable frontier** (pruning higher would delete not-yet-applied rows → silent loss). It is fully failure-isolated: a position-read or prune error is logged at WARN and swallowed, never breaking or stalling the sync. Zero-value-safe (off ⇒ no-op for every construction path and every non-trigger source).

## [0.99.173] - 2026-07-02

### Added
