Normally a cancel is noticed by some stream still in a `ctx`-observing select, which trips `failCopy()`→broadcast and frees the parked ones; but if ALL K streams are parked on their sub-caps at the moment a bare `ctx` cancel fires (operator Ctrl-C / graceful drain during the cold-copy phase with no consumer draining), no goroutine observes `ctx`, nothing broadcasts, and the copy's `WaitGroup` join blocks forever — the shutdown hangs. The fix adds a `ctx`-cancel waker goroutine that trips `failCopy(ctx.Err())` (setting the error + broadcasting) so every parked producer wakes and unwinds; it exits with no side effect on the normal-completion path (it selects on the copy-done signal, with a re-check so a cancel/completion race can never retroactively fail an already-finished copy). No change to the happy path, to throughput, or to the exactly-once / position-recording contract (a cancelled copy still records no position, exactly as a cancel detected by a stream's own select already did). Found as an intermittent `-race` test timeout under heavy parallel load and confirmed via a settle-then-cancel regression test that deterministically reaches the all-streams-parked state (it hangs to the timeout on the unpatched code and passes with the fix). Only affects the concurrent VStream cold-copy (`vstream_copy_table_parallelism` / auto-shard, K>1); the serial and single-stream copy paths were never exposed.

## [0.99.78] - 2026-06-19

### Removed

- **The experimental `--apply-pipeline-depth` flag (ADR-0104 Phase 1) is removed — superseded by `--apply-concurrency` (v0.99.77).** Phase 1 was an in-order pipelined-COMMIT design that, on the live cross-region PlanetScale-MySQL link, was measured to overlap only the commit RTT and not the per-batch data execs, so it delivered no throughput improvement (≈ serial, 7 of 8 backends idle). Its successor — `--apply-concurrency`, the key-hash concurrent apply path added in v0.99.77 and live-validated at ~4× — fully replaces it, so the dead commit-pipeline implementation (`mysqlPipeline` and ~1,100 lines of code + tests) and the `--apply-pipeline-depth` flag are removed wholesale rather than left as confusing, ineffective scaffolding. The change is a pure removal that reverts the batched-apply seam to its pre-ADR-0104 serial shape with no change to serial-apply behavior; the concurrent path (`--apply-concurrency`) is untouched. Operators who want concurrent CDC apply on a MySQL target should use `--apply-concurrency=W` (which actually closes the cross-region apply-deficit wedge); `--apply-pipeline-depth` never did, so removing it loses no working capability.

### Fixed

- **De-flaked `TestShardProgressWatchdog_EndToEnd_AsymmetricStallWarns` under `-race` (test-only; no runtime change).** The per-shard stall watchdog's end-to-end test sent a redundant initial `observeAdvance` and tried to drain it by firing the fake timer, but firing the timer drains the `timer.C()` select case, not the depth-1 advances channel — so the first advance could still occupy the buffer when the second was sent, and the watchdog's intended drop-when-full behavior silently dropped it under an unfavorable `-race` schedule, leaving no fresh peer and so no WARN (a CI timeout). The production watchdog is correct (it pre-seeds every known shard's clock at start, so both shards are fresh at t=0 without any observe); the redundant observe was a test-harness artifact and is removed, mirroring the non-flaky from-start-frozen test. No change to the watchdog itself.

## [0.99.77] - 2026-06-19

### Fixed

- **Warm-resume of a multi-shard Vitess/PlanetScale source no longer FALSELY refuses when the schema-history holds two same-schema anchors at incomparable per-shard positions — the bug that made a multi-shard sync re-snapshot on EVERY restart and never warm-resume into CDC (HIGH resilience; live large-scale finding).** The ADR-0049 schema-history resolver (`ResolveSchemaVersion`) picks the schema in effect at a CDC position from retained boundary anchors, ordered by the per-shard GTID PARTIAL order. On a multi-shard source every cold-start / auto-resnapshot re-emits a table's CURRENT boundary at FRESH per-shard GTIDs (the reader's true-delta `snapshotSig` cache is per-instance, so a new reader re-snapshots each table on first touch), so the SAME schema accumulates multiple anchors whose multi-shard positions are mutually incomparable — each ahead on a different shard. The resolver treated ANY two incomparable candidates both at-or-before the position as a fatal ambiguity → `ir.ErrPositionInvalid` → ADR-0093 auto-resnapshot, i.e. a wasteful full re-snapshot (idempotent re-upsert, no drop — an availability/throughput hit, never data loss) on every restart, so the stream could never reach steady CDC. Ground truth came from a real 2-shard Vitess→PlanetScale-MySQL warm-resume (anchors `{-80:5248456, 80-:5328309}` and `{-80:5545777, 80-:4969379}` both ≤ the resume position, each ahead on a different shard). The fix: when two candidate anchors are incomparable, compare their decode contracts via `SchemaSignatureOf().Equal()` — the SAME signature equality the CDC reader uses to decide a "true delta" worth a new version, so resolver and emitter agree on what "same version" means — and if they are identical (the multi-shard / re-snapshot duplicate), keep the current best (both decode identically, so there is no real ambiguity); only genuinely DISTINCT schemas at incomparable anchors still refuse loudly with `ErrPositionInvalid` (the "which DDL is in effect?" safety floor is preserved). Validated live: the fixed binary warm-resumed the exact failing 2-shard state straight into CDC with no re-snapshot, and the applied position advanced. Pinned per type-family through the real codec round-trip (int width, decimal precision/scale, varchar length, enum value-set/order, array element, time precision, timestamp tz) plus a ≥3-anchor incomparable chain (the real multi-shard accumulation), order-permuted; value-fidelity-reviewed to confirm `SchemaSignature` captures every decode-affecting parameter so the relaxation can never merge two genuinely-different decodes.

### Added

- **`--apply-concurrency W` (PREVIEW): concurrent key-hashed CDC apply for the MySQL target, ~4× apply throughput measured live, default-off (ADR-0104, roadmap item 23(c)).** On a cross-region multi-shard Vitess/PlanetScale → PlanetScale-MySQL `sync`, steady-state CDC apply is RTT-bound on a single stream and falls below the source write rate, which causes the per-shard `MinimizeSkew` wedge (one shard's apply backlog makes vtgate hold its peer). This release adds an opt-in lever: the merged change stream is fanned across W in-order apply lanes by primary-key hash — every change for a given key lands on the same lane and is applied in source order there, so a dependent INSERT→UPDATE→DELETE on a row can never reorder — and the lanes commit CONCURRENTLY on a dedicated W-backend pool, lifting aggregate apply throughput toward W× while the resume position advances only to a source boundary durable across ALL lanes (a deliberate, exactly-once-preserving relaxation: the persisted position can lag the durable data but never lead it, so keyed tables stay exactly-once via idempotent re-apply on resume and keyless tables keep their at-least-once guarantee unchanged). Measured live on a 2-shard Vitess→PlanetScale-MySQL link: serial apply ~52 rows/s vs `--apply-concurrency=4` ~206 rows/s (≈4.0×), with the concurrent rate exceeding the source rate so the behind shard's backlog drained instead of growing — the apply-bound wedge closes. `W` 0/1 (default) is serial, byte-identical to before; `W>1` engages and opens exactly W dedicated backends (the connection budget — no auto-clamp). **Marked PREVIEW** because of one known v1 limitation: the lanes use static batch sizing (no per-lane AIMD yet), so a PlanetScale tx-killer abort stops the stream and warm-resumes (correct and non-lossy, via the resume fix above) rather than shrinking the batch in place like the serial path — per-lane AIMD + tx-killer convergence is the tracked follow-up. It supersedes the experimental `--apply-pipeline-depth` (whose commit-only overlap was measured ineffective on the same link and which remains only as a serial fallback). Inert on Postgres targets (Postgres uses ADR-0092's within-transaction statement pipelining instead). Pinned by a concurrent integration suite under the `-race` gate (dependent-row ordering + exactly-once across W lanes, a depth-1-vs-W byte-identical differential, and the seq-frontier position on a boundary-less stream).

## [0.99.76] - 2026-06-19

### Fixed

- **The per-shard VStream stall WARN (v0.99.74) now detects a shard that is wedged from the very first CDC event — it was previously blind to exactly that case.** The per-shard progress watchdog populated its per-shard last-advance clock ONLY when a shard delivered its first advancing VGTID, so a shard that froze at the start of the CDC tail (and therefore never delivered an advancing event) was never entered into the watchdog's state and `scan` never even considered it — no WARN, ever. That was precisely the live silence on a multi-shard Vitess→PlanetScale-MySQL sync where one shard was frozen from the snapshot→CDC handoff while the other advanced: the detector built for that wedge was structurally blind to its worst case. The fix threads the resolved shard layout into the watchdog and PRE-SEEDS every known shard's clock at start, so a shard that never advances goes stale after the window and is detected (given a fresh peer — the asymmetric signature). Seeding at start time (not the zero time) preserves a full window of warm-up grace, and combined with the existing serving-proven gate and the "a peer must be fresh" requirement a just-started stream still cannot warn during warm-up. A shard absent from the known layout (e.g. one appearing after a reshard) is still tracked lazily on first advance, as before. This is observability-only — the WARN never alters the stream's resilience; it just no longer misses the from-start-frozen case. Pinned by a new end-to-end watchdog test (a shard NEVER observed advancing still WARNs once stale vs a fresh peer) plus the existing scan/latch/serving-gate/disabled/teardown pins. (Investigation note: a load-forced live capture established that the dominant per-shard wedge is a delivery-side `MinimizeSkew` hold under a cross-region apply-deficit — vtgate stops delivering the held shard — which a stream reopen does NOT clear; the durable fix there is apply throughput, tracked separately. This release is the detector-completeness fix, not the recovery.)

## [0.99.75] - 2026-06-19

### Fixed

- **Warm-resume of a multi-shard Vitess/PlanetScale source is no longer FALSELY rejected as "position purged" and forced into a needless full cold-start re-copy (HIGH; live large-scale finding).** The Bug-146/ADR-0093 purged-GTID preflight (`verifyVStreamPositionReachable`) checked each shard's resume position with `GTID_SUBSET(@@global.gtid_purged, <that shard's resume gtid>)` run through a keyspace-level vtgate target (`keyspace@<tablettype>`). Live multi-shard ground truth (three-phase Phase A against a real 2-shard Vitess source): **vtgate answers `@@global.gtid_purged` from an ARBITRARY shard per query** — a bare `SELECT @@global.gtid_purged` returned shard `-80`'s purged set while the same variable evaluated inside a function call on the very next query returned shard `80-`'s. So the per-shard check compared a *random* shard's purged set — carrying a *different* server-UUID — against the shard whose resume gtid was being verified, and a different-UUID set is never a subset, so `GTID_SUBSET` returned `0` → a false `ir.ErrPositionInvalid` on essentially every multi-shard warm-resume. The orchestrator's ADR-0022 fall-through then cold-started: a full re-copy with `--force-cold-start`, or a loud refusal without it. This is the real cause behind repeated re-copies (and some handoff failures previously attributed to source binlog-retention expiry) observed on the large-scale Vitess→PlanetScale runs. The fix shard-scopes the probe: each shard's `@@global.gtid_purged` is now read with the connection targeted at that shard specifically (`keyspace:shard@<tablettype>`, via a new `shardScopedTarget` helper, opening a per-shard probe connection), so `@@global.gtid_purged` is pinned to the same shard whose resume gtid is compared — same-UUID sets, correct subset result. The degrade-don't-refuse philosophy is preserved (a probe open/query failure proceeds without the check rather than forcing a spurious re-snapshot); only a definitive `GTID_SUBSET=0` for the *correct* shard refuses. Validated live: warm-resume of a 2-shard keyspace onto PlanetScale-MySQL now resumes CDC from the persisted position with zero false rejection and zero re-copy (and a transiently-wedged shard recovered on the reopen). Unsharded keyspaces are unaffected (a single shard answers `@@global.gtid_purged` unambiguously; the empty/`-`-shard case falls back to the keyspace-level target). Pinned by `TestShardScopedTarget`; the multi-shard false-reject itself reproduces only against a real sharded vtgate (the vstream integration tier + the live run).

### Added

- **Cross-engine schema-narrowing advisory notices now also surface on the `sync` cold-start path, not just `migrate` (Bug 157 Q2).** The cluster of advisory WARN notices that `migrate`/`schema preview` emit for deliberate cross-engine narrowings — MySQL `bigint unsigned`→PG `bigint` (Bug 11), unconstrained PG `numeric`→MySQL `DECIMAL(65,30)` (Bug 69), and wide PG `varchar(N)`→MySQL TEXT (Bug 72) — were never emitted on the `sync` cold-start path, so a continuous-sync operator got no up-front heads-up and could hit a mid-copy abort on the first offending value. A shared `emitCrossEngineTranslationNotices` helper now emits the identical cluster on both `migrate` and `sync` cold-start (single-db and multi-db), before any target table is created or any row moves. Each scanner self-short-circuits its non-applicable engine pair, so a same-engine sync (e.g. MySQL→MySQL, where `bigint unsigned`→`BIGINT UNSIGNED` is lossless) emits ZERO notices — no false warning on the faithful path. These remain advisory (the common case must still flow), never refusals. (Phase-A investigation confirmed the related Bug-157 MySQL-target Error 1264 was NOT a type-mapper narrowing — `bigint unsigned` maps to `BIGINT UNSIGNED` correctly on both `migrate` and `sync`, vanilla and Vitess — but a stale *signed* column on a reused target left in place by `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`; that broader target-schema-drift class is roadmap item 25.)

## [0.99.74] - 2026-06-18

### Added

- **Table-level work-stealing for the native-MySQL concurrent cold-copy — the W readers now pull from a shared atomic cursor instead of each draining a fixed group, closing the K-wide tail (roadmap 21a).** ADR-0099/0101's cross-table concurrent cold-copy partitions the in-scope tables into K disjoint groups ONCE at cold-start and drains each group serially per pipeline, with no rebalancing — so the effective width tapers below K at the tail as the lighter groups finish and idle while a heavier group grinds its last large table (observed live on Track B: K=4 collapsing to 2 active read streams at 22 of 27 tables). This release adds work-stealing for the engine where it is clean: the native-MySQL multi-snapshot reader's N connections are each a consistent-snapshot transaction from the SAME FLUSH TABLES WITH READ LOCK cut (ADR-0101), so ANY connection can read ANY table identically. A new engine-neutral `ir.WorkStealingCopyReader` capability (`ConcurrentReaderCount` + `ReadRowsOn`) is implemented by `concurrentBinlogRows`; when the reader supports it, `runConcurrentTableCopy` flattens the static partition into one ordered work list and runs W = min(N, tables) pipelines that PULL the next table from a shared atomic cursor, each reading its claimed table on its OWN pinned connection (`pinnedRowReader` adapter) — keeping the copy W-wide all the way to the last table. The D-way per-table write fan-out (ADR-0097/0102) composes per claimed table unchanged, so total concurrency stays W × D. Scoped to native MySQL on purpose: the VStream reader does NOT implement the surface (its K streams are each `Match`-scoped to their group at the source, so a stealing consumer would have no rows for an out-of-group table) and keeps the static partition — VStream work-stealing needs source-stream restructuring and is deferred (roadmap item 21). Exactly-once is preserved by the atomic claim (each table claimed by exactly one pipeline; full coverage), one in-flight read per connection (the reader index is owned by one pipeline), and the native single-FTWRL position being independent of which connection read a table, so the snapshot→CDC seam is unaffected. Pinned by `TestRunConcurrentTableCopy_WorkStealing_ExactlyOnce` (full coverage with the WS path taken and no static `ReadRows`) and `_CrossesGroupBoundary` (a deterministic proof that a pipeline pulls tables from BOTH original static groups — something the static drain can never do); as a concurrency chunk the `-race` Integration gate is the merge gate.
- **Per-table ETA for the native concurrent cold-copy, via a side metadata pool.** The native concurrent reader's progress ticker had no per-table total because its inner snapshot readers no-op `CountRows` — a metadata query on a connection actively streaming a table would be a concurrent query on a single `*sql.Conn`, which deadlocks. `concurrentBinlogRows` now implements `ir.RowCounter` by running the cheap `information_schema.TABLE_ROWS` ESTIMATE (the SAME estimate the serial RowReader uses, not an exact `COUNT(*)` scan) on a FRESH connection drawn from the retained source metadata pool (`metaDB`): catalog metadata needs no snapshot and never touches the N pinned snapshot connections, so it is collision-free, and that pool has no `MaxOpenConns` cap so the count never starves behind the pinned conns. `pinnedRowReader` (the work-stealing adapter) forwards `CountRows` so the per-table ETA works on the work-stealing path too, and a reader without `ir.RowCounter` degrades gracefully to `(0, nil)` (no-ETA). This also gives the STATIC native concurrent path (no work-stealing) an ETA it never had. Honest about what the number is: `total_rows` here is an `information_schema` ESTIMATE, not an exact count — it can drift from the true row count and the progress bar may briefly read past 100% on a fast-growing table (labeling it explicitly as an estimate is roadmap item 22, a follow-up). Pinned by `TestPinnedRowReader_ForwardsCountRows` (forwards the estimate; `(0, nil)` fallback for a non-`RowCounter` reader) plus compile-time `ir.RowCounter` assertions on both `concurrentBinlogRows` and `pinnedRowReader`.
- **Per-shard VStream progress WARN — a loud, rate-limited heads-up when one shard of a multi-shard Vitess/PlanetScale source wedges while a peer shard keeps advancing (roadmap item 23, increment B-1; WARN-only).** On a multi-shard source the VStream reader merges every shard into ONE channel, so any shard's events (plus the ~5s heartbeats) keep the whole-stream Phase-2 liveness timer and the soft idle-WARN re-armed even while another shard's emitted GTID component is frozen — i.e. the existing single-channel liveness watchdog structurally cannot see a per-shard wedge. Live testing hit exactly this: shard `-80` frozen for hours while `80-` advanced, no stream error, only heartbeats. This release adds a per-shard progress watchdog that emits ONE rate-limited WARN naming the wedged shard when its GTID component has frozen while a peer shard keeps advancing and the overall stream stays alive. OBSERVABILITY ONLY (increment B-1): the watchdog WARNs and nothing else — it never sets an error, cancels, or alters resilience; auto-reopen/escalation (B-2) is gated on a live debug capture plus a `MinimizeSkew`-hold-vs-wedge discriminator and is a future release. The WARN names BOTH candidate causes — a genuine per-shard source stall (a throttle, a large transaction, or a tablet issue; check `SHOW VITESS_THROTTLED_APPS`) OR a normal Vitess `MinimizeSkew` catch-up hold — and states explicitly that the stream stays connected. The watchdog mirrors `vstreamLiveness`'s race discipline exactly: a single goroutine owns the timer and per-shard state, fed a non-blocking buffered per-VGTID shard-advancement diff computed where `r.currentVgtid` is updated (capturing `prev` before overwrite); it reuses the `livenessTimer` seam, and a window ≤ 0 disables it (no goroutine; nil/zero-value-safe no-ops). Controlled by a new source-DSN knob `vstream_shard_stall_warn_timeout` (Go duration, default 60s; `0`/negative disables this WARN only; malformed value is a loud error), which is added to the `vstream_` DSN-param strip allowlist (Bug-126 class) so it never leaks as a `SET` on the MySQL session. As a concurrency chunk (new goroutine/timer/shared state) the `-race` Integration gate is the merge gate. Pinned by `cdc_vstream_shard_progress_test.go` (stall fires once and is rate-limited; a peer must advance for a stall to count; window ≤ 0 is a no-op; nil/zero-value safety).

## [0.99.73] - 2026-06-18

### Fixed

- **The ADR-0093 auto-resnapshot now actually RECOVERS a GTID/binlog source whose resume position was purged, instead of dead-ending on the populated-target refusal (HIGH; live Track-B/D finding).** When a Vitess/PlanetScale (`CDCVStream`) or vanilla-MySQL (`CDCBinlog`) cold-copy runs long enough that the source's binlog/GTID retention window advances past the snapshot position, the snapshot→CDC handoff (or a warm-resume) surfaces `ir.ErrPositionInvalid` (`gtid_purged advanced past the saved position`). The ADR-0093 auto-resnapshot is supposed to recover by re-snapshotting — but the **proactive** warm-resume fall-through called `coldStart` with `force=false`, so the populated-target cold-start preflight (Bug 9) REFUSED (`cold-start refused: target already contains data`). A resnapshot of an existing stream is populated *by definition*, so the recovery could never succeed; the stream dead-ended requiring a manual `--reset-target-data`. (The accompanying "no cdc-state row exists" text is a static recovery hint — misleading here, since the row did exist; the refusal fired purely on the non-empty target.) The `#52` work had given `--restart-from-scratch` the populated-target handling, but the separate auto-resnapshot fall-through never got it. The reactive (mid-stream) path was unaffected — it already sets `RestartFromScratch` and re-copies. The fix threads a `forceFresh` flag through `coldStart` / `coldStartGatePreflight` / `coldStartMultiDatabase`, set by both `--restart-from-scratch` and the automatic fall-through, so the populated target is handled the same way in both: an idempotent reader (VStream/PlanetScale) re-copies with UPSERT (absorbs the overlap, no drop); a non-idempotent reader (native MySQL binlog, plain INSERT) drops + recreates the in-scope target tables first so the re-copy can't dup-key; the `sluice_cdc_state` row is preserved. **The automatic fall-through is scoped to the source's CDC method:** `CDCBinlog` and `CDCVStream` (where a purged position is a routine retention event) auto-resnapshot, while `CDCLogicalReplication` (PostgreSQL) and `CDCTriggers` keep the deliberate loud refusal — a lost PG logical-replication slot is an abnormal failover/config event whose data-preserving recovery (`--reset-target-data` / `--force-cold-start`) must remain an explicit operator choice (the ADR-0075 Phase 2b contract). A genuine fresh cold-start onto a populated target is still refused (Bug 9 preserved). Pinned by unit tests over the gate discriminator (forceFresh true proceeds / drops vs false refuses on a populated target) and the CDC-method gate (binlog/vstream → resnapshot; logical-replication/triggers → refuse), plus the existing PG multi-schema slot-loss integration test (still refuses) and the `-race` Integration gate.

## [0.99.72] - 2026-06-18

### Fixed

- **The postgres-trigger engine no longer installs its capture trigger on its OWN internal tables, which on a re-run could recurse infinitely and block ALL writes on the source (HIGH; live Track-A finding).** `trigger setup` takes an explicit table list from the caller, and a caller that enumerates "every table with a primary key" (the common shape) sweeps in sluice's own `sluice_change_log` and `sluice_change_log_meta` once they exist — both carry a PRIMARY KEY. On the FIRST setup the change-log table doesn't exist yet, so it's never in the list; on a RE-setup (re-configure) it does, and the engine dutifully installed the `sluice_capture` row trigger on it. Because the capture function INSERTs into `sluice_change_log`, a capture trigger on that very table re-fires on every insert → unbounded recursion → PostgreSQL `stack depth limit exceeded`. That doesn't fail just the change-log write — it fails EVERY INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on EVERY triggered table, i.e. a source-wide write outage (in a real migration this takes the customer's source database offline for writes). `Setup` now filters its own internal tables (`sluice_change_log`, `sluice_change_log_meta`) out of the table list before the preflight and DDL, emits a loud WARN naming what was excluded, and errors only if nothing caller-supplied remains — so the engine is self-protecting regardless of caller hygiene. Pinned by unit tests over the filter (mixed / only-internal / none, order-preserving) and a render assertion that the post-filter DDL never attaches a trigger to an internal table while still triggering the real user tables. (The companion `sluice-heroku-migrator` also excludes `sluice_*` in its table enumeration as defense-in-depth, but this engine-side guard is the load-bearing fix.) No effect on a correctly-scoped setup: the change-log tables were never meant to be replicated, so excluding them changes nothing for any healthy install.

## [0.99.71] - 2026-06-18

### Added

- **Native (non-Vitess) MySQL concurrent cold-copy now fans EACH table across D plain-INSERT writer connections too — taking the path from W × 1 to W × D and closing most of the remaining cross-region throughput gap toward Vitess parity (ADR-0102).** ADR-0101 (v0.99.70) made the native cold-copy copy W tables CONCURRENTLY, but each table was still a single plain-INSERT stream (W × 1); the live Track-D re-test on v0.99.70 measured that at ~10.4 MB/s (~15.7× over serial) against a cross-region PlanetScale-MySQL target, versus the Vitess W × D path's ~26 MB/s. PlanetScale blocks `LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE`, so each table's copy is a batched-`INSERT` stream on one pinned connection, and a single cross-region INSERT connection is round-trip-bound — so the remaining lever is fanning each table across D connections to overlap D round-trips, exactly as ADR-0097 already does for the idempotent VStream path. This release composes that D-way per-table write fan-out onto the native concurrent path: a new engine-neutral writer capability `ir.ParallelCopyWriter.WriteRowsParallel` (the plain-INSERT mirror of ADR-0097's `WriteRowsIdempotentParallel`, calling the same `writeBatchedConn` plain-INSERT core extracted byte-identically from `writeBatched`) is implemented by the MySQL target, and a new pipeline helper `copyTablePlainMaybeParallel` REUSES ADR-0097's `partitionRowsByPK` PK-hash row routing VERBATIM — only the per-worker write call differs (plain INSERT vs upsert). Total write concurrency is **W × D** (W disjoint tables concurrent, each fanned across D PK-hash-routed writers); the ADR-0100 consumer's native branch and the serial bulk-copy loop both route through it. Exactly-once is the product of two independently unit-pinned partitions — the disjoint table partition (W, ADR-0101/0099) × the PK-hash row routing (D, ADR-0097) — and plain INSERT is correct here because the snapshot is a gap-free read onto a FRESH target (the #52 restart-from-scratch reset guarantees no leftover rows), so there is no overlap to absorb and a stray double-write surfaces as a LOUD duplicate-key error rather than silent corruption. A no-PK table routes to the single-writer serial copy (fully copied, NOT refused — distinct from the idempotent keyless refusal). Composes the existing `--copy-fanout-degree` (D) and `copy_table_parallelism` (W = N) knobs with no new knob, and is zero-value-safe: D = 1 is byte-identical to the ADR-0101 W × 1 path, and W = 1 gains a 1 × D fan-out. The W × D connection budget is the operator's responsibility on this path (the native path has no target-slot prober) with an honest WARN naming W × D — no false auto-clamp claimed. A log-clarity fix rides along: the ADR-0079 cold-start gate no longer logs "using serial cold-start" when the source actually surfaced a concurrent-copy partition (the native ADR-0101/0102 path), which had muddied the Track-D measurement interpretation. This affects the native (binlog-flavor) MySQL cold-copy only; the VStream/Vitess/PlanetScale-source cold-copy, the PostgreSQL cold-copy, the ADR-0079 fast shareable-snapshot path, and the `sluice migrate` path are untouched.

### Fixed

- **CDC apply now CONVERGES under sustained PlanetScale/Vitess transaction-killer aborts instead of exhausting its retry budget and killing the stream (Error 1105 "code = Aborted … for tx killer rollback"; live Track-B finding).** On a loaded cross-region PlanetScale-MySQL target, a CDC apply batch could repeatedly trip Vitess's transaction killer — the commit takes longer than the target's tx-timeout window, Vitess aborts and rolls back the transaction, and sluice (correctly) classifies the 1105 abort as transient and retries. But the apply batch size never SHRANK in response, so each of the ADR-0038 retries re-submitted the same ~1000-row batch that was again too large to commit inside the window, exhausting the retry budget and killing the stream. Three causes compounded (confirmed by code-read, three-phase Phase A): a tx-killer abort fires at commit and propagates out of `runOnce` to the streamer-level retry loop (the batch loop never retries internally); the AIMD apply-batch controller (ADR-0052) only multiplicative-decreases after more than its retriable-error threshold accumulate inside a one-minute window, but a single tx-killer abort is fatal to the `runOnce` attempt so they never accumulate; and the controller is constructed PER-`runOnce`, so any shrink it did make was discarded when the streamer retry re-attached a fresh controller at the `ApplyBatchSize` ceiling. The fix introduces a narrow `ir.TransactionKilledError` surface (an optional extension of `ir.RetriableError`) that the MySQL classifier sets ONLY for the "tx killer" 1105 shape — never the other retriable 1105 codes that a same-size retry legitimately rides out; the controller treats a tx-killer abort as a STRONG, IMMEDIATE multiplicative decrease (bypassing the threshold-of-three), and a new `Config.OnShrink` hook persists the shrunk size into a `Streamer`-level atomic so the next `runOnce` attaches at the shrunk size instead of the ceiling — successive restarts converge (1000 → 500 → 250 → …) rather than dying at a fixed large size. Exactly-once is preserved with NO explicit batch-split path: the AIMD shrink plus the existing ADR-0010 warm-resume re-apply IS the split — a tx-killed batch was rolled back server-side (Vitess `Aborted` = rolled back) so the persisted position never advanced (the position write rides the batch transaction, ADR-0007), and the retry warm-resumes from the prior commit and re-applies the same source changes in source order regrouped into smaller batches, with per-row upsert idempotency unchanged. This was always a loud failure (stream death after budget exhaustion), never silent data loss; it affects CDC apply onto tx-killer-enforcing targets (PlanetScale/Vitess MySQL) under load.
- **`--restart-from-scratch` and the ADR-0093 auto-resnapshot recovery no longer dup-key-error (MySQL Error 1062) when re-copying onto a populated NATIVE-MySQL target — they now clean the target first instead of trusting an idempotency the native path never had (LOUD failure, broken recovery path; #244 value-fidelity review).** Both paths force a fresh cold-start onto a non-dropped target while skipping the Bug-9 populated-target preflight, on the premise that "the idempotent copy absorbs the overlap". That premise only ever held for IDEMPOTENT snapshot readers — the VStream/PlanetScale cold-copy (UPSERT, Bug 125) and the Postgres cold-copy. The native MySQL binlog snapshot does NOT implement `ir.IdempotentCopyReader`, so its cold-copy runs PLAIN INSERT, which collided on the prior copy's leftover rows and errored loudly (`Error 1062`) — a broken, misleadingly-hinted recovery path for the common native-MySQL → target case (and ADR-0101's concurrent native cold-copy inherited it). The fix makes "from scratch" actually start from a clean target on the non-idempotent path: the cold-start gate detects a non-idempotent reader and DROPS + recreates the in-scope target tables (reusing the FK-safe `--reset-target-data` drop machinery — PG CASCADE / MySQL InnoDB referential drops) before the re-copy, so the plain-INSERT copy lands cleanly; the `sluice_cdc_state` row is preserved (only the position is discarded, distinguishing this from the fully-destructive `--reset-target-data`). The idempotent VStream/Postgres path is unchanged — it keeps its tables and genuinely absorbs the overlap via UPSERT. The misleading `--restart-from-scratch` / `--no-auto-resnapshot` help text and the auto-resnapshot WARN/terminal-error wording were corrected to describe the actual per-source behavior. Affects native (binlog-flavor) MySQL sources on the multi-table single-database path and the multi-database fan-out (ADR-0074); no behavior change for VStream/PlanetScale or Postgres sources. Pinned by unit tests over the gate dispatch (non-idempotent restart drops; idempotent restart skips; a surface-present-but-false reader is treated as non-idempotent) and the helper's drop/refuse/propagate shapes, plus an integration pin that restarts-from-scratch onto a populated native-MySQL → MySQL target and asserts a clean re-copy with no dup-key error.

## [0.99.70] - 2026-06-17

### Added

- **Native (non-Vitess) MySQL cold-copy can now copy multiple tables to the target CONCURRENTLY — N consistent-snapshot readers under one FLUSH TABLES WITH READ LOCK, closing the serial-copy bottleneck for vanilla-MySQL → PlanetScale/MySQL migrations (ADR-0101).** Live Track-D measurement showed a vanilla MySQL → PS-MySQL cold-copy held at the serial single-table rate because the native binlog cold-start copied tables ONE AT A TIME — the same serial-consumer bottleneck ADR-0100 diagnosed for the VStream path, but with no native lever to lift it (`vstream_copy_table_parallelism` is VStream-source-only, and native MySQL has no exported/shareable snapshot like Postgres and no VStream like Vitess). This release opens **N reader connections, each running `REPEATABLE READ` + `START TRANSACTION WITH CONSISTENT SNAPSHOT`, all under ONE briefly-held FLUSH TABLES WITH READ LOCK** (the mydumper / `mysqldump --single-transaction --master-data` pattern, generalized from sluice's existing one-reader binlog snapshot to N), records ONE binlog position, then `UNLOCK TABLES` — so all N readers share a single consistent cut and the snapshot→CDC seam is that single recorded position (no per-reader stitch). The in-scope tables partition into N disjoint groups via the same deterministic `partitionTablesForStreams` ADR-0099 unit-pins, surfaced through the existing `ir.ConcurrentCopyPartitioner` capability, so the existing ADR-0100 pipeline consumer (`runConcurrentTableCopy`) drives W = N concurrent read→write pipelines verbatim — each copying its group's tables from its own pinned-snapshot connection. The only new core is the FTWRL-coordinated N-snapshot opener plus a multi-snapshot RowReader that routes each table's `ReadRows` to its group's connection. **Honest scope: the v1 native concurrent copy is W × 1** — W tables concurrent, one plain-INSERT writer per table — which lifts the serial-consumer ceiling (throughput rises ~N× the serial single-table rate) but does NOT by itself reach the Vitess W × D ceiling (~26 MB/s); each table is still a single cross-region INSERT stream, and folding the per-table write fan-out in (true W × D) on the native path is a documented follow-up. No headline native MB/s figure is quoted — the live re-measurement runs after this ships. Opt-in via a new source-DSN knob `copy_table_parallelism=N`, distinct from the VStream knob and added to the DSN-param strip allowlist (Bug-126 class) so it never reaches a MySQL session; zero-value-safe (absent / `0` / `1` / single-table → N=1, the existing serial single-snapshot path byte-identical — the v0.99.51 default-inversion trap avoided by defaulting to serial). Four silent-loss-class invariants, each pinned and value-fidelity reviewed: (a) all N readers pin the SAME cut as the recorded position (the FTWRL is held across all N `START TRANSACTION` calls AND the position read, then released — the open/record/unlock ordering is pinned); (b) every in-scope table is read by exactly one reader (the disjoint partition IS the table→connection map; an unowned table is refused loudly); (c) the CDC position equals the one recorded position and is read only after the W-way errgroup joins (structural — no per-pipeline position write); (d) FTWRL-unavailable (no RELOAD — RDS/Aurora) FALLS BACK to the serial single-snapshot path with a loud WARN, NEVER opening N independent (potentially inconsistent) snapshots. N is clamped to `[1, min(len(tables), 32)]`; N is NOT folded into the connection-budget preflight in v1 (a source-DSN knob invisible to the pipeline preflight) — sluice emits a WARN naming N and stating the operator contract. ctx-cancel releases the lock and closes all N connections with no leaked goroutines; an open-failure partway unwinds every opened connection and releases the lock. This affects the native (binlog-flavor) MySQL cold-copy only; the VStream / Vitess / PlanetScale-source cold-copy, the PostgreSQL cold-copy, the ADR-0079 fast shareable-snapshot path, and the `sluice migrate` path are untouched.

## [0.99.69] - 2026-06-17

### Added

- **The `sluice sync` / VStream cold-copy now writes multiple tables to the target CONCURRENTLY — ADR-0099's K read streams become K end-to-end read→write pipelines, removing the serial-write consumer that was the missing throughput lever (ADR-0100).** ADR-0099 (v0.99.67) made the cold-copy READ side concurrent (K independent vtgate VStreams over disjoint table groups), but the orchestrator's bulk-copy loop (`runBulkCopyWithOpts`) still drained those tables ONE AT A TIME through a single serial consumer — so only one table was ever written at a time. Live Track-B measurement confirmed it: the target PROCESSLIST received rows for exactly one table at a time (28 of 28 polls over 90 s) and the path held at the same ~1.4× ceiling ADR-0097's (v0.99.65) write fan-out alone reached. K (reads) and D (per-table write fan-out) are orthogonal levers on a serial-write workload, so their product was capped by the serial consumer; the missing lever is making the WRITE side concurrent across tables. This release turns the K read streams into **K end-to-end read→write pipelines**: the mysql engine surfaces the EXACT disjoint partition it gave the producers via a new optional reader capability (`ir.ConcurrentCopyPartitioner`), and the pipeline runs W = K consumer goroutines (one per group, joined by an errgroup), each looping its group's tables serially through the SAME per-table copy helper (`copyTableColdStartIdempotentMaybeParallel`) — so the ADR-0097 D-way fan-out composes per table and total write concurrency is **W × D** (W tables concurrent, each through a D-way fan-out). Multiple tables now receive rows on the target at once, removing the serial-consumer ceiling and making K (reads) and D (per-table writers) multiply on the cross-region PlanetScale-MySQL cold-copy; bounded by source/target/network capacity and the `W × D` connection budget, not by sluice's consumer. (No headline speedup figure is quoted — the live Track-B re-measurement runs after this ships; the mechanism is the multiplicative write concurrency, not a measured number.) W = K reuses the existing source-DSN knob `vstream_copy_table_parallelism` — **no new knob** — and is zero-value-safe: absent / `0` / `1` resolves to K=1, the engine surfaces no concurrent groups, and the pipeline takes the serial table loop BYTE-IDENTICALLY (the v0.99.51 default-inversion trap avoided by tying to the existing zero-value-safe knob). Four silent-loss-class invariants, each pinned and value-fidelity reviewed: (a) every in-scope table is written by exactly one pipeline (disjoint partition — coverage + disjointness inherited from ADR-0099's unit pin since the consumer reads the SAME groups, plus a per-table write-count == 1 pin); (b) the global CDC position advances only after BOTH the W-way write join AND ADR-0099's K-way producer join precede the streamer's `Position` read (ADR-0007 — no per-pipeline position write, so CDC never starts past an un-copied table); (c) the mid-COPY durable checkpoint stays DISABLED on the concurrent path by two independent code facts (the concurrent engine pump records no breadcrumb; the pipeline does not wire the durable-progress reporter on this branch — the ADR-0097 §3 silent-loss class), unit-pinned that no checkpoint sink fires during a concurrent copy; (d) the partition is a deterministic, shuffle-invariant pure function of `(sorted tables, K)`, so cold-start and resume re-derive the IDENTICAL groups, composing with the ADR-0098 auto-shard-aware resume per pipeline. Any pipeline error aborts the whole copy loudly (errgroup cancels peers, no position advance); ctx-cancel joins all W consumers with no leaked goroutines or connections. Connection budget: W = K is **not** auto-clamped into the target-connection preflight in v1 (W = K is a source-DSN knob parsed inside the mysql engine, invisible to the pipeline-layer preflight, which resolves only D) — sluice emits a WARN at copy start naming K and stating the `K × D ≤ --max-target-connections` operator contract; the D-axis preflight still fires its loud refusal exactly as before. This affects the `sluice sync` / VStream cold-copy only; the vanilla MySQL binlog, PostgreSQL cold-copy, the ADR-0079 fast shareable-snapshot path, and the `sluice migrate` path are untouched.

## [0.99.68] - 2026-06-17

### Fixed

- **The cold-start→CDC handoff now joins a COPY-completion barrier before reading the CDC-resume `Position`, closing a data race and a potential silent-gap window on the VStream auto-shard / concurrent-COPY paths (HIGH, latent race).** The VStream auto-shard (ADR-0095, v0.99.63) and cross-table concurrent COPY (ADR-0099, v0.99.67) paths close each table's row channel on a *per-table* completion signal, so the orchestrator's bulk-copy drain could return BEFORE the producer goroutine finished stitching and writing the stitched-minimum CDC-resume `Position`. The handoff (`coldStartBeginCDC`) then read `stream.Position` racing that write — two consequences: a data race on the `Position` field (caught by a CI `-race` run), and a window where the handoff could observe an empty/stale `Position`, pick the wrong CDC start position, and leave a potential gap at the snapshot→CDC seam. The single-stream (non-auto-shard) VStream path was unaffected — its row-channel close already orders the `Position` write. The fix adds an optional engine-supplied COPY-completion barrier (`ir.SnapshotStream.WaitCopyCompleteFn`), wired on the MySQL VStream snapshot to a `copyDone` channel the COPY pump closes only AFTER `finishCopy`/`finishCopyAutoShard` writes `Position` under its mutex; the handoff joins that barrier after bulk-copy drains and BEFORE it reads `Position`, establishing the happens-before edge (write `Position` under `mu` → close `copyDone` → handoff waits → handoff reads `Position`). It is nil-safe (non-VStream paths leave the hook unset → no-op, since their row drain already orders the read), idempotent (an already-closed channel returns immediately), and ctx-cancellable (a shutdown mid-wait unwedges); `startPump` de-dups onto the same helper. Affected releases: the auto-shard variant since **v0.99.63** (ADR-0095) and the concurrent-COPY variant since **v0.99.67** (ADR-0099); the single-stream path in those and all earlier releases is not affected. Honesty note: this was always loud-or-correct in practice — a wrong `Position` surfaces as a re-stream/overlap or a gap, not exit-0 with silently-wrong data in the normal case — and in practice the handoff almost always lost the race so `Position` was populated; every regression cycle and live large-scale run observed clean handoffs, and the reliably-triggering case is a concurrent-resume path that shipped in no release. Cold-start copies themselves were always correct; this hardens only the handoff seam. Pinned by `TestVStreamSnapshot_AutoShard_WaitCopyCompleteOrdersPosition` (asserts the barrier hook is wired — guarding the zero-value-nil-hook trap — and that `Position` is non-empty and equals the stitched per-shard minimum after the barrier returns) and by the CI `-race` Integration (vstream) job, which proves the race is gone and is the merge gate (CGO is off locally).

## [0.99.67] - 2026-06-17

### Added

- **The `sluice sync` / VStream cold-copy can now open K independent concurrent COPY streams over a disjoint table partition, lifting the read-side throughput ceiling (ADR-0099).** ADR-0095 (v0.99.62) auto-shards the VStream/PlanetScale cold-copy by table — one single-table COPY at a time → bounded memory — but drains them *sequentially* over a single read stream, and ADR-0097's (v0.99.65) write-side fan-out only reached ~1.4× on this path because the single vtgate read stream plus one dispatcher is the bottleneck (the write workers starve). The lever that scales — confirmed ~4× near-linear in live Track-B measurement — is N *independent* read streams, each on a different table, each its own vtgate gRPC session. This release runs **K concurrent single-table COPY streams**, each copying a disjoint subset of the in-scope tables (each running the ADR-0095 per-table auto-shard sub-sequence restricted to its group), multiplexed over a single gRPC connection (K logical streams, one TCP/TLS connection). The table→stream partition is a deterministic, shuffle-invariant pure function of the (sorted) table set and K (size-balanced when row-count estimates exist, round-robin as the floor), so cold-start and resume re-derive the identical assignment and every in-scope table is copied by exactly one stream. Memory stays bounded at the same total: each stream gets a `--max-buffer-bytes / K` sub-budget (`K × cap/K = cap`), which is also what prevents a look-ahead stream from monopolizing the buffer and starving the table the serial consumer is actively draining. The snapshot→CDC handoff stitches the per-shard GTID-set **minimum** across the union of all K streams' every per-table snapshot (ADR-0095's set-min, parallelism-agnostic): gapless because the minimum is `⊆` every captured set so no committed change after any table's snapshot is skipped, overlap-safe via the ADR-0010 idempotent upsert, and the global CDC position advances only after **all** K streams complete all their tables (ADR-0007) so CDC never starts past an incompletely-copied table. Because the K streams are independent vtgate sessions rather than one causally-ordered session, a genuinely disjoint per-shard set is refused loudly (shard + divergent sets named) rather than guessed; any stream's error aborts the whole copy loudly with no position advance, and ctx-cancel joins all K with no leaks. Opt-in via the source-DSN knob `vstream_copy_table_parallelism=K`; zero-value-safe (absent / 0 / 1 → K=1 sequential, byte-identical to ADR-0095/0098 — the v0.99.51 trap avoided). Composes multiplicatively with ADR-0097 (K read streams × D write workers) and with the ADR-0098 auto-shard-aware resume (per stream; resume must use the same K). **Caveat stated honestly:** K is a source-DSN knob and is **not** folded into the target connection-budget preflight in v1 — the operator is responsible for keeping `K × D ≤ --max-target-connections`; sluice emits a WARN at concurrent-copy start naming K and that contract. This affects the `sluice sync` / VStream cold-copy only; the vanilla MySQL binlog, PostgreSQL cold-copy, and `sluice migrate` paths are untouched.

## [0.99.66] - 2026-06-17

### Fixed

- **Auto-shard-aware VStream cold-copy resume (Bug 156, HIGH; ADR-0098).** ADR-0095 (v0.99.62) made the VStream/PlanetScale cold-copy auto-shard by table — one single-table COPY at a time, bounded memory, no interleave — but gated that path on a *fresh* cold-start (`start == nil`). A `--resume` (a position carrying an ADR-0072 per-shard `TablePKs` cursor) fell back to the legacy single keyspace-wide interleaved stream, which buffers every not-yet-active table and hits the ADR-0071 bounded-memory cap; on a large multi-table keyspace the second table inevitably exceeds any host-fittable `--max-buffer-bytes` while the first drains, so the resume failed with `table "X" would buffer … exceeding the --max-buffer-bytes cap …` and, under a restart-on-failure supervisor, crash-looped without ever making progress — the resume of exactly the large-keyspace case auto-shard exists for was broken. Affected releases: **v0.99.62 through v0.99.65** (every version that shipped ADR-0095 auto-shard; the resume gate was introduced by ADR-0095 in v0.99.62 and persisted unchanged through v0.99.65). The gate now drops the `start == nil` clause (`autoShard := len(tables) > 1 && !singleStream`), so a resume drives the same per-table pump as cold-start: it resolves the single in-progress table from the persisted cursor, re-copies the already-finished tables idempotently (Bug-125 upsert — no double-apply), resumes the in-progress table from its cursor (no row-0 restart), copies the remaining tables fresh, and stitches the per-table snapshot positions to the per-shard set-minimum for the CDC handoff exactly as cold-start does — so the resume is gapless (CDC never starts past an incompletely-copied table) and bounded-memory (exactly one single-table COPY in flight; the ADR-0071 interleave refusal is structurally unreachable on resume). No new position-token field — completion is re-derived from the cursor plus the known table order; a legacy single-stream / multi-table cursor or a cursor naming an out-of-scope table is refused loudly rather than mis-resumed. Cold-start itself was always correct; only the resume path was affected, and the bug was always a loud failure (buffer-cap error / crash-loop), never silent data loss. Pinned by `resolveResumeAutoShard` unit tests (single in-scope → table; multi-table / out-of-scope / empty → loud refuse), resume-routing unit tests (seeds the in-progress table from the cursor, re-copies the prefix, copies the tail fresh, including the in-progress-at-index-0 edge), the set-min stitch pin, and a `vstream`-tagged integration regression that interrupts an auto-shard copy mid-flight and asserts a resume under a tiny `--max-buffer-bytes` completes with no buffer-cap error, full per-table delivery, and a clean CDC handoff.

## [0.99.65] - 2026-06-17

### Added
- **The `sluice sync` / VStream cold-start copy can now fan the snapshot out to N concurrent batched-INSERT writer workers, closing the cross-region PlanetScale-MySQL throughput gap (ADR-0097).** A `sync` cold-copy from a Vitess/PlanetScale source previously drained the snapshot through a single reader into a single writer; against a cross-region PlanetScale-MySQL target that one connection is round-trip-bound — PlanetScale blocks `LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE`, so the copy falls back to one pinned-connection batched-`INSERT` stream measured at ~13 GB/h. The read side cannot be PK-range-chunked here (vtgate streams the snapshot down one logical channel — there is no arbitrary range-`SELECT` to split, which is exactly what makes this path differ from `sluice migrate`), so the only lever is write-side fan-out. This release fans the single incoming snapshot row-stream out to N concurrent batched-INSERT writer workers, each pinning its own target connection with its own ~1 MB batch buffer, via a new engine-neutral writer capability (`ParallelIdempotentCopyWriter`) implemented by the MySQL target. Rows are routed by a deterministic PK-hash (`worker = fnv1a(pk_bytes) % degree`), not round-robin, so every emission of a given primary key always goes to the **same** worker — re-emitted rows during a VStream COPY (the Bug-125 idempotent path) serialize cleanly within one worker's batch stream and never race two upserts on the same row across workers; order-independence across workers is already guaranteed by the idempotent upsert (ADR-0010). Correctness is load-bearing: each row is sent to exactly one worker channel, and **no snapshot position advances until every worker has drained, flushed its final batch, and committed** — the whole-table join at `COPY_COMPLETED` is the sole durability guarantee on a fanned-out table. To keep resume gapless the ADR-0072 mid-COPY durable-progress watermark is disabled on fan-out tables (a flat flushed-row count summed across workers is not order-equivalent to the reader's enqueue-order frontier, so a mid-COPY breadcrumb could otherwise checkpoint past a row a lagging worker has not yet flushed); serial (non-fan-out) tables in the same run keep their mid-COPY checkpoint unchanged. Any worker error aborts the table copy loudly (no position advance, no partial silent success); ctx-cancel leaks no goroutines or pinned connections; the reader stream-error gate (Bug 68) still surfaces a mid-stream abort loudly. Opt-in via the new `--copy-fanout-degree N` flag on `sync`; the degree is a zero-value-safe `int` (0 → the default of 4, 1 → the existing serial path, avoiding the v0.99.51 `Enable…`-bool inversion trap), and the connection-budget preflight accounts for the fan-out degree. A table with no usable primary key, a non-parallel-capable writer, or a degree of 1 falls through to the serial path unchanged — the fan-out never silently does nothing, and the no-PK loud-refusal contract is never weakened. Resume of a fanned-out table stays single-stream (a documented v1 limitation, consistent with ADR-0095, not a correctness gap). This affects the `sluice sync` / VStream snapshot path only; the `sluice migrate` path is untouched (it has its own within-table chunking, ADR-0096).

## [0.99.64] - 2026-06-17

### Added
- **`sluice migrate` now parallelizes a large single table across N connections even when its primary key is non-integer (UUID/string/binary/decimal/temporal) or composite — not just a single integer PK (ADR-0096).** Since v0.5.0 (ADR-0019) the migrate path has split a large single table into N PK-range chunks copied concurrently, but a table was eligible only when it had a single integer primary key; anything else (a UUID/string surrogate key, a `(tenant_id, id)` composite key) fell back to a single-connection copy. On a cross-region PlanetScale target that one connection is round-trip-bound — PlanetScale blocks `LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE`, so the copy uses one pinned-connection batched-`INSERT` stream — so a UUID-keyed table single-streamed the tail while the integer-keyed tables next to it ran N-way. This release lifts the limit: a single non-integer *orderable* PK (string/varchar/char, uuid, inet/cidr/macaddr, decimal/numeric, binary/bytea, temporal) or a composite PK whose columns are all orderable is now chunk-eligible via a new **sampled-keyset** boundary strategy — a single windowed `ROW_NUMBER()` scan over the PK index that splits the table by *actual row count* (each chunk gets ~total/N rows, so it is skew-free regardless of how clustered or sparse the keyspace is, unlike the integer path's MIN/MAX-divide on a non-uniform key). Exactly-once chunk coverage is guaranteed by construction: both chunk bounds are pushed into SQL in the PK column's own collation (`WHERE (pk) > ($lower) AND (pk) <= ($upper) ORDER BY pk`), so the read order and the boundary partition always agree, and the half-open `(lower, upper]` convention over the engine's own `ORDER BY` total order partitions the keyspace with no gap and no overlap (open ends capture everything below the minimum and above the sampled maximum, including rows inserted past the sampled max during the copy). It composes with the idempotent cold-copy writer (Bug 125 — disjoint ranges never key-collide) and with resume (ADR-0072 — boundaries are computed once, persisted as the already-`[]any` chunk-progress tuples, and read verbatim on resume, never recomputed). There is no new flag or config key — strategy selection is derived from the table's PK type, so the existing `--bulk-parallelism` / `--bulk-parallel-min-rows` knobs simply engage on a strictly wider set of tables, drawing from the same connection budget (ADR-0076) the integer path already respects. Tables with no usable PK (no PK, or a non-orderable PK column such as `JSON`/array/geometry) still take the safe single-reader path, as do shard-injected (`--inject-shard-column`) PK tables. This affects the `sluice migrate` within-table copy only; the `sluice sync` / VStream snapshot path is untouched.

## [0.99.63] - 2026-06-17

### Added
- **A full multi-table Vitess/PlanetScale keyspace now cold-copies in one `migrate`/`sync` command at bounded memory, with no per-table `--include-table` workaround (ADR-0095).** A keyspace-wide VStream COPY (`Match:"/.*/"`) makes vtgate interleave every table down one stream, and sluice's orchestrator drains one table at a time — so every not-yet-active table buffered in memory (RAM ≈ Σ all-but-active table sizes), which on a large keyspace hit the ADR-0071 loud-refusal floor (or OOM). The engine now auto-shards the COPY by table internally: it opens, drains, and completes one single-table VStream COPY (`Match:<table>`, constant memory, no interleave) at a time on the same gRPC connection, then opens the keyspace-wide CDC tail. You still run one command; the per-table loop is invisible above the `SnapshotStream` boundary. On by default for a fresh cold-start with more than one table; opt out via `vstream_copy_single_stream=true` in the source DSN (restores the legacy single interleaved stream and its ADR-0071 refusal floor). The snapshot→CDC handoff resumes CDC from the per-shard GTID-set intersection `P_start = ⋂ P_i` (the set-minimum, never the maximum) of the per-table snapshot positions: gapless because `P_start` is a subset of every `P_i` (no committed change after any table's snapshot can be skipped — the silent-loss class), and overlap-safe because the re-delivered window `(P_start, P_i]` is the same at-least-once seam the ADR-0010 idempotent upsert and the Bug-125 idempotent-copy writer already absorb. A genuinely-disjoint per-shard set (which one causally-ordered VStream session should never produce) refuses loudly rather than guess a position that might gap. `--include-table` is preserved (it scopes the per-table loop), and the persisted handoff token shape is unchanged. Resume (an ADR-0072 `TablePKs` cursor) stays on the single-stream path — a documented v1 limitation, not a correctness gap.

### Fixed
- **PostgreSQL cold-start `ENUM` `CREATE TYPE` is now idempotent, so a resumed/restarted cold-start with enum columns no longer crash-loops on `SQLSTATE 42710` (Bug 154, HIGH).** The PG cold-start schema phase (`CreateTablesWithoutConstraints`) emitted a bare `CREATE TYPE ... AS ENUM` with no existence guard — `CREATE TABLE` on the same path is already `IF NOT EXISTS`, so only the enum type was non-idempotent. Once the first cold-start created the type, any mid-COPY interruption followed by a restart re-entered the schema phase and hard-failed with `type already exists`, turning every restart into a crash-loop with zero progress; the ADR-0072 resume path deliberately skips the populated-target preflight on the (until now false) assumption that this phase is fully idempotent. PostgreSQL has no `CREATE TYPE IF NOT EXISTS`, so the cold-start create now routes through the same DO-block `duplicate_object` guard the CDC enum-forward path already used (`ensureEnumType`), extracted into a shared `guardedCreateEnumType` helper. This was always a loud failure (crash-loop, no progress), never silent data loss.
- **`--reset-target-data` now drops synthesized enum TYPES too, including a PG-source enum carried by its original name (Bug 154).** `DropSchemaTypes` dropped only the synthesized `<table>_<col>_enum` name and missed a PostgreSQL-source enum type carried by its original `TypeName` (Bug 19c), leaving that type orphaned to re-trigger 42710 on the next fresh cold-start. The drop now mirrors exactly what cold-start creates (`resolveEnumTypeName`, skip generated columns, dedup shared types), so a reset leaves no orphan and a fresh cold-start after a reset succeeds.

## [0.99.62] - 2026-06-16

### Added
- **A PlanetScale/Vitess source reshard mid-stream is now followed automatically, instead of halting the sync (ADR-0094).** When the source keyspace reshards (a shard split, merge, or `MoveTables`), vtgate emits a JOURNAL marker and ends the stream at the cut. Previously sluice surfaced that as a loud terminal error (`shard layout changed … reopen required`) and the sync stopped until an operator restarted it. The CDC reader already detected the reshard and could rebuild the stream against the new shard layout from the journal-stamped GTIDs (proven exactly-once by the reshard chaos test), but the orchestrator never invoked that path. It now does: on the reshard signal the Streamer reopens the stream onto the new layout and continues applying, with no gap and no overlap at the seam (the journal GTIDs anchor the cut), no re-snapshot, and no operator intervention. The follow is bounded (a pathological reshard storm or a misbehaving reader still fails loud rather than spinning), and a pending schema-forwarding error is never masked by a reopen. Validated end-to-end on a real multi-process Vitess cluster: a cold-started sync through a live 1→2 reshard lands every pre- and post-reshard row exactly once on the target.

### Notes
- Scope: single-stream Vitess/PlanetScale (VStream) sync. Reshard while `--inject-shard-column` (Shape-A multi-shard consolidation) is engaged is intentionally **not** auto-followed yet — that interplay is deferred to a follow-up; it keeps the prior loud-terminal behavior. No effect on non-Vitess sources.

## [0.99.61] - 2026-06-16

### Added
- **Cross-shard-collision preflight: a multi-shard Vitess/PlanetScale source with no shard discriminator is now refused before any data moves (Bug 152).** A sharded keyspace fronted by vtgate transparently merges every shard into one logical stream, so a `migrate`/`sync` pointed at a multi-shard source *without* `--inject-shard-column` writes every shard's rows into one target table. If that table has a primary key or a UNIQUE constraint, rows from different shards that share a key value (per-shard auto-increment ranges, tenant-local ids) would silently overwrite each other — an exit-0, fewer-rows-than-source data-loss class. sluice now detects a multi-shard source (via `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` on the PlanetScale/Vitess flavors) and **loudly refuses** a collision-capable (PK/UNIQUE) target table before any data moves, naming the two ways forward: add a per-shard discriminator with `--inject-shard-column NAME=VALUE` (ADR-0048), or pass the new `--allow-cross-shard-merge` opt-out when the key is globally unique across shards (e.g. Vitess sequences or UUID keys). Keyless tables don't trip it (already at-least-once, no overwrite), and single-shard / non-sharded sources (vanilla MySQL, Postgres) are unaffected. If the shard layout can't be determined the preflight fails closed (refuses) rather than risk a silent overwrite.

### Changed
- **New flag `--allow-cross-shard-merge`** on `migrate` and `sync start` — the explicit opt-out for the Bug 152 preflight above. Off by default (guard active).

## [0.99.60] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **PostgreSQL-source `ENUM` columns now replicate over PG→PG CDC (catalog Bug 151).** The `pgoutput` CDC reader's OID-to-type map had no case for a user-defined `ENUM` — its OID is dynamic (assigned at `CREATE TYPE` time), so the static lookup declined it and the first enum `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` wedged the stream with `unsupported column type OID <dyn> (typmod -1)`. Enums cold-started (bulk copy) fine but could not be continuously synced PG→PG. This is the same class as the closed Bug 144 (arrays) and Bug 147 (geometry) reader gaps, and it's fixed the same way: the reader resolves the set of user-defined enum type OIDs (`pg_type.typtype='e'`) at the relation boundary — cumulatively, so a mid-stream `CREATE TYPE` + `ADD COLUMN` is picked up too — and maps a matching column to an enum whose value rides the wire as its text label. A non-enum user-defined type (composite, domain) and enum **arrays** (`enum[]`) stay loudly refused (no silent loss). MySQL→PostgreSQL enum sync and the cold-start path were already correct; this closes the PG-source CDC path.

## [0.99.59] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **A forwarded `DROP COLUMN` of a synthesized ENUM column now drops the per-column PostgreSQL enum type too (Bug 150).** When a MySQL-source `ENUM` column is migrated to PostgreSQL, sluice synthesizes a dedicated `"<table>_<col>_enum"` type for it (MySQL enums carry no type identity). A schema-change-forwarded `DROP COLUMN` dropped the column but left that type behind as an orphan — harmless to existing data, but a later re-add of a same-named column with a *different* value set would have collided with (or silently reused) the stale type. `AlterDropColumn` now also issues `DROP TYPE IF EXISTS "<schema>"."<table>_<col>_enum"`, but **only** for these synthesized per-column types. A preserved/named PostgreSQL enum type (`TypeName` set — which may be shared across columns or tables) is never auto-dropped, so same-engine PG→PG enum sharing is unaffected.

## [0.99.58] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **MySQL `SET` columns now replicate to a PostgreSQL `text[]` over CDC (Bug 149).** With v0.99.56's SET fix, a MySQL `SET` decodes to a Go string slice and its PG target is `TEXT[]` — but the CDC applier's array binding required a different internal slice shape and rejected it with a loud `expected []any for Array column, got []string`, halting the stream at apply (no silent loss). The applier's array binding now accepts the string-slice shape and routes it through the same path as a native `text[]`, so a MySQL `SET` lands as its member labels in a PG `text[]` column. (Cold-start migration of `SET` → `text[]` already worked; this closes the continuous- sync apply path.)

## [0.99.57] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **PlanetScale/Vitess (VStream) resume from a purged GTID position now reliably auto-recovers (Bug 146, ADR-0093 amendment).** v0.99.51 added a *reactive* recovery (classify vtgate's "purged required binary logs" error → cold-start re-snapshot), but a local Vitess-24 cluster reproduction proved vtgate does **not** surface that error on a purged resume — it accepts the stale position, the tablet drops the binlog dump (errno 2013), and vtgate idles on heartbeats, so the stream looped on a retriable liveness timeout and never cold-started. sluice now does a **proactive pre-flight** before opening the stream — `GTID_SUBSET(@@global.gtid_purged, <resume>)`, mirroring the binlog reader — and returns the cold-start signal when the resume position is unreachable. The check is routed at the **same tablet type the stream binds to** (`gtid_purged` is tablet-type-routed by vtgate; a replica can purge independently of the primary), strips the Vitess GTID flavor prefix, and degrades gracefully (proceeds, never a spurious re-snapshot) if the probe can't run. The reactive classifier is retained as defence-in-depth. `--no-auto-resnapshot` still turns the recovery into a loud terminal error.

## [0.99.56] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **MySQL `SET` columns now sync correctly over binlog CDC (Bug 148).** The go-mysql binlog decoder hands a `SET` cell back as its **numeric bitmask** (not the member text), and sluice's CDC value decoder passed that through — so a replicated `SET('a','c')` carried `["5"]` (the mask, stringified) instead of `["a","c"]`. The decoder now maps the bitmask to its member labels via the column's value list (bit *i* → the *i*-th declared member, in declaration order), errors loudly on a bit with no member, and treats mask 0 as the empty set. Comma-joined label text (the snapshot/copy path and the VStream reader) still passes through unchanged. This is the `SET` sibling of the v0.99.52 ENUM fix (Bug 145); it applies to **self-hosted MySQL binlog CDC** (snapshot and PlanetScale/Vitess sync were already correct, as they deliver the label text).

## [0.99.55] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **PostGIS `geometry` now syncs over CDC from a PostgreSQL *source* too (Bug 147) — completes geometry-over-CDC.** v0.99.54 added the apply-side geometry codec (which made MySQL→PG work); this release fixes the read side. The pgoutput CDC reader's type map had no `geometry` case — its OID is dynamic (assigned at `CREATE EXTENSION postgis`), so it can't be a static entry — so the first geometry change over PG→PG CDC killed the stream with `unsupported column type OID <n> (geometry)`. (Cold-start COPY was unaffected.) The reader now resolves the runtime geometry OID and decodes the value (and correctly treats pgoutput's text-format hex-EWKB bytes as hex, not raw EWKB — a silent-corruption trap caught in review). Pinned across point / polygon / multipolygon / geometrycollection, 2D / Z / M / ZM, SRID 4326 and 0, `POINT EMPTY`, NULL, and UPDATE/DELETE images.
- `geography` over CDC remains **loudly refused** (no applier codec yet) — tracked as a follow-up; no silent loss.

## [0.99.54] - 2026-06-16

### Added
- **PostGIS `geometry` columns now apply over CDC to a PostgreSQL target (#20)** — the apply (write) side; combined with the Bug 147 read-side fix in v0.99.55 this makes geometry-over-CDC work in both PG-source and MySQL-source directions. Geometry was previously un-appliable over continuous sync: the CDC applier had no codec for PostGIS's (dynamically-assigned) `geometry` type OID, so the EWKB bytes were shipped in text format and PostGIS rejected them (`parse error - invalid geometry`) — a loud refusal on both the serial and pipelined (ADR-0092) apply paths. (Cold-start COPY was unaffected.) A binary geometry codec is now registered on the applier connections and ships EWKB to `geometry_recv`, so an INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE carrying a geometry value applies correctly — every subtype (point / line / polygon / multi* / collection), dimension (2D/Z/M/ZM), and byte order, pinned against a real PostGIS target.

### Fixed
- **Per-column SRID is now preserved on geometry CDC apply (#20).** The CDC readers strip per-row SRID to raw WKB (the IR carries SRID as a per-column property, ADR-0035), and the applier previously defaulted the column SRID to 0 — so a replicated value into a constrained `geometry(<type>,<srid>)` column lost its SRID. The applier now recovers each geometry column's real SRID (and subtype) from `geometry_columns` / `geography_columns`, so the stored geometry matches the source (verified `ST_AsEWKB` + `ST_SRID` src==dst).

### Compatibility / notes
- No flag or config change; geometry-over-CDC works by default once the target has PostGIS installed.
- `geography` columns and `geometry[]` (arrays of geometry) remain **loudly refused** over CDC apply (no silent loss) — separate follow-up work. A per-row SRID stored in an *unconstrained* `geometry` column is still dropped by design (ADR-0035).

## [0.99.53] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **Keyless-table CDC WARN now states at-least-once delivery honestly (Bug 143).** The ADR-0089 keyless guard applies each INSERT into a table with no PRIMARY KEY (and no usable unique index) as its own transaction so the adaptive batch default never makes such tables worse than `--apply-batch-size=1`. Its WARN, however, claimed this meant "crash-replay cannot duplicate rows" — which is **false**. A keyless INSERT is not idempotent, and crash-resume granularity is the **source transaction** (the GTID/LSN only advances at its commit; for VStream the position-bearing VGTID arrives *after* every row event of the transaction), so a hard kill before that commit checkpoint re-inserts **every keyless row in the interrupted source transaction** on resume — not one. Keyless CDC is at-least-once; per-row checkpointing cannot change that (you cannot resume mid-source- transaction). This release corrects the WARN (and the matching ADR-0089 / code-comment claims) to state the at-least-once semantics plainly and point at adding a PRIMARY KEY (or NOT NULL UNIQUE index) for exactly-once, batched throughput. **Behaviour is unchanged** — this is a truth-in-logging fix; the long-standing guidance (ADR-0010: tables without a key are not recommended for continuous sync) is unchanged.

## [0.99.52] - 2026-06-16

### Fixed
- **MySQL ENUM values now sync correctly over CDC (Bug 145).** The MySQL binlog hands an `ENUM` cell back as its **1-based ordinal index**, not the label, and the CDC value decoder passed that straight through — so a replicated INSERT carried `"2"` instead of `"active"`. A PostgreSQL enum target **rejected** it (`SQLSTATE 22P02 invalid input value for enum`); a MySQL target only appeared to work because it coerces the numeric string by index (fragile, and wrong the moment the value list shifts). The decoder now maps the index to its label via the column's value list; a label string (the snapshot/copy path and the VStream reader both deliver labels) passes through unchanged. Applies to **all** MySQL enum CDC, not just schema-change forwarding.
- **MySQL ENUM schema changes now forward to a PostgreSQL target (Bug 145).** Two DDL gaps in the forward path: an `ADD COLUMN <enum>` referenced the named PG enum type without creating it (`42704 type does not exist`), and a MySQL `MODIFY … ENUM(...)` that appends a value (which arrives as an alter-column-type) hit an internal "enum DDL requires column context" error. The forward path now creates the enum type first (idempotent `CREATE TYPE`) for an added enum column, and forwards an appended value as `ALTER TYPE … ADD VALUE IF NOT EXISTS`. Appending a value is exact; a value rename/removal on the source leaves the PG enum a superset (no data loss — every value the source can still produce remains valid). Combined with the value fix above, MySQL→PG ENUM now works end-to-end (add column, add value, rows landing by label).

## [0.99.51] - 2026-06-15

### Fixed
- **A PlanetScale / Vitess (VStream) resume from a purged GTID position now auto-recovers instead of restart-looping (ADR-0093).** When a persisted resume position falls behind the source's retained binlogs (`gtid_purged` advanced past it — routine on PlanetScale's binlog-retention window), the stream used to exit with an unclassified error and, on supervisor restart, hit the same purged position again — a restart loop. The self-hosted MySQL binlog source already auto-recovers this via a pre-flight check → cold-start (ADR-0022); the VStream source had no parity because vtgate is a proxy with no single `gtid_purged` to pre-flight, so the condition only surfaces reactively from the stream and was never classified as an invalid position. Now the vtgate "purged required binary logs" error is classified as an invalid position and routed to a **one-shot, non-destructive cold-start re-snapshot** (the idempotent copy absorbs the overlap; the target is not dropped). The recovery is bounded — a second consecutive invalid position after a fresh re-snapshot fails loudly (the source is purging faster than a snapshot can complete, which auto-retry cannot fix). This was always a loud failure, never silent data loss; it now self-heals. Found by cross-referencing PlanetScale's own `fivetran-source` connector.

### Added
- **`--no-auto-resnapshot`** (`sync start`): opt out of the automatic re-snapshot above. With this flag, a purged/invalid resume position surfaces as a loud, actionable terminal error naming the recovery commands (`--restart-from-scratch` / `--reset-target-data`) instead of auto re-snapshotting — for operators who would rather decide a (potentially expensive) full re-snapshot of very large tables deliberately. The flag gates both the binlog pre-flight fall-through and the new VStream reactive recovery, keeping the two paths consistent. Default (unset) = auto-recover, parity with the binlog path.

## [0.99.50] - 2026-06-15

### Fixed
- **Postgres array columns now sync over CDC (Bug 144).** A Postgres source table with any array column (`int4[]`, `text[]`, `numeric[]`, `timestamptz[]`, …) wedged the continuous-sync stream on the first INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE to that table with `unsupported column type OID 1007/1009/1231…`: the pgoutput CDC reader's type resolver (`oidToType`) had no array-OID cases, even though the initial bulk-copy (cold-start) handled arrays fine. Array columns are now resolved to the IR array type by mapping each array OID to its element OID and recursing — so an array element decodes identically to the same scalar column — and the array-value decoder now also accepts the `[]byte` text form that the pgoutput wire delivers (it previously only handled the cold-start `[]any` / `string` shapes, which silently mis-walked the text bytes). Multi-dimensional arrays are preserved (not flattened), NULL elements survive as NULLs, and `numeric[]` keeps full scale. This was a loud failure (the stream stopped with a clear error), never silent data loss. The decode↔write asymmetry is unchanged: `timetz[]`, `bytea[]`, and `json[]`/`jsonb[]` arrays still refuse loudly at apply (no silent acceptance). Same dual-registry-drift class as the earlier Bug 97/118 fix; a parity guard now pins the CDC array registry to the schema reader's so they can't drift again.

## [0.99.49] - 2026-06-15

### Changed
- **Postgres CDC apply is now pipelined (ADR-0092) — ~70× higher apply throughput on latency-bound (cross-region/cross-cloud) links.** The batch apply path used to send a batch of N changes as N serial `Exec` round trips inside one transaction, plus a position upsert and a commit (N+2 round trips). Batching (ADR-0017/ADR-0089) amortized the commit fsync but did nothing for the N data round trips — they stayed serial, so steady-state apply throughput was bounded by `1 / per_row_exec_latency`, which on any non-co-located link is dominated by network RTT. The data statements **and** the position upsert are now queued onto a single `pgx.Batch` and sent in one pipelined flush; round trips per batch drop from N+2 to O(1) (begin + flush + commit), independent of N, so throughput becomes bounded by the server's execution rate rather than `N × RTT`. Measured on a live PlanetScale soak, apply was pinned at ~90 rows/s on a ~7 ms cross-cloud link and a PS-10 → PS-80 database upsize moved it 0% (the bottleneck was the wire, not the DB); pipelining lifts that ~70×. The win scales with RTT — co-located/low-latency targets were already fast (batching amortized the commit) and see a smaller gain, never a regression. Default-on for the batch apply path (`--apply-batch-size` > 1, the `auto` default); `--apply-batch-size=1` keeps the serial per-change path verbatim.
